4. The facts show more than a mere gratuitous promise or revocable license. Anna’s statement was conditional—Claire was to marry and move onto the property—and Claire then undertook substantial expenditures and long-term occupation in reliance on receiving beneficial ownership. Marriage is a problematic form of consideration because promises made in consideration of marriage are themselves within the statute; standing alone, it looks more like an unenforceable marriage-based settlement. 5. Two Statute of Frauds categories are triggered. First, this is an oral transfer of an interest in land, or at minimum an oral declaration that Anna would hold title for Claire’s benefit. Second, because the promise was made before marriage and expressly tied to marriage, the “agreement upon consideration of marriage” category may also be implicated. Her strongest point is that those acts are not easily explained by a temporary license: paying $6,500 of personal funds for a large addition and outbuildings, plus utility and landscaping improvements, is conduct of an owner-beneficiary, not a casual occupant.

Under Neale-style doctrine, possession plus valuable improvements with the promisor’s knowledge is the classic route to specific performance notwithstanding the statute. Promissory estoppel is a useful backup because Anna reasonably should have expected Claire to rely, Claire did rely substantially, and injustice would follow if the heirs kept the improved land. The alleged “breach” based on Claire’s use of some rental proceeds for personal expenses is the weakest defense unless the oral agreement expressly made restricted use of rents a condition precedent to title. 8. The heirs’ strongest argument is not simply “no writing,” but that Claire’s acts were not unequivocally referable to an ownership contract and are equally consistent with a permissive family occupancy arrangement. That is the best doctrinal path because many courts require part performance to point clearly to the alleged land agreement. To win on that theory, the heirs would need to prove that Anna remained the real owner in practice: she funded key improvements, kept material rental income, retained control over decisions, never surrendered beneficial possession, and that Claire’s occupancy and spending were what any daughter might do while living on her mother’s land, not acts explainable only by a promise of ownership.

9. The main uncertainties are whether State X requires acts “unequivocally referable” to the contract; whether possession alone plus some improvements is enough, or whether receipt of rents and exclusive control are also required; whether the marriage clause changes the analysis; and whether State X allows promissory estoppel to overcome the land Statute of Frauds at all. Second, counsel should assemble strict proof tracing Claire’s funds and labor to permanent improvements and long possession: receipts, contractor testimony, utility records, photographs, tax and rental records. Third, counsel should secure third-party testimony and admissions showing Anna treated the property as Claire’s beneficially—statements to family, contractors, tenants, or Ben acknowledging that Anna held title for Claire.

2. On these facts, Claire should likely prevail on her equitable claim to enforce the oral promise. The facts closely track Neale: an oral family promise tied to marriage, possession, long acquiescence, and substantial improvements made from the promisee’s own funds. If the trial court finds uncertainty about specific performance because of the marriage-related aspect or indefinite trust language, the fallback should still be a constructive trust, and at minimum reliance damages under promissory estoppel.

3. The dispositive doctrine on the face of the pleadings is the Statute of Frauds requirement that transfers of land, and usually declarations of trust in land, be in writing. The doctrine most likely controlling the result, however, is equitable part performance, reinforced by estoppel. Neale’s core principle is that equity will not let the Statute of Frauds become an instrument of fraud where the promisee, in reliance on the oral land promise, has taken possession and made substantial, permanent improvements clearly referable to that promise. Claire’s long possession, construction, utility and landscaping work, and rental management fit that pattern.

4. The consideration question is mixed. Anna’s statement can be characterized either as a bargain (“if Claire marries and moves onto the property, Anna will give her the lot”) or as a conditional family gift. A court need not resolve that question in orthodox contract terms to grant equitable relief. Marriage alone can look like the condition of a gift, and “move in” likewise resembles a condition. But Claire’s substantial expenditures and assumption of beneficial use were not merely incidental; they were induced by Anna’s promise and undertaken with Anna’s knowledge. Under Neale-style analysis, those acts function at least as reliance sufficient for equity, even if not treated as classic bargained-for consideration.

5. Three writing categories are implicated. First, the land-transfer provision plainly applies. Second, the alleged arrangement that title would be “held for Claire’s benefit” looks like an express trust in land, which ordinarily also requires writing. Third, because the promise was made before marriage and conditioned on marriage, the Statute’s marriage-related provision may also be implicated. The clearest barrier is the land-transfer rule. Claire’s answer is part performance: she married, moved in, possessed for fifteen years, paid materially for additions and outbuildings, improved utilities and landscaping, and acted as beneficial owner by collecting rents. Those acts are the basis for removing the land-transfer bar in equity.

6. Claire can argue both classical part performance and modern promissory estoppel, but part performance is stronger. It directly matches Neale and supports specific relief in land cases. Her possession plus substantial improvements are exactly the facts courts traditionally require because damages are often inadequate and the acts are hard to explain except by an ownership promise. Promissory estoppel is a useful alternative if State X is reluctant to order conveyance or treats the marriage provision more strictly. Still, courts are divided on whether promissory estoppel can override the Statute of Frauds for land transfers, so it is doctrinally less secure than classical part performance here.

7. The heirs’ defenses likely do not defeat Claire’s claim, though they may affect accounting. If Anna paid for some improvements, that weakens the exclusivity of Claire’s reliance but does not erase it so long as Claire’s own expenditures were substantial and permanent. If Anna retained some rental income, that may show imperfect implementation of the arrangement, but not necessarily negate Claire’s beneficial occupancy and investment; family arrangements often involve shared income or informal accounting. The alleged “breach” from Claire’s use of some rent for personal expenses is weakest. Unless Anna clearly imposed a definite condition that all rents be reinvested in the property, Claire’s personal use is consistent with being the beneficial owner. At most it supports an offset, not forfeiture.

8. The heirs’ best argument is that Claire’s conduct was not unequivocally referable to an enforceable oral conveyance but was equally consistent with a revocable family license or promised gift conditioned on marriage. To make that argument succeed, they must prove that Anna remained the real owner throughout: that Anna materially funded or controlled the construction, retained rents as owner rather than occasionally, supervised decisions as principal rather than helper, and never treated Claire as having a present beneficial interest. They would also want to show the promise’s terms were too indefinite—especially as to any trust—and that Claire’s expenditures were ordinary occupancy costs, not ownership-type improvements.

9. The principal uncertainties are whether State X lets part performance overcome the separate marriage-related Statute of Frauds provision; how “substantial” and “unequivocally referable” Claire’s improvements must be; and whether mixed facts such as shared rents or partial funding by Anna undermine exclusivity of reliance. To maximize success, Claire’s counsel should first plead in the alternative for specific performance, constructive trust, and promissory-estoppel damages, expressly alleging possession, improvements, and unjust fraud under Neale. Counsel should also develop a detailed evidentiary record tracing Claire’s own expenditures, dates of possession, the permanent character of each improvement, and the rental history. Finally, counsel should secure testimony and documents showing Anna’s repeated acknowledgments that the property was Claire’s or held for her benefit, because those admissions make Claire’s acts far more clearly referable to the oral promise.

Bottom line: assuming State X follows Neale-style equity, Claire has the better claim and should receive conveyance of title, or at least a constructive trust, with any disputes over rents or shared expenditures handled through accounting rather than denial of relief.

1. I assume State X follows the ordinary U.S. common-law Statute of Frauds for land transfers, together with the equitable part-performance doctrine recognized in Neale v. 3. The key standard is not merely reliance in the abstract, but reliance manifested by acts that are substantial and sufficiently referable to the alleged land agreement.

4. Claire’s case is stronger if characterized as a bargained-for exchange or at least an induced reliance transaction, not a mere revocable license or conditional gift. Marriage historically counts as consideration, and here it was not the only act: Claire also moved onto the property, took possession, spent $6,500 of her own money on a large addition and outbuildings, improved utilities and landscaping, and treated the premises as beneficial owner by collecting rent. The better characterization is that marriage was part of the consideration or at minimum the central inducement, while the expenditures and possession are the clearest part-performance acts.

5. The writing requirement for an interest in land is plainly triggered. If State X includes the traditional marriage-consideration clause, that is also triggered because the promise was made “if Claire married and moved onto the property.” But the land clause is enough and is the practical barrier. Claire’s response is that part performance removes the bar because she did what owners, not mere guests, ordinarily do: she married in reliance, entered possession, paid substantial sums for permanent improvements, managed rental use, and altered her position over fifteen years with Anna’s knowledge and encouragement. Those acts are the kind equity treats as making it fraudulent to insist on the absence of a writing.

6. Claire can proceed under both classical part performance and modern promissory estoppel, but part performance is materially stronger. Part performance is stronger because it is tailored to land contracts and supports title-based relief, which is what Claire wants. Her possession and permanent improvements are classic equitable markers under Neale. Promissory estoppel is a useful backup because Anna made a clear promise, expected reliance, and Claire’s reliance was substantial and foreseeable; however, courts are divided on whether promissory estoppel can overcome the Statute of Frauds in land cases, and some limit it to reliance damages rather than conveyance. Thus, classical part performance is the main path to specific relief; promissory estoppel mainly protects against a court’s narrower view of part performance.

7. The heirs’ defenses are weak on this record. First, that Anna paid for some improvements does not defeat Claire’s claim so long as Claire’s own expenditures and labor were substantial; at most it raises a factual issue about the extent of reliance or the precise equitable accounting. Second, Anna’s retention of some rental income likewise does not negate part performance. It may show the arrangement was imperfectly carried out, but it does not erase Claire’s possession, improvements, and beneficial use. At most it bears on whether the court orders a full conveyance outright or imposes a trust with accounting. Third, the alleged “breach” by Claire in using some rental income for personal expenses should not defeat relief unless the heirs prove an actual condition of the oral agreement requiring rents to be used only for property purposes. The facts as given state no such condition. If Claire was beneficial owner, personal use of some rents is entirely consistent with ownership.

8. The heirs’ strongest nonfrivolous argument is that Claire’s conduct was not unequivocally referable to a contract to convey land, but is equally explainable as permissive family occupancy plus parental assistance and informal sharing of expenses and rents. That argument matters because many courts require acts that point specifically to the alleged agreement, not merely to a close family arrangement. To win on that theory, the heirs would need to prove at trial that Claire occupied with Anna’s permission as a daughter, that Anna materially funded the improvements, that Anna continued exercising ownership by keeping rents and supervising the property, and that there was no settled understanding that Claire held beneficial title.

9. The main uncertainties are jurisdictional and evidentiary. Some courts following Neale require possession plus substantial improvements; some demand acts “unequivocally referable” to the precise land promise; some are skeptical when possession can be explained by family permission; some may treat the marriage-consideration clause as an added barrier; and some limit promissory estoppel in land cases to damages. To maximize success, Claire’s counsel should first plead multiple equitable theories carefully: specific performance of the oral land settlement, constructive trust on the heirs’ title, and promissory estoppel damages in the alternative. Second, counsel should assemble detailed proof tying Claire’s expenditures and possession to Anna’s promise: receipts, contractor testimony, photographs, utility records, tax and rent records, and witnesses who heard Anna describe the property as Claire’s. Third, counsel should develop evidence negating the “mere family license” narrative by showing the permanence and owner-like nature of Claire’s acts over fifteen years, including exclusive management decisions, the scale of improvements, and Anna’s repeated acquiescence and praise. On the assumed Neale-style framework, those facts should justify equitable conveyance.

4. The facts show more than a revocable license, but they are not a perfect bargained exchange either. 5. The principal writing requirement triggered is the land-transfer provision. Under Neale, those acts are the critical gate-opening facts.

6. Claire could prevail under either classical part performance or modern promissory estoppel, but part performance is the stronger route. It is stronger because the remedy sought is land itself, the reliance acts are tangible and property-specific, and Neale squarely treats possession plus valuable improvements as enough to justify equitable enforcement. Promissory estoppel is a useful backup if the court is unwilling to order conveyance, but some courts are reluctant to let estoppel wholly override the Statute of Frauds and will limit relief to reliance damages. 7. The heirs’ specific defenses likely do not defeat Claire’s equity claim, though they may affect accounting. Anna’s paying for some improvements does not erase Claire’s own substantial expenditures; it may reduce the weight of reliance only if the heirs prove Claire’s contribution was minor or ordinary maintenance rather than substantial improvement. Anna’s retention of some rental income likewise cuts against exclusivity, but it is not fatal where Claire lived there for fifteen years, made major improvements, and also collected rents herself; mixed rent handling is more consistent with a later accounting than with denial of all relief. The claim that Claire breached a condition by using some rental proceeds for personal expenses is weakest unless the heirs can prove an actual express condition limiting rent use. On these facts, using rents personally is at least as consistent with beneficial ownership as with breach; absent a clear term, it should not forfeit her equitable interest.

8. The heirs’ best argument is that the alleged promise was an intrafamily, premarital conditional gift, not an enforceable land contract, and that Claire’s acts were not unequivocally referable to ownership because they can be explained by permissive family occupancy. That argument becomes materially stronger if State X treats promises upon consideration of marriage as separately barred and refuses to extend part performance to that category. To make the argument succeed, the heirs would need to prove that Anna’s financial contributions were substantial, that Claire’s possession was never exclusive or owner-like, that rent handling remained under Anna’s control, that the improvements were ordinary or jointly funded family projects, and that Anna never made a definite promise of conveyance or trust beyond informal family encouragement.

9. The main uncertainties are doctrinal and factual: whether State X allows part performance to overcome a marriage-based Statute of Frauds objection; how “substantial” the improvements must be; whether possession must be exclusive; whether receipt of rents is necessary or merely corroborative; and whether an oral trust theory is treated differently from an oral conveyance theory. Counsel should also neutralize the heirs’ defenses by tracing Anna’s contributions and the rent stream, showing Claire’s net reliance remained substantial and that no express condition restricted Claire’s personal use of rents.

Bottom line: under a Neale-style equity framework, Claire has the stronger case for conveyance of the property, most plausibly through a constructive trust and order to convey, with damages reserved only as an alternative if State X takes a narrower view of part performance or estoppel.

It is stronger because Neale itself is the close analogue: an oral intra-family land arrangement tied to marriage, followed by possession and improvements. Promissory estoppel is also plausible because the promise was clear enough, reliance was foreseeable, and injustice would be serious after fifteen years and permanent improvements. Still, trial courts often face uncertainty about whether promissory estoppel may compel conveyance of land despite the statute, or only support reliance damages. For that reason, the principal route should be equitable title by constructive trust or specific performance, with estoppel serving as a fallback.

7. The heirs’ defenses are not likely to defeat Claire’s equity claim. First, the assertion that Anna paid for some improvements may reduce Claire’s net equitable claim and justify an accounting, but it does not defeat part performance unless Claire’s own expenditures were insubstantial or not clearly proven. The pleaded facts suggest the opposite. Second, Anna’s retention of some rental income may show she did not entirely surrender every incident of ownership, but it is not fatal where Claire lived there, managed the property, collected some rents herself, and invested heavily as an owner would. Again, that point sounds in accounting, not complete defeat. Third, the alleged “breach” based on Claire’s using some rental income for personal expenses is weak unless the heirs can prove a definite condition that rents were restricted to property expenses. The stated condition in the promise was marriage and moving onto the property, both of which Claire satisfied. Absent a clearly proven trust restriction, personal use of rents is consistent with beneficial ownership.

8. The heirs’ strongest non-frivolous argument is that Claire’s acts were not unequivocally referable to an enforceable promise to convey land, but were equally consistent with permissive family occupancy on Anna’s property. That argument gains force from the facts that Anna allegedly paid for some improvements, retained some rental income, never signed anything, and may have continued exercising dominion. To make that argument succeed at trial, the heirs would need clear proof that Claire was merely allowed to live there as a family member, that Anna remained the true decisionmaker and financier, that the improvements were not principally Claire’s or were expected under a license, and that the terms of the alleged trust or conveyance are too indefinite to enforce.

9. The main uncertainties are whether State X applies Neale-style part performance to family gift/trust cases as readily as to sale contracts; how substantial and permanent the improvements must be; whether possession must be exclusive; whether receipt of rents is necessary or merely corroborative; whether a marriage-consideration clause makes courts more reluctant; and whether promissory estoppel can support conveyance rather than only damages. Claire’s counsel should first plead specific performance and constructive trust, with promissory estoppel damages in the alternative, and expressly request an accounting so the court can adjust for Anna’s contributions without denying relief altogether. Counsel should second develop detailed proof of Claire’s possession and permanent improvements through receipts, contractor and tenant testimony, photographs, utility records, and other documents showing that the expenditures were substantial and primarily hers. Counsel should third secure testimony from family members and neutral witnesses that Anna repeatedly described the property as Claire’s, directed Ben to hold it for Claire’s benefit, praised the work, and allowed Claire to act as owner, because in a Neale-style case the credibility and definiteness of the oral arrangement are critical.

1. I assume State X follows the ordinary U.S. common-law Statute of Frauds for land transfers, but also recognizes the traditional equitable part-performance exception in the Neale v. The key standard is that the acts of reliance must be substantial and sufficiently referable to the alleged promise.

4. Claire’s case is stronger if the court treats Anna’s promise not as a mere revocable gift, but as an induced family settlement supported by reliance that Anna solicited. Marriage alone can be doctrinally awkward because promises made “upon consideration of marriage” often also fall within the Statute of Frauds. Even if the original promise had a donative flavor, Claire’s marriage, possession, and especially her $6,500 expenditure and major improvements convert the case from a bare gift into one of induced reliance that equity protects. At minimum, those acts are powerful inducement-based reliance; a court need not insist on classical bargain consideration to grant relief under Neale-style part performance.

5. The writing requirements potentially triggered are, first and definitely, the land-transfer provision, and, second and more uncertainly, any State X provision covering agreements made upon consideration of marriage. The land-transfer requirement is dispositive because Anna never signed a deed or memorandum. Claire’s answer is part performance: she married, moved onto the property, occupied it for fifteen years, built a large addition and outbuildings with her own money, improved landscaping and utilities, and collected rents, all with Anna’s knowledge and encouragement. Those acts are not easily explained as casual permission to use another’s land; they look like conduct undertaken by someone who believed she had the beneficial interest promised to her.

6. Classical part performance is Claire’s stronger theory. It directly fits Neale and supports the exact relief she wants: specific enforcement of an oral land promise. Modern promissory estoppel also helps because Anna made a clear promise, reasonably expected reliance, and Claire in fact relied to her detriment. But promissory estoppel is doctrinally less secure here because some courts refuse to use it to circumvent the land Statute of Frauds or confine it to reliance damages. If the court is comfortable with Neale-style equity, Claire should win specific performance or a constructive trust; promissory estoppel is the backup route to at least recover the value of her expenditures if the court finds the land promise insufficiently definite.

7. The heirs’ defenses are not likely to defeat Claire’s equity claim. The fact that Anna paid for some improvements weakens the claim only at the margins; it may affect accounting or offsets, but it does not negate Claire’s own substantial expenditures and possession. Anna’s retention of some rental income similarly creates ambiguity about the exact beneficial arrangement, but shared or intermittent rent collection is consistent with imperfect family administration and does not erase Claire’s long possession and owner-like improvements. The supposed “breach” based on Claire’s use of some rental income for personal expenses is the weakest defense unless the heirs can prove that using rents solely for property expenses was an actual condition of vesting. On the stated facts, the express conditions were marriage and moving onto the property, both satisfied. Personal use of rents is in fact consistent with beneficial ownership, not inconsistent with it.

8. The heirs’ best doctrinal argument is that Claire’s conduct was not unequivocally referable to an enforceable oral transfer of land, but equally explainable as permissive family occupancy on Anna’s land. Family settings make courts cautious: a daughter may live on a parent’s land, improve it, and handle rents without owning it. To make that argument succeed, the heirs would need to prove at trial that Anna materially financed and controlled the improvements, that Claire’s occupancy was understood as revocable permission rather than ownership, that Anna remained the true manager of the premises and rents, and that the alleged promise lacked definite terms about when and how title would pass or what Ben’s trusteeship meant.

9. The main uncertainties are whether State X requires both possession and substantial improvements, whether marriage-related promises trigger a separate statutory bar, whether receipt of rents is necessary or merely corroborative, how “substantial” the improvements must be, and whether promissory estoppel can override the land Statute of Frauds or only support damages. To maximize success, Claire’s counsel should first plead with precision an oral trust/specific-performance theory and, in the alternative, promissory-estoppel reliance damages, making clear that the same facts support either equitable title or fallback compensation. Second, counsel should develop a detailed evidentiary record of Claire’s expenditures, construction records, utilities work, possession, and rent collection over the fifteen years, tied chronologically to Anna’s statements and knowledge. Third, counsel should secure corroboration from witnesses and documents showing that Anna repeatedly described the property as Claire’s, directed Ben to hold title for Claire’s benefit, and treated Claire’s improvements as done in her capacity as beneficial owner rather than as a mere licensee.

Bottom line: under a Neale-style common-law framework, Claire has the better claim and should receive specific equitable relief, preferably a decree of conveyance or a constructive trust, with any disputed contributions or rents handled through an accounting rather than by denying enforcement altogether.

5. 5. 6. Under classical part performance, Claire has the stronger claim. The heirs’ strongest doctrinal argument is that Claire’s acts were not unequivocally referable to a definite contract to convey land, but are equally consistent with a permissive family occupancy or conditional gift arrangement. To make that argument succeed, the heirs would need to prove that Anna retained meaningful control, paid a substantial share of improvements, regularly took rents, never surrendered possession in an owner-like way, and that the alleged terms were too indefinite as to when title would pass, what Ben’s trusteeship meant, and whether Claire’s rights were present, future, or revocable.

9. The main uncertainties are jurisdictional and factual. State X may require possession plus substantial improvements, may treat marriage as insufficient part performance by itself, may insist that the acts be unequivocally referable, and may refuse to use promissory estoppel to circumvent the Statute of Frauds for land. There is also uncertainty over whether an oral express trust in land can be specifically enforced directly, or only through a constructive trust imposed to prevent fraud. To maximize success, Claire’s counsel should first plead in the alternative with precision: specific performance of the oral conveyance, constructive trust based on part performance/estoppel, and reliance damages under promissory estoppel if land-specific relief is denied. Second, counsel should develop concrete proof that Claire acted as beneficial owner, including receipts, permits, utility records, witnesses to Anna’s promise and supervision, and evidence of Claire’s possession and rent collection over fifteen years. Third, counsel should present an accounting that segregates Claire’s expenditures from Anna’s contributions and offers offsets for any rents Anna retained, thereby reducing the force of the heirs’ mixed-funding and breach arguments while preserving the core equity for conveyance.

Overall, under a Neale-style framework, the better recommendation is to grant equitable land-based relief, most likely by decree of conveyance or constructive trust, with accounting adjustments rather than denial of Claire’s claim.

1. I assume State X follows the ordinary U.S. common-law equitable approach reflected in Neale v. Neales: an oral promise concerning land is barred on its face by the Statute of Frauds, but equity may specifically enforce it, or impose a trust, when the promisee’s reliance amounts to part performance that is clearly referable to the promise and nonenforcement would itself work a fraud. That assumption matters because a strict jurisdiction that rejects promissory estoppel against the Statute of Frauds, or requires especially “unequivocal” acts for part performance, would make Claire’s case somewhat harder, though still substantial under Neale.

2. The clearest likely outcome is that Claire should prevail on equitable enforcement of the oral promise. 3. On its face, the dispositive doctrine is the Statute of Frauds requirement that transfers of interests in land be in writing; depending on State X’s statute, the alleged trust concerning land may also require a writing. The equitable doctrine most likely controlling the result is part performance, sometimes described as equitable estoppel against pleading the Statute of Frauds. Under Neale, equity enforces the oral land promise when the promisee, in reliance, enters into possession and makes substantial, lasting improvements or otherwise changes position in a way not readily explainable except by the alleged agreement, so that refusal to enforce would permit the promisor or heirs to perpetrate a fraud.

4. The facts support more than a mere revocable gift or bare license. True, Anna’s words sound donative—“give the lot”—and marriage can look like a condition rather than bargained-for consideration. But Claire did not merely marry; she moved onto the land, expended $6,500 of her own savings, built a large addition and outbuildings, improved landscaping and utilities, and treated the property as beneficially hers for fifteen years. Legally, the better characterization is that marriage and moving in were the inducement for the promise, while Claire’s possession and expenditures are the acts of reliance/part performance that make the promise enforceable in equity. Even if a court hesitates to call this a fully bargained exchange, it is not just an unenforceable expected gift once Claire materially changed position in the precise way Anna invited.

5. The writing requirements triggered are, first, the land-transfer provision of the Statute of Frauds and, second, possibly the rule requiring a writing for an express trust in land. There is also a possible “consideration of marriage” issue because the promise was made before marriage, though the practical dispute is about title to land. The land-transfer requirement is the main bar. Claire’s answer is that her possession, management of rents, and substantial permanent improvements remove the case from the writing requirement under part performance. Those acts are much closer to Neale than mere payment of money or mere continued family occupancy.

6. Claire could reach relief under either classical part performance or modern promissory estoppel, but part performance is the stronger route. It fits the land-specific equitable tradition and supports specific performance, which is especially apt because land is unique. Promissory estoppel also helps: Anna made a definite promise, expected Claire to rely by marrying and moving onto the property, Claire in fact relied extensively, and injustice is obvious if the heirs retain the enhanced land free of the promise. The uncertainty is remedial and jurisdictional: some courts use promissory estoppel only for reliance damages, and some are reluctant to let it override the Statute of Frauds where part performance doctrine already governs land cases. Thus, classical part performance is Claire’s best theory; promissory estoppel is a useful alternative.

7. The heirs’ specific defenses are weak on this record. The fact that Anna paid for some improvements does not defeat Claire’s claim unless it shows Claire’s own expenditures were minor or that Anna, not Claire, remained the true economic actor. Here Claire’s own $6,500 and the described substantial improvements remain significant; at most, Anna’s payments may justify an accounting or limit reimbursement issues. Anna’s retention of some rental income likewise does not defeat equity if Claire had possession and at least partial beneficial use of the property; shared rents may show an imperfect family arrangement, but not necessarily the absence of a promise. Finally, the claim that Claire breached a condition by using some rental income for personal expenses should fail unless the heirs prove a clear, express condition that all rents had to be property-related. Equity disfavors forfeiture, and personal use of rents is entirely consistent with beneficial ownership. At most, that allegation supports an accounting, not denial of title.

8. The heirs’ strongest doctrinal argument is that Claire’s conduct was not “unequivocally referable” to an enforceable land-transfer agreement, but instead is equally consistent with a family permission to live on Anna’s land, an expected future gift, or a revocable family arrangement. That argument gains force from Anna’s retaining legal title, paying for some improvements, keeping some rent, and never signing any memorial. To make it succeed at trial, the heirs would need to prove that Claire lacked exclusive possession or ownership-type control, that her expenditures were ordinary occupant improvements or were substantially funded/reimbursed by Anna, that Anna continued to act as owner in a way inconsistent with any trust for Claire, and that the alleged promise was too indefinite or too donative to be specifically enforced.

9. The main uncertainties are whether State X demands acts “unequivocally referable” rather than merely consistent with the promise; how substantial the improvements must be; whether possession alone would suffice without exclusive receipt of rents; whether an oral trust in land is treated differently from an oral promise to convey; and whether promissory estoppel may bypass the Statute of Frauds or only yield damages. To maximize success, Claire’s counsel should first plead in the alternative with precision: specific performance of the oral land promise, constructive trust/equitable trust, and promissory-estoppel damages if equitable title is denied. Second, counsel should build a detailed evidentiary record tracing Claire’s own funds, dates, permits, contractors, utility work, rental collection, and fifteen years of possession, coupled with testimony from tenants, neighbors, and family about Anna’s statements praising the work and acknowledging the property was Claire’s. Third, counsel should neutralize the heirs’ ambiguity defense by seeking an accounting that credits Anna for any proven contributions while showing that Claire’s improvements were permanent, substantial, and made only because Anna promised the property. On a Neale-style record, that should be enough for equitable enforcement.

1. I assume State X follows the mainstream U.S. common-law equitable framework reflected in Neale v. Neales, 76 U.S. (9 Wall.) 1 (1869): oral transfers of land are ordinarily barred by the Statute of Frauds, but equity may specifically enforce an oral promise when the promisee’s part performance is clearly proved and is sufficiently referable to the agreement that nonenforcement would itself operate as a fraud. 3. The dispositive doctrine on the face of the case is the Statute of Frauds requirement that transfers of interests in land be in writing, and likely also the writing requirement for an express trust concerning land. The doctrine that most likely controls the outcome, however, is part performance, sometimes described as equitable estoppel against pleading the Statute of Frauds. Under Neale, equity will enforce an oral land settlement when the promisee, induced by the promise, marries, takes possession, receives or exercises the benefits of the property, and makes valuable permanent improvements with the promisor’s knowledge, because refusal to perform after such reliance would amount to fraud.

4. The facts support more than a mere conditional gift or revocable license. Anna’s statement was framed as an exchange: Claire would marry and move onto the property, and Anna would give the lot to Claire and hold title for her benefit. Marriage can function as consideration, although it also raises a separate Statute of Frauds concern, and Claire’s move plus substantial expenditures make the bargain much stronger. At minimum, Claire’s acts are legally significant induced performance and reliance, not merely the fulfillment of a gratuitous condition. A court should characterize the improvements, occupancy, and assumption of ownership-like burdens as substantial performance referable to the promise, even if it hesitates to label the arrangement a fully bargained commercial contract.

5. The writing requirements triggered are, first, the land-transfer provision of the Statute of Frauds and, second, likely the requirement that a trust in land be evidenced by writing. There is also an arguable “consideration of marriage” Statute of Frauds issue because the promise was made before the wedding. The practically dispositive problem is still the land requirement, because the suit seeks conveyance of real property. Claire’s way around the bar is classic part performance: she entered and remained in possession, spent significant personal funds on permanent improvements, and exercised incidents of beneficial ownership by collecting rent and improving the premises over many years. Those acts are substantially referable to an ownership promise, not merely to temporary permission to reside there.

6. Classical part performance is Claire’s stronger route. These facts fit Neale unusually well: family promise, marriage, possession, permanent improvements, and the promisor’s knowledge and acquiescence. Modern promissory estoppel also supports relief because Anna’s promise foreseeably induced serious reliance and injustice would result if the heirs kept the improved property without compensation. But courts are less uniform about using promissory estoppel to overcome the land Statute of Frauds entirely, especially for specific performance. Accordingly, part performance is the stronger title-based doctrine, while promissory estoppel is the better fallback for reliance damages if the court is reluctant to order conveyance.

7. The heirs’ defenses likely do not defeat Claire’s claim. The fact that Anna paid for some improvements may reduce the scope of the equitable relief only if it shows Claire’s own expenditures were insubstantial or that the work was really Anna’s project; on the pleaded facts, Claire still made major personal investments, including a large addition and outbuildings. Anna’s retention of some rental income likewise weakens but does not eliminate Claire’s claim; mixed handling of rents in a family arrangement is compatible with an incompletely formalized equitable ownership and does not erase Claire’s long possession and improvements. The alleged “breach” based on Claire’s use of some rental funds for personal expenses is the weakest defense unless the promise was expressly conditioned on using all rents only for property-related purposes. On these facts, that sounds more like a possible accounting offset than a basis for forfeiting her entire equitable interest.

8. The heirs’ strongest doctrinal argument is that Claire’s conduct was not unequivocally referable to an enforceable contract to convey land, but instead to a family license or expected future gift. In intrafamily settings, occupation of a parent’s property and even substantial improvements can sometimes be explained by permissive use, shared family finances, or hope of inheritance rather than a binding land agreement. To make that argument succeed, the heirs would need to prove that Anna retained actual dominion throughout, that Claire’s expenditures were partly reimbursed or jointly undertaken in a way inconsistent with ownership, that rent control remained materially with Anna, and that Anna’s statements were indefinite donative intent rather than a definite promise to transfer title or hold for Claire’s benefit.

9. The main uncertainties are whether State X allows part performance to overcome any separate marriage-based Statute of Frauds objection, how substantial improvements must be before equity will act, and whether possession must be exclusive or accompanied by receipt of rents. Counsel should second assemble detailed proof that Claire’s acts were ownership-like and induced by the promise, including receipts, contractor testimony, photographs, utility and tax records, rental records, and witnesses to Anna’s repeated acknowledgments that the property was Claire’s. Counsel should third be prepared with a full rental-and-improvements accounting, including offsets for any Anna-funded work, so that any alleged misuse of rents is framed as a minor equitable adjustment rather than a material breach. Bottom line: under a Neale-style jurisdiction, Claire should likely obtain title-oriented equitable relief, with promissory-estoppel damages as a fallback only.

1. I assume State X follows the ordinary U.S. common-law equitable approach reflected in Neale v. Neales: an oral promise concerning land is facially barred by the Statute of Frauds, but equity may enforce it where the promisee, in reliance and with the promisor’s knowledge, takes possession and makes substantial permanent improvements such that nonenforcement would itself work a fraud. That assumption matters because, under a stricter state rule requiring acts “unequivocally referable” to a land transfer and rejecting estoppel against the land Statute of Frauds, Claire’s case becomes closer. I also assume State X allows constructive-trust or specific-performance relief against heirs who take title subject to the decedent’s equitable obligations.

2. The clearest conclusion is that Claire should prevail on her claim to enforce the oral promise in equity. On these facts, the best remedy is a decree declaring the heirs to hold title in constructive trust for Claire and ordering conveyance to her; that is functionally equivalent to specific performance and is cleaner than trying to validate an oral express trust as such. Her alternative promissory-estoppel damages claim should remain pleaded, but it is weaker and mainly a fallback if the court refuses land-specific relief.

3. The controlling doctrine on the face of the pleadings is the Statute of Frauds as to transfers of interests in land, and possibly also the statute’s clause for agreements made upon consideration of marriage if State X retains that traditional category. The equitable doctrine that most likely controls the result is part performance, sometimes framed as equitable estoppel against use of the statute as an instrument of fraud. Neale’s core standard is that where a marriage-related oral land promise is followed by possession and valuable improvements made in reliance and with the promisor’s knowledge, equity may enforce the arrangement because refusal to do so would permit fraud by the titleholder.

4. The better characterization is not a mere revocable license or naked conditional gift. Anna’s promise induced Claire to marry, move onto the land, and sink substantial personal funds into permanent improvements; those acts are legally significant reliance and part performance, and they also supply consideration in the broad equitable sense. Still, because “consideration of marriage” is itself within the Statute of Frauds, Claire should not depend on marriage alone as the bargain. Her strongest position is that the marriage, possession, expenditures, utility and landscaping work, and management of the tenant together show induced detrimental reliance and partial execution of the promised beneficial ownership.

5. The writing requirement is triggered first and foremost because this is an oral transfer of land or beneficial ownership in land. If State X separately requires writings for declarations of trust concerning land, that gate is also implicated. The dispositive problem is the absence of any signed writing by Anna. Claire’s response is classical part performance: she married and moved in as requested, remained in possession for fifteen years, paid substantial sums for permanent additions and outbuildings, improved utilities and landscaping, and exercised incidents of ownership by collecting rent, all with Anna’s knowledge and encouragement. Those facts are the type Neale treats as taking the case out of the statute in equity.

6. Under classical part performance, Claire has a strong claim because she has the classic trio of possession, substantial improvements, and reliance known to the promisor. Under modern promissory estoppel, she also has a plausible alternative: Anna made a clear promise, expected reliance, and Claire’s reliance was substantial and foreseeable, so injustice may require a remedy. But part performance is the stronger route because many jurisdictions are wary of using promissory estoppel to circumvent the land Statute of Frauds, or they confine estoppel to reliance damages rather than conveyance. On these facts, Neale-style part performance better supports land-specific relief.

7. The heirs’ defenses are not likely to defeat Claire’s equity claim, though they may affect accounting. Anna’s paying for some improvements does not negate Claire’s own substantial expenditures; it may reduce the reimbursable or equitable amount but does not erase part performance if Claire’s contributions were material and permanent. Anna’s retention of some rental income likewise shows the arrangement was imperfectly executed, not necessarily nonexistent; mixed control over rents is consistent with family informality and may call for an accounting, not denial of title. The alleged “breach” because Claire used some rents for personal expenses is weak unless the heirs prove an actual condition that all rents had to be used solely for the property. The stated condition in the facts is marriage and moving onto the property, which Claire satisfied. Absent a defined trust term limiting rent use, personal use of some rents is at most a minor issue relevant to offsets, not forfeiture of the entire equitable claim.

8. The heirs’ best doctrinal argument is that Claire’s conduct is not sufficiently unequivocally referable to a contract or trust to convey land, because in a family setting long occupancy, improvements, supervision by the owner, shared funding, and rent-sharing can all be explained by a permissive license or expectation of future bounty rather than present equitable ownership. To make that argument succeed at trial, they would need to prove that Anna remained the true economic owner in practice: that Anna paid a significant share of the capital work, controlled the tenant and rents, treated Claire as a mere occupant, and never made statements or directions clearly indicating that Claire already had the beneficial interest. If the court sees the arrangement as a familial accommodation rather than a partially executed land transfer, the Statute of Frauds may bar relief.

9. The main uncertainties are doctrinal and evidentiary. State X may require acts that are “unequivocally referable,” may insist on possession plus substantial improvements rather than either alone, may treat the marriage-consideration clause as an additional barrier, and may refuse promissory estoppel as a way around the land statute. A trial court may also question how substantial Claire’s improvements were once Anna’s contributions and the rent flows are netted out. To maximize success, Claire’s counsel should first plead specific performance and constructive trust in the alternative, while also pleading promissory estoppel damages against all heirs and the estate representative so there is no remedial gap. Second, counsel should assemble detailed proof that Claire’s acts were permanent, substantial, and ownership-like: receipts, contractors, photos, utility records, tax and maintenance records, tenant records, and an accounting separating Claire’s funds from Anna’s. Third, counsel should secure corroborating testimony and admissions showing the promise and Anna’s assent, especially evidence that Anna said the property was Claire’s, directed Ben to act as trustee, and knowingly stood by while Claire made improvements she would not have made as a mere licensee.

Bottom line: under a Neale-style equitable framework, Claire has the better claim and should receive a constructive trust and conveyance, with any disputes over Anna’s contributions or rent usage handled through accounting rather than forfeiture.

The equitable doctrine most likely controlling the result is part performance, understood in Neale as conduct that so materially changes the promisee’s position in reliance on the oral land promise that it would be fraudulent to permit the promisor or heirs to invoke the statute. Neale’s core standard is that marriage alone is not enough, but marriage coupled with possession and substantial, valuable improvements made in reliance on the promise and with the promisor’s knowledge can take the case out of the statute and warrant specific enforcement.

4. The facts support treating this as more than a mere gratuitous promise. Those acts are the kind equity treats as “unequivocally referable” to an ownership arrangement rather than a mere revocable license, though the marriage-related Statute of Frauds issue remains the doctrinal complication.

6. Claire can reach relief under both classical part performance and modern promissory estoppel, but part performance is stronger. Under Neale, the combination of possession and substantial improvements to the land is precisely what supports specific performance of an oral family land promise. Promissory estoppel is a useful alternative because Anna’s promise foreseeably induced marriage, relocation, and expenditures, and injustice would be serious if Claire were left only with the loss. But modern estoppel doctrine is less certain in Statute of Frauds cases: some courts allow it freely, others only for reliance damages, and some resist using it to compel conveyance of land. Thus the cleaner path to the land itself is Neale-style part performance; promissory estoppel is the fallback if the court is uneasy about the marriage aspect or the precision of the oral terms.

7. The heirs’ defenses likely do not defeat Claire’s equity claim unless they materially undercut the inference of reliance and ownership. Third, the claim that Claire breached a condition by using some rents for personal expenses is weak unless the oral promise clearly imposed a condition that rents be used solely for property-related purposes. 8. 9. The result could change with several uncertainties: whether State X allows part performance to overcome the marriage-consideration provision at all; how “substantial” the improvements must be; whether possession must be exclusive; and whether receipt of rents and profits is necessary or merely corroborative. To maximize success, Claire’s counsel should first plead in the alternative for specific performance, constructive trust, and promissory-estoppel damages, expressly alleging possession and improvements as acts unequivocally referable to the oral promise. Second, counsel should build a tight evidentiary record tracing Claire’s personal expenditures, the scope and permanence of the improvements, and Anna’s knowledge and assent through receipts, photographs, utility and tax records, and witness testimony. Second, counsel should assemble detailed proof that Claire’s possession and improvements were substantial, permanent, and primarily Claire-funded, including receipts, contractor testimony, before-and-after evidence, and admissions by Anna praising the work as Claire’s. Bottom line: assuming State X follows Neale’s equitable approach, Claire should likely obtain a constructive trust or specific performance, subject to an accounting; promissory-estoppel damages are the backup remedy if the court applies the Statute of Frauds more rigidly.

1. I assume State X follows the traditional U.S. common-law equity approach reflected in Neale v. Neales, under which an oral agreement concerning land is ordinarily barred by the Statute of Frauds but may be specifically enforced if there is clear proof of the agreement plus part performance that would make nonenforcement itself fraudulent. I also assume State X recognizes promissory estoppel at least as a possible basis for reliance damages, though perhaps not always for conveyance of land. That assumption matters because the result turns less on contract formalities than on whether Claire’s possession and improvements are treated, as in Neale, as sufficiently referable to the promise to remove the Statute’s bar.

2. The clearest bottom line is that Claire should prevail on her claim to enforce the oral promise. These facts are very close to a Neale-style case: the promise was made before marriage, Claire married and moved onto the land in reliance, she remained there for years, spent substantial personal funds on permanent improvements, exercised dominion over the property, and did so with Anna’s knowledge and encouragement. The best remedy is a decree compelling the heirs to convey legal title to Claire. If the court is uneasy about treating the oral “trust” language as an enforceable express trust, it should impose a constructive trust on the heirs and order conveyance. Damages under promissory estoppel should be treated as a fallback, not the primary remedy.

3. On its face, the dispositive doctrine is the Statute of Frauds requirement that transfers of interests in land be evidenced by a signed writing. The equitable doctrine that most likely controls the result is part performance, sometimes framed as equitable estoppel against pleading the Statute. Neale’s core standard is that equity will not allow the Statute to be used as an instrument of fraud where the promisee, in reliance on an oral land arrangement, has taken possession and made substantial, permanent improvements or otherwise acted in a manner clearly referable to the agreement. Claire’s long possession, improvements, and tenant-related use fit that pattern.

4. The harder characterization issue is whether Anna made a real bargain or merely a conditional gift. Marriage alone can look like a condition attached to a donative promise, especially in a family setting. But Claire’s conduct goes beyond acceptance of a gift. She changed position substantially by moving in, investing her own money in permanent additions and outbuildings, improving utilities and landscaping, and acting as the property’s practical manager. A court should therefore characterize her acts at minimum as induced reliance sufficient for equitable enforcement, and quite plausibly as consideration in fact, rather than as a mere revocable license. Neale is important here because it treats comparable family reliance as enough to justify specific enforcement even where the transaction has a donative cast.

5. The triggered Statute of Frauds category is chiefly the writing requirement for transfers of land. The oral direction that Ben would “hold title for her benefit” also implicates the writing requirement for a trust in land. There is a possible additional wrinkle that the promise was made in contemplation of marriage, but the land-transfer aspect is the practical center of the case, and Neale suggests equity focuses on the land and the reliance acts. Part performance is Claire’s route around the Statute: she will argue that taking possession, remaining for fifteen years, making major permanent improvements with personal funds, and collecting rents are acts not naturally explained except by the promised beneficial ownership.

6. Claire could reach relief under either classical part performance or modern promissory estoppel, but part performance is the stronger route. It is stronger because her acts are land-specific and because the relief she wants is land-specific: conveyance or a trust in her favor. Modern promissory estoppel would also help because Anna made a foreseeable promise, Claire relied substantially, and injustice is obvious if the heirs keep the improved property free of the promise. Still, many courts are reluctant to use promissory estoppel to override the land Statute of Frauds when traditional part-performance doctrine is available, and some limit promissory estoppel to reliance damages. So Claire’s best theory is Neale-style specific enforcement, with promissory estoppel as an alternative damages theory if the court finds the proof of the land agreement too uncertain.

7. The heirs’ listed defenses are not likely to defeat Claire’s equity claim. The fact that Anna paid for some improvements weakens the inference that every expenditure was Claire’s alone, but it does not erase Claire’s substantial personal contribution; at most it supports an accounting or offset. The fact that Anna retained some rental income likewise shows imperfect transfer of control, but Neale does not require total exclusivity in rents; receipt of rents is evidentiary, not indispensable, and Claire still exercised meaningful dominion. The claim that Claire “breached a condition” by using some rentals for personal expenses is weakest unless the heirs can prove a clear condition that all rents had to be applied only to the property. If Claire was the promised beneficial owner, using rents personally is not obviously inconsistent with that status. Absent a clearly defined condition precedent or material condition subsequent, this point should not defeat specific enforcement, though it may justify a limited accounting.

8. The heirs’ strongest nonfrivolous doctrinal argument is that Claire’s acts are not sufficiently unequivocally referable to a binding contract to convey land because this was an intra-family arrangement that could equally be understood as permissive occupancy under a revocable license or expected inheritance. That argument gains force from the facts that Anna never signed anything, sometimes paid for improvements, sometimes kept rent, and never fully surrendered legal or economic control. To make that argument succeed at trial, the heirs would need to prove that Anna’s words were precatory or donative rather than contractual, that the terms of the alleged promise were indefinite, that Claire’s expenditures were ordinary family-use improvements rather than ownership-type investments, and that Anna’s continuing control was inconsistent with any transfer of beneficial ownership.

9. The result could change depending on several uncertainties: whether State X requires possession plus substantial improvements, or insists that the acts be “unequivocally referable” to the oral land agreement; whether a separate Statute of Frauds rule for promises made on consideration of marriage is treated as an independent bar; whether partial receipt of rents matters materially; and whether promissory estoppel can support conveyance or only reliance damages. To maximize success, Claire’s counsel should first plead in the alternative for specific performance, constructive trust, and reliance damages so the court has a full remedial path even if it rejects one theory. Second, counsel should assemble dense proof that the promise was definite and known to others, including witness testimony, receipts, photographs, utility and permit records, tenant records, and evidence tying Claire’s expenditures to Anna’s promise and consent. Third, counsel should specifically rebut the “license” and “condition breach” theories by tracing who paid for which improvements, showing Claire’s long possession and management were ownership-like, and proving there was no clear restriction requiring rental income to be used only for property expenses.

Overall, under a Neale-style approach, Claire has the better claim and should receive equitable conveyance of the property, with promissory-estoppel damages only as a backup.

Claire’s long occupancy, improvements, and treatment of the premises as her own fit that pattern.

4. The formation issue is the heirs’ best way to recast this as a mere conditional gift or revocable family permission. 5. Still, the land-transfer bar is the practical obstacle. Under Neale-style doctrine, those acts can remove the transaction from the statute because they are difficult to explain as merely casual occupancy and because nonenforcement would work a fraud after fifteen years of reliance.

6. Claire can argue both classical part performance and modern promissory estoppel, but part performance is stronger. Part performance is stronger because it is specifically tailored to oral land promises and supports land-specific equitable relief, which is exactly what Claire wants. Promissory estoppel is more uncertain: some courts allow it to overcome the Statute of Frauds only in exceptional cases, while others refuse to use it to contradict the land statute absent unconscionable injury. Claire has a decent estoppel claim because the promise was clear enough and the reliance substantial, foreseeable, and long-lived, but if State X is cautious about promissory estoppel in land cases, Neale-style part performance is the safer path.

7. The heirs’ specific defenses probably do not defeat Claire’s claim. First, the fact that Anna paid for some improvements goes to degree, not entitlement: mixed funding may require an accounting, but it does not erase Claire’s own substantial expenditures and reliance. Second, Anna’s retention of some rental income weakens the picture of complete beneficial ownership, yet it is not fatal where Claire occupied the property, also collected rent, and made major owner-like improvements over many years; many family arrangements involve imperfect bookkeeping without negating the underlying promise. Third, the claim that Claire breached a condition by using some rents for personal expenses should fail unless the heirs can prove a clear and material condition that all rents had to be reinvested in the property. 8. The single best counterargument is that Claire’s acts were not unequivocally referable to a contract or trust to convey land, but are equally consistent with a permissive family occupancy under an intended future gift or informal support arrangement. That argument becomes serious if the heirs can prove at trial that Anna remained the true economic owner throughout: that she funded the principal improvements, controlled the property, retained most rents, paid taxes or utilities, and never allowed Claire exclusive possession under a claim of right. If State X requires possession and improvements that can be explained only by the alleged contract, those facts could let the heirs keep the Statute of Frauds bar in place.

9. The result could change depending on several uncertainties: whether State X requires “unequivocally referable” acts or merely substantial reliance; how substantial the improvements must be; whether possession alone is ever enough; whether receipt of rents is necessary to show beneficial ownership; and whether promissory estoppel may override the land Statute of Frauds at all. To maximize success, Claire’s counsel should first plead specific performance, constructive trust, and promissory estoppel in the alternative, expressly tying the case to Neale’s pattern of marriage, possession, and lasting improvements. Second, counsel should build a detailed evidentiary record of Claire’s expenditures and possession—receipts, permits, photographs, utility records, tenant records, witness testimony, and Anna’s statements praising and supervising the work—to show reliance unequivocally referable to ownership. Third, counsel should preempt the defenses with an accounting that separates Claire-funded improvements from Anna’s contributions, explains rent flows, and frames any personal use of rents as either permitted, immaterial, or long acquiesced in by Anna.

2. Claire should likely prevail on her claim to enforce the oral promise. 3. On its face, the dispositive doctrine is the Statute of Frauds requirement that transfers of interests in land be in writing; the oral trust direction to Ben would also ordinarily require writing if treated as an express trust of land. Neale’s core standard is that marriage alone is not enough, but marriage coupled with possession, enjoyment of the premises or rents, and substantial improvements undertaken in reliance on the promise can justify specific enforcement because those acts are not readily undone and make repudiation fraudulent.

4. The better characterization is that this was more than a mere revocable family license or gratuitous promise. Under Neale, those acts can remove the land contract from the statute in equity. Neale is directly analogous because it treats marriage plus possession plus improvements as sufficient equitable part performance in a family land-transfer setting. Promissory estoppel would require a clear promise, foreseeable reliance, actual reliance, and injustice absent relief.
