3. On its face, the case falls within the Statute of Frauds because it concerns a transfer of land and, as stated, an oral direction that title be held “for her benefit,” which also resembles an oral trust of land. The equitable doctrine most likely controlling the result is part performance, sometimes described as equitable estoppel against using the Statute of Frauds as an instrument of fraud. Under Neale, equity will enforce a definite oral family settlement involving land when the promisee, induced by the promise and with the promisor’s acquiescence, marries, takes possession, enjoys the property, and makes valuable lasting improvements such that nonenforcement would be inequitable.

4. The facts are stronger than a mere conditional gift or revocable license. Anna did not simply say she hoped someday to give Claire the property; she tied the transfer to Claire’s marriage and moving onto the land, then stood by while Claire materially changed position and invested her own money. Marriage in an ante-nuptial family settlement is real consideration, though many courts are reluctant to treat marriage alone as the sort of “part performance” that defeats the Statute of Frauds. The better characterization is that Claire’s marriage and move were the requested inducements, while her possession and substantial expenditures supply the most persuasive evidence of either bargain or equity-supporting reliance. Even if a court labels the promise donative, Claire’s conduct exceeds a bare license.

5. The writing requirement plainly triggered is the land-transfer provision of the Statute of Frauds. The trust language also implicates the usual writing requirement for an express trust in land. If State X retains the traditional Statute of Frauds provision for agreements made upon consideration of marriage, that clause may also be implicated because Anna’s promise was made before Claire married. The most important barrier, however, is still the land-writing requirement. Claire’s response is that her possession, permanent improvements, and partial receipt of rents are the classic acts of part performance that, under Neale-style doctrine, remove the case from the statute’s bar.

6. Classical part performance is Claire’s strongest theory. These facts are close to the paradigm equity case: long possession plus substantial, lasting improvements that are difficult to explain unless Claire believed she held beneficial ownership. Modern promissory estoppel is also plausible because Anna’s promise foreseeably induced serious reliance and injustice would follow from refusal to compensate. But promissory estoppel is doctrinally less certain in land cases: some courts permit it only for damages, some require “unconscionable” injury, and some resist using it to bypass the land Statute of Frauds where part performance is the established route. Accordingly, Claire’s primary path should be equitable conveyance under part performance; promissory-estoppel damages are the backup.

7. The heirs’ specific defenses are not likely to defeat Claire’s equity claim. Anna’s payment for some improvements may reduce the extent of Claire’s net reliance, but it does not negate the large improvements Claire herself financed and made in reliance on the promise; at most it supports an accounting or setoff. Anna’s retention of some rental income likewise weakens but does not destroy Claire’s case. Receipt of rents is corroborative evidence of beneficial ownership under Neale-style reasoning, not an all-or-nothing prerequisite, and mixed rent handling is consistent with an informal family trust arrangement. The supposed breach—that Claire used some rents for personal expenses—also seems weak unless the heirs can prove that exclusive use of rent for property expenses was an express and material condition. The facts as stated identify marriage and moving in as the conditions; personal use of some rental proceeds is entirely consistent with Claire’s position that the property was held for her benefit.

8. The heirs’ single best argument is that Claire’s conduct was not unequivocally referable to a contract or trust to transfer ownership, but instead just as consistent with permissive family occupancy and expectation of a future gift. Courts are often skeptical in intra-family cases because relatives commonly live on land and improve it without any legally enforceable transfer. To make that argument succeed at trial, the heirs would need to prove that Anna retained the normal incidents of ownership throughout: that she paid for the major work, controlled rents, made the key property decisions, paid taxes and utilities as owner, and stated or demonstrated that Claire was allowed to live there but was never to own it. If that factual picture emerges, a court could deny specific enforcement.

9. The main uncertainties are whether State X requires acts “unequivocally referable” to ownership, whether part performance applies equally to an oral trust of land, whether receipt of rents is necessary or merely corroborative, whether a marriage-consideration clause independently bars enforcement, and whether promissory estoppel can support conveyance rather than only damages. To maximize success, Claire’s counsel should first plead in the alternative an oral contract to convey, constructive trust/specific performance, and promissory-estoppel reliance damages. Counsel should second develop detailed corroboration of Anna’s promise and Claire’s referable reliance through receipts, permits, photographs, utility records, contractor testimony, and testimony that Anna repeatedly treated the property as Claire’s. Counsel should third seek a full accounting tracing every improvement payment and every rental receipt so the mixed-funding and mixed-rent facts are handled as offsets, not as proof that no equitable ownership interest ever arose.

1. I assume State X follows the ordinary U.S. common-law equity rule reflected in Neale v. Neales, 76 U.S. (9 Wall.) 1 (1869): an oral agreement concerning land is within the Statute of Frauds, but equity may enforce it where the promise is clearly proved and the promisee has partly performed by taking possession and making substantial, permanent improvements, or otherwise changing position so that nonenforcement would itself operate as a fraud. 2. On these facts, Claire should likely prevail on her claim to enforce the oral promise in equity. 4. Claire’s case is stronger if the court treats her acts not as mere acceptance of a gift, but as induced reliance and performance in return for Anna’s promise. Those acts are not just conditions of a gratuitous promise in the abstract; they are the kind of performance and detriment equity treats as consideration-like reliance. Even if a court hesitates to call this a classic bargain, Neale supports enforcing such family settlements where the promise induced substantial, irreversible change of position.

5. The writing requirements potentially triggered are, first, the land-transfer provision of the Statute of Frauds and, second, possibly the “agreement in consideration of marriage” category because the promise was made before Claire’s marriage and conditioned on marriage. The land provision is the clearest bar. Part performance is Claire’s answer to that bar: her possession, extensive improvements, and beneficial use of the property are the classic acts equity uses to remove the case from the statute. The marriage category creates more uncertainty, because some courts are reluctant to let part performance circumvent the specific policy requiring marriage settlements to be written. Still, Neale strongly suggests that where the marriage promise is followed by possession and improvements of land, equity may grant relief to prevent fraud.

6. Claire can argue both classical part performance and modern promissory estoppel, but part performance is plainly stronger. Her possession and permanent improvements fit Neale almost exactly, and land-specific equitable relief is the traditional response. Promissory estoppel is a viable alternative because Anna made a clear promise, expected reliance, Claire relied substantially, and injustice would follow from nonenforcement. The uncertainty is doctrinal: some courts allow promissory estoppel to avoid the Statute of Frauds; others confine it or limit relief to reliance damages. Thus, if State X is orthodox and Neale-like, the court should prefer part performance for specific relief; promissory estoppel is the backup if the court is unwilling to enforce the oral land promise directly.

7. The heirs’ defenses likely do not defeat Claire’s equity claim. If Anna paid for some improvements, that does not erase Claire’s own substantial expenditures and permanent changes to the property; it goes only to weight and perhaps to accounting. If Anna retained some rental income, that weakens but does not destroy Claire’s showing of beneficial possession. Exclusive receipt of rents is helpful under Neale, not invariably essential; the key question is whether Claire’s occupancy and improvements are still more naturally explained by the alleged promise than by a revocable family license. The alleged “breach” that Claire used some rentals for personal expenses is the weakest defense unless the heirs can prove an actual condition of the promise that rents be used only for property-related purposes. The stated condition appears to have been marriage and moving onto the property, which Claire performed. Equity is unlikely to treat incidental use of rental proceeds as a forfeiture of the promised land absent clear proof of a material express condition.

8. The heirs’ strongest doctrinal argument is that Claire’s conduct was not unequivocally referable to an enforceable land-transfer agreement, but equally consistent with a familial permission to live on Anna’s property and improve it, coupled with an oral donative promise in consideration of marriage that the Statute of Frauds specifically requires to be written. To make that argument succeed at trial, the heirs would need to prove that Anna retained real dominion over the property, that Claire’s occupancy and use were permissive rather than proprietary, that the improvements were materially funded or controlled by Anna, that the rental arrangements showed Anna remained the beneficial owner, and that the alleged trustee arrangement and promise lacked definite terms. If the court sees the case as an unenforceable parol gift rather than a partly performed land settlement, relief could be denied.

9. The main uncertainties are whether State X applies Neale fully to oral marriage-related land promises, whether part performance can overcome both the land and marriage branches of the Statute of Frauds, how “substantial” and “unequivocally referable” Claire’s improvements must be, and whether partial rather than exclusive receipt of rents suffices. To maximize success, Claire’s counsel should first plead the case in the alternative as specific performance of an oral land agreement, constructive trust based on part performance/estoppel, and reliance damages under promissory estoppel. Second, counsel should develop precise proof of possession and improvements: dates of move-in, receipts, contractor testimony, photographs, utility upgrades, permit records, and evidence tracing Claire’s $6,500 and later expenditures. Third, counsel should secure direct evidence of Anna’s promise and Ben’s trustee role—through admissions, family witnesses, letters, statements praising the work, or any acts by Anna recognizing Claire as beneficial owner—because clear proof of the promise and Claire’s reliance is what turns Neale-style equity from sympathy into enforceable relief.

1. I assume State X follows the ordinary U.S. common-law Statute of Frauds for interests in land, but also retains the equitable part-performance principle applied in Neale v. If State X instead treats the “consideration of marriage” clause as independently immune from part-performance, Claire’s case becomes harder.

2. The clearest recommendation is that Claire should prevail on her claim to enforce the oral promise in equity. The best remedy is specific performance in the form of a decree requiring conveyance of legal title, or, if title mechanics are disputed because Anna purported to “hold title for Claire’s benefit” and orally involved Ben as trustee, a constructive trust or decree declaring the heirs to hold title for Claire and ordering conveyance. On these facts, damages should be secondary only. 3. The controlling doctrine on the face of the case is the Statute of Frauds requirement that transfers of interests in land be in writing. The equitable doctrine most likely controlling the result is part performance, sometimes described as equitable estoppel against pleading the statute. Under Neale, the key inquiry is whether the promisee, in reliance on the oral land undertaking, so changed position through possession, improvements, and acts of ownership, with the promisor’s knowledge and acquiescence, that refusal to enforce would work a fraud. Claire’s long possession, substantial expenditures, improvements, and enjoyment of rental benefits fit that standard.

4. Claire’s case is stronger if the court characterizes Anna’s promise as an induced exchange, not a bare conditional gift. Marriage has historically counted as valuable consideration, and here it was coupled with a required move onto the property and major expenditures by Claire. Even if a court hesitates to call the promise a bargained-for contract in the modern sense, Claire’s acts are at least reliance that Anna invited and expected. The better characterization is that marriage and relocation were part of the inducement, while the $6,500 outlay and improvements were reliance acts that make the promise specifically enforceable in equity. This is more than a revocable family license.

5. Two Statute of Frauds categories are implicated. The principal one is the land-transfer category because Anna promised to give Claire the lot and buildings and to hold title for her benefit. A second possible category is agreements made upon consideration of marriage, because the promise was made before Claire’s marriage and conditioned on it. The land category is the more direct bar; part performance is argued to remove that bar because Claire married, moved in, possessed the property for fifteen years, built major additions and outbuildings, improved utilities and landscaping, and collected rent, all with Anna’s knowledge. The marriage category introduces uncertainty because some courts treat it more rigidly.

6. Claire can proceed under both classical part performance and modern promissory estoppel, but part performance is the stronger path. Classical part performance fits the facts almost exactly: possession plus substantial, permanent improvements and acts of ownership are the classic grounds for specific performance of an oral land promise under Neale. Promissory estoppel is a useful alternative, especially if State X requires “unconscionable injury” or allows only damages when estoppel is used against the statute. But many courts are cautious about using promissory estoppel to nullify the Statute of Frauds. Here, because the reliance is land-specific and longstanding, Neale-style equity is more natural and more likely to support conveyance rather than only money relief.

7. The heirs’ defenses likely do not defeat Claire’s equity claim. First, the assertion that Anna paid for some improvements weakens, but does not eliminate, part performance; the issue is whether Claire herself made substantial, reliance-based improvements, and the facts say she spent $6,500 and made major additions and utility work. Second, Anna’s retention of some rental income does not necessarily negate Claire’s beneficial possession; it matters only if it shows Anna kept such control that Claire’s occupancy looked merely permissive. Third, Claire’s use of some rentals for personal expenses does not obviously breach any proved condition of the oral promise. Unless the heirs can show a clear condition that all rents be used only for property purposes, that point sounds at most in accounting, not forfeiture of title.

8. The heirs’ strongest argument is that Claire’s conduct was not unequivocally referable to an enforceable promise to convey land, but instead consistent with a permissive family arrangement or license. That is the best doctrinal route because part performance requires acts that point to the land agreement itself, not merely to residence on family property. To make that argument succeed, the heirs would need to prove at trial that Anna retained dominant control over the property, funded the important improvements, kept the meaningful rental benefits, and treated Claire’s occupancy as a revocable accommodation rather than ownership-in-waiting. If they can make Claire’s expenditures look like ordinary family living expenses or improvements by a permissive occupant, the equitable case narrows substantially.

9. The main uncertainties are whether State X allows part performance to overcome not only the land clause but also a marriage-consideration clause; how “substantial” and “unequivocally referable” the improvements must be; and whether possession must be exclusive or be accompanied by receipt of rents or other acts of ownership. Claire’s counsel should therefore do three things. First, plead specific performance and constructive trust in the alternative, while also pleading promissory estoppel damages if equity is denied. Second, develop exact proof that Claire personally financed identifiable, permanent improvements and took possession in reliance on Anna’s promise, including dates, amounts, contractors, and Anna’s statements approving the work. Third, neutralize the heirs’ control arguments by documenting Claire’s long occupancy, management of the tenant, use of the property as her own, and by offering an accounting that explains rental flows and shows any personal use of rents was immaterial or offset.

Bottom line: under a Neale-style common-law framework, Claire has the better claim and should receive conveyance or a constructive trust, with promissory estoppel damages reserved only as a fallback if State X applies the Statute of Frauds more rigidly.

But the stronger view is that Anna solicited concrete acts—marriage, relocation, possession, and occupation under a trustee-like arrangement—and then knowingly accepted Claire’s major expenditures and owner-like conduct. Even if the initial promise is not a classic bargain contract, Claire’s acts are at minimum induced reliance and part performance, which is enough under Neale-style equity. Her $6,500 expenditure and permanent improvements are especially important because they move the case beyond a bare promise attached to marriage.

5. Two Statute of Frauds gates are triggered. First, this is an oral transfer of land, or at least an oral promise to convey beneficial ownership of land, which ordinarily requires a signed writing. Second, because the promise was expressly tied to Claire’s marriage, the “agreement upon consideration of marriage” category may also be implicated. The land-transfer category is the practical center of gravity. Claire’s argument is that her possession plus permanent improvements remove the bar under part performance. There is some uncertainty, however, because a court may ask whether part performance defeats only the land-transfer rule or also the marriage-consideration rule. The safest route is to treat the case as one for equitable enforcement of an oral land settlement proved by possession and improvements, with marriage supplying context but not doing all the work.

6. Claire can argue both classical part performance and modern promissory estoppel, but part performance is the stronger path. Under classical doctrine, she has the most important indicators: possession, substantial valuable improvements, long acquiescence by the titleholder, and conduct strongly referable to an ownership promise. Under modern promissory estoppel, she can also show a clear promise, foreseeable reliance, actual reliance, and likely injustice absent enforcement. The weakness of promissory estoppel is doctrinal: some courts are reluctant to let it override the Statute of Frauds in land cases except for damages, fearing it would swallow the writing rule. By contrast, Neale-style part performance is tailored exactly to this kind of dispute and better supports title-based relief.

7. The heirs’ specific defenses are not likely to defeat Claire’s equity claim. Anna’s payment for some improvements may narrow the measure of Claire’s reliance, but it does not undo the undisputed fact that Claire herself spent substantial funds and made permanent changes with Anna’s knowledge. Mixed funding weakens the neatness of the proof, not the existence of part performance. Anna’s retention of some rental income likewise does not defeat the claim. It may show that legal title and some incidents of control remained with Anna, but that is consistent with Anna’s alleged retention of title while holding for Claire’s benefit; it does not negate Claire’s possession or improvements. The alleged “breach” based on Claire’s use of some rent for personal expenses is weakest. The stated condition was marriage and moving onto the property, not a covenant to segregate rents for property use only. At most, the heirs might seek an accounting or offset if Claire misapplied funds that in equity belonged elsewhere; that should not forfeit her basic claim to the land.

8. The heirs’ best non-frivolous argument is that Claire’s conduct was not unequivocally referable to a definite oral conveyance or trust, but only to a family license or expectation of future generosity. Because this arose in a close family setting, possession, improvements, and even rent collection can sometimes be explained as permissive occupancy on a parent’s land, especially where the parent also paid for improvements, supervised work, and kept some rents. To make that argument succeed, the heirs would need to prove at trial that Anna never definitively promised present beneficial ownership, that Ben’s supposed trustee role was vague or nonexistent, that Claire’s expenditures were ordinary family-sharing expenses rather than owner-level improvements, and that Anna’s continuing financial control showed Claire understood she had no enforceable property right.

9. The principal uncertainties are doctrinal and evidentiary. A court may differ on how “substantial” the improvements must be, whether possession alone plus improvements suffices without exclusive receipt of rents, whether part performance can bypass the marriage-consideration branch of the Statute of Frauds, and whether the oral arrangement is best treated as specific performance of a conveyance or imposition of a constructive trust. To maximize success, Claire’s counsel should first plead in the alternative with precision: specific performance, constructive trust, and promissory-estoppel damages, while alleging the oral promise’s exact terms and Ben’s trustee designation. Second, counsel should assemble detailed proof that Claire’s acts were unequivocally referable to ownership: receipts, permits, utility and contractor records, photos, witness testimony, and Anna’s statements praising or acknowledging Claire’s beneficial interest. Third, counsel should preempt the mixed-funding and rent arguments by offering a careful accounting showing Claire’s own net expenditures, the permanence of the improvements, and why any personal use of rents is either consistent with beneficial ownership or remediable by offset rather than forfeiture.

1. I assume State X follows the ordinary U.S. common-law equitable approach reflected in Neale v. Neales, 76 U.S. (9 Wall.) 1 (1869): an oral transfer or family settlement of land is facially within the Statute of Frauds, but equity may enforce it through specific performance or a trust where the promisee, in reliance and with the promisor’s assent, takes possession and makes substantial permanent improvements such that nonenforcement would work a fraud. I also assume State X recognizes promissory estoppel at least as an alternative damages theory, though some jurisdictions limit estoppel against the Statute of Frauds. That assumption matters because under a stricter rule—especially one treating promises made “in consideration of marriage” as categorically unenforceable absent a writing—the result would be less certain.

2. On these facts, Claire should likely prevail on her claim to enforce the oral promise. The strongest remedy is equitable conveyance of title, implemented either as specific performance requiring the heirs to deed the property to Claire or, if the court is more comfortable avoiding enforcement of an oral express trust, by imposing a constructive trust on the heirs’ legal title for Claire’s benefit. Neale strongly supports relief where marriage was followed by possession, long acquiescence, and substantial improvements made with the promisor’s knowledge. Promissory-estoppel damages should be pleaded in the alternative, but they are a fallback, not the best fit.

3. The dispositive doctrine on the face of the transaction is the Statute of Frauds rule requiring a signed writing for a transfer of an interest in land; depending on State X, the promise may also implicate the separate Statute of Frauds category for agreements made upon consideration of marriage. The equitable doctrine most likely controlling the result is part performance, sometimes described in this setting as equitable estoppel against use of the statute as an instrument of fraud. Under Neale, equity may enforce a parol family settlement of land when the promisee has so changed position in reliance—especially by taking possession and making valuable, permanent improvements—that refusing enforcement would itself be fraudulent.

4. The facts show more than a mere revocable license, but they do not look like a conventional commercial bargain either. Anna’s statement was donative in tone (“give the lot”), yet it was expressly conditioned on Claire’s marriage and move onto the land, and Claire then expended her own money and labor in ways consistent with ownership, not bare occupancy. A court should characterize Claire’s marriage and especially her substantial expenditures as induced reliance and part performance of a family settlement; whether they count as strict consideration is less important than that they were the very acts Anna sought and that Anna knowingly stood by while Claire materially altered position. Even if the promise began as a conditional gift, Neale permits equity to protect the donee once she has entered and improved the property in reliance.

5. The writing requirement is triggered because the promise concerns conveyance of land and, insofar as Anna said Ben should “hold title for her benefit,” possibly an oral trust concerning land. If State X retains a marriage-consideration clause, that is also implicated because the promise was made before marriage and conditioned on marriage. Still, the land-transfer aspect is the practical bar here, and Claire’s best answer is part performance: she married, moved onto the property, spent $6,500 of her own funds on a large addition and outbuildings, improved landscaping and utilities, and exercised incidents of ownership by collecting rents, all with Anna’s knowledge and encouragement. Those acts are the classic route for taking an oral land agreement out of the statute in equity.

6. Claire could prevail either under classical part performance or, in a modern formulation, promissory estoppel. Part performance is stronger. It is anchored directly to Neale and to land-specific equity: possession plus substantial permanent improvements plus long acquiescence are exactly the facts courts treat as making nonenforcement fraudulent. Promissory estoppel is useful as an alternative because the promise was definite enough and reliance was foreseeable and substantial, but courts are divided on whether estoppel can override the Statute of Frauds for land and, if so, whether the remedy is full enforcement or only reliance damages. So the cleaner route is specific equitable relief under Neale-style part performance, with promissory estoppel reserved as a backup.

7. The heirs’ defenses are not strong on this record. Anna’s payment for some improvements does not negate Claire’s reliance if Claire still made substantial expenditures of her own and materially changed position; at most it may justify an accounting or offset. Anna’s retention of some rental income likewise does not defeat equity by itself. It may be evidence that Anna retained some control, but Claire’s own collection and use of rents still points toward beneficial ownership, and mixed handling of rents over a long family arrangement is not unusual. The alleged “breach” by Claire in using some rents for personal expenses appears weakest. The stated condition was marriage and moving onto the property, not a covenant to reinvest all rents in the land. Unless the heirs can prove a definite oral term requiring strict rent use, that issue sounds in accounting, not forfeiture, and would not justify denying conveyance.

8. The heirs’ best doctrinal argument is that Claire’s acts are not unequivocally referable to an enforceable agreement to convey land, but are equally consistent with permissive family occupancy under an expected gift or inheritance. That argument gains force from the absence of any writing for fifteen years, Anna’s partial payment of improvements, and Anna’s retention of some rent. To make that defense succeed at trial, the heirs would need to prove that Anna never surrendered beneficial control, that Claire’s possession was merely tolerated as a daughter living on family land, that the improvements were substantially Anna-funded or ordinary occupancy improvements, and that there was no sufficiently definite promise of present beneficial ownership or trust.

9. The principal uncertainties are whether State X recognizes part performance for an oral trust in land, whether a separate “consideration of marriage” Statute of Frauds provision exists and can be avoided by part performance, how demanding State X is about acts being “unequivocally referable,” and whether promissory estoppel is available to obtain enforcement rather than only damages. Substantiality also matters: some courts require very significant, permanent improvements and possession closely tied to the alleged agreement; others also look for receipt of rents or exclusive control. To maximize success, Claire’s counsel should first plead in the alternative for specific performance, constructive trust, and promissory-estoppel damages, expressly alleging part performance and any accounting offsets. Second, counsel should develop concrete proof that the promise was definite and ownership-like: testimony from Ben and other witnesses about Anna’s direction that title be held for Claire’s benefit, contractor records, receipts, photographs, tax and utility records, and tenant evidence showing Claire acted as owner. Third, counsel should neutralize the defenses affirmatively by tracing which improvements Claire funded, conceding any proper credits for Anna’s contributions or rents retained, and framing the alleged rental misuse as at most an accounting issue rather than a condition causing forfeiture. On a Neale-style record, equity should order conveyance.

# BENCH MEMO: Claire v. Heirs of Anna — Oral Promise to Convey Land

## 1. Jurisdiction Assumption

This analysis assumes State X follows traditional U.S. common-law equitable principles as articulated in *Neale v. Neales*, 76 U.S. (9 Wall.) 1 (1869), which held that an oral agreement to convey land, otherwise barred by the Statute of Frauds, may be specifically enforced in equity where the promisee has taken possession, made valuable and permanent improvements, and changed position in reliance on the promise such that refusing enforcement would itself constitute a fraud. I assume State X has not enacted a statutory override eliminating the part-performance exception. This choice matters because jurisdictions vary on whether possession alone suffices or whether possession plus improvements plus payment are all required; under *Neale*, the Supreme Court demanded a combination of acts — particularly possession coupled with substantial, permanent improvements — rendering the claimant's position irreversible and the oral agreement unmistakable.

## 2. Bottom-Line Outcome

Claire's claim should succeed. The recommended remedy is imposition of a constructive trust on the property in Claire's favor, with an order directing the heirs to execute a deed conveying fee simple title. Alternatively, specific performance of the oral promise is warranted. The facts satisfy every element *Neale* requires. Damages under promissory estoppel should be preserved as an alternative but are inferior because they cannot make Claire whole — the value of the land and fifteen years of reliance-based life choices exceed any monetary proxy.

## 3. Controlling Doctrine

The Statute of Frauds, requiring a signed writing for conveyances of interests in land, is facially dispositive and would bar enforcement of Anna's oral promise standing alone. However, the equitable doctrine of part performance, as crystallized in *Neale*, controls the result. *Neale* established that where the promisee, in reliance on the oral agreement, takes exclusive possession, makes valuable and lasting improvements, and so alters her position that restoration to the status quo ante is impossible, equity will enforce the oral promise to prevent the statute from becoming an instrument of fraud rather than a shield against it. Claire's fifteen years of possession, $6,500 in improvements (substantial for the era-adjusted context), construction of outbuildings, and management of the property satisfy this standard.

## 4. Formation/Consideration

Claire's acts constitute valid consideration, not mere conditions of a gratuitous gift. Anna's promise was explicitly bargained for: Claire was to marry, move onto the property, and occupy it. Claire's marriage, relocation, and expenditure of $6,500 in personal savings on permanent improvements constitute legal detriment and bargained-for exchange. Even if characterized as a conditional gift, Claire's substantial improvements transform the transaction into one supported by reliance-based consideration. A court should characterize Claire's marriage and move as inducement and her expenditures and improvements as both consideration and detrimental reliance, reinforcing enforceability under both contract and equitable principles.

## 5. Statute of Frauds Gates

The writing requirement triggered is the land-conveyance provision of the Statute of Frauds (typically Section 4 or its state equivalent), requiring a memorandum signed by the party to be charged for any transfer of an interest in real property. No signed writing exists. The dispositive aspect is the complete absence of any written instrument from Anna. Part performance removes the bar because Claire can demonstrate the *Neale* triad: (i) exclusive possession taken under the oral agreement, (ii) substantial, permanent, and valuable improvements (addition, outbuildings, landscaping, utilities), and (iii) acts referable exclusively to the oral agreement rather than explicable by any other relationship such as tenancy.

## 6. Exceptions / Promissory Estoppel

Under *Neale*'s classical part-performance doctrine, Claire's claim is strong: possession for fifteen years, $6,500 in improvements, construction, and property management are textbook acts of part performance. Under modern promissory estoppel (Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 139), Claire can also show a promise reasonably expected to induce action, actual and substantial reliance, and injustice avoidable only by enforcement. Classical part performance is the stronger theory here because it directly yields specific performance as a remedy, whereas promissory estoppel in some jurisdictions limits relief to reliance damages. The facts are overwhelmingly suited to the *Neale* framework given the permanence and magnitude of Claire's improvements.

## 7. Defenses / Mistake

Defense (a): Anna paid for some improvements. This does not defeat Claire's equity; it actually corroborates the oral agreement, since Anna's contributions are consistent with a grantor facilitating the promised transfer. Defense (b): Anna retained some rental income. Partial retention of rents during a long period is consistent with Anna holding legal title as informal trustee; it does not negate Claire's equitable ownership, especially since Claire also collected and used rents with Anna's knowledge. Defense (c): Claire used some rentals for personal expenses. This is the weakest defense. No condition in the oral promise restricted rental use. Even if such a condition existed, minor diversion of rental income over fifteen years is not a material breach justifying forfeiture of the entire equitable interest, particularly under the doctrine disfavoring forfeitures in equity. None of these defenses is likely to defeat Claire's claim.

## 8. Strongest Counterargument for the Heirs

The heirs' best argument is that Claire's acts of possession and improvement are equally referable to a family license or informal tenancy rather than an oral contract to convey, and therefore fail the referability requirement that some jurisdictions impose on part-performance claims. If the heirs can prove at trial that Claire paid no rent precisely because she was a family member permitted to live there gratuitously, that Anna retained dominion and control (collecting rents, supervising work, paying for improvements), and that the improvements were understood as benefiting Anna's property rather than Claire's own, the court could conclude that Claire's acts do not unequivocally point to an oral conveyance agreement, thus failing the *Neale* threshold.

## 9. Bounded Uncertainty and Practical Directions

Key doctrinal uncertainties include: whether State X requires strict "exclusive referability" of the acts to the oral contract or merely clear and convincing evidence of the agreement; whether $6,500 in improvements qualifies as "substantial" given the property's total value; and whether Claire's failure to exclude Anna from rent collection undermines the exclusivity of possession. Additionally, some jurisdictions require all three elements (possession, improvements, payment of consideration) while others treat any two as sufficient.

Three concrete steps for Claire's counsel: First, secure testimony from disinterested witnesses (the tenant, neighbors, contractors) establishing that Claire's occupancy and improvements were understood in the community as ownership conduct pursuant to Anna's promise, thereby defeating the alternative-referability argument. Second, obtain appraisals and receipts documenting the current value of Claire's improvements relative to the property's value, establishing that denial of relief would result in unjust enrichment that cannot be remedied by money damages alone. Third, plead both constructive trust and specific performance as primary equitable claims with promissory estoppel damages as a fallback, ensuring that even if the court applies a narrow part-performance standard, Claire retains a viable path to substantial recovery.

# BENCH MEMO: Claire v. Heirs of Anna — Oral Promise to Convey Land

## 1. Jurisdiction Assumption

I assume State X follows traditional U.S. common-law equitable doctrine as articulated in *Neale v. Neales*, 76 U.S. (9 Wall.) 1 (1869), which holds that an oral agreement concerning land, otherwise barred by the Statute of Frauds, may be specifically enforced in equity where there has been sufficient part performance — specifically, possession combined with valuable and permanent improvements made in reliance on the oral promise. I further assume State X has not enacted a statutory abrogation of the part-performance doctrine and recognizes the Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 129 and § 90 as persuasive supplementary authority. This choice matters because *Neale* sets a relatively claimant-friendly threshold: possession plus substantial improvements plus payment of consideration suffice to take the case out of the Statute, whereas some jurisdictions require all three elements while others accept possession and payment of purchase money alone.

## 2. Bottom-Line Outcome

Claire's claim should succeed. The recommended remedy is the imposition of a constructive trust on the property in Claire's favor, with an order directing the heirs to execute and deliver a deed. Specific performance of the oral promise is the most appropriate equitable relief because monetary damages cannot adequately compensate for the loss of a unique parcel of real property improved over fifteen years. If the court is reluctant to order outright conveyance, a constructive trust achieves the same functional result and is the standard equitable device recognized in *Neale* for precisely this fact pattern.

## 3. Controlling Doctrine

The Statute of Frauds, requiring a signed writing for agreements transferring interests in land, is dispositive on its face and would bar Claire's claim if applied mechanically. However, the equitable doctrine of part performance, as articulated in *Neale*, controls the result. The Supreme Court in *Neale* held that where a party, in reliance on an oral agreement to convey land, takes possession, makes valuable and lasting improvements, and alters her position so substantially that restoration to the status quo ante is impossible, equity will enforce the oral agreement notwithstanding the absence of a writing. The rationale is that the Statute of Frauds was enacted to prevent fraud, and refusing enforcement where such reliance has occurred would itself perpetrate a fraud. Claire's fifteen years of possession, $6,500 in improvements (a very substantial sum at the time, and analogous to the improvements in *Neale*), and life-altering decision to marry and relocate satisfy every element of this standard.

## 4. Formation/Consideration

Claire's acts constitute both consideration and induced reliance, not a mere gratuitous condition. Anna's promise was bargained-for: Claire married, moved onto the property, and expended her own savings — acts she was under no prior obligation to perform and which directly benefited Anna by improving the property's value and maintaining it. A court should characterize the marriage-plus-relocation as a condition precedent that was fully performed and the expenditures as classic consideration, transforming what might superficially appear to be a conditional gift into an enforceable bilateral arrangement. Even if characterized solely as a conditional gift, Claire's full performance of the conditions coupled with substantial detrimental reliance prevents revocation under both part-performance and promissory estoppel theories.

## 5. Statute of Frauds Gates

The writing requirement triggered is the land-conveyance provision (and arguably the trust provision, since Anna directed Ben to act as trustee). The dispositive aspect is the absence of any signed memorandum by Anna. Part performance removes the bar because Claire can demonstrate the classic triad recognized in *Neale*: (i) actual, exclusive possession under the oral agreement; (ii) substantial, valuable, and permanent improvements ($6,500 in construction, landscaping, and utilities); and (iii) payment of her own funds as consideration. These acts are "unequivocally referable" to the oral agreement and inexplicable absent it, satisfying even the stricter formulation some jurisdictions require.

## 6. Exceptions / Promissory Estoppel

Under classical *Neale*-style part performance, Claire's claim is strong: possession, improvements, and expenditure are the paradigmatic facts the doctrine was designed to address. Under modern promissory estoppel (Restatement § 90), Claire can independently show a clear promise, reasonable and foreseeable reliance, and injustice avoidable only by enforcement. The part-performance theory is stronger here because it leads directly to specific performance, whereas promissory estoppel in many jurisdictions limits relief to reliance damages rather than expectation enforcement. Claire's counsel should plead both but lead with part performance.

## 7. Defenses / Mistake

Defense (a), that Anna paid for some improvements, is weak; Anna's partial contribution is consistent with her role as the promisor-benefactor and does not negate Claire's independent $6,500 expenditure, which alone constitutes substantial improvements. Defense (b), that Anna retained some rental income, similarly fails; Anna's occasional receipt of rents is consistent with her retaining nominal legal title during her lifetime and does not disprove the beneficial arrangement. Defense (c), that Claire breached a condition by using some rentals for personal expenses, is the weakest. No express condition restricted Claire's use of rental income; the oral promise was to "give the lot and all buildings." Even if such a condition existed, minor diversion of rental income over fifteen years of faithful occupation and improvement would not constitute a material breach justifying forfeiture of the entire equitable interest. Equity abhors forfeiture, and a court would at most order an accounting rather than deny the claim entirely. None of these defenses is likely to defeat Claire's equity claim.

## 8. Strongest Counterargument for the Heirs

The heirs' single best argument is that Claire's possession was merely that of a licensee or family member permitted to reside on the property, not possession "referable to" an enforceable oral contract. If the heirs can prove at trial that Claire's occupancy and improvements are equally consistent with a revocable family license — pointing to Anna's retained rental income, Anna's supervision and partial payment of improvements, and the absence of any witness to the specific oral promise — they can argue the acts fail the "unequivocal referability" test that some jurisdictions impose as a prerequisite to part performance. To succeed, the heirs must produce evidence that Anna treated the property as her own throughout (tax payments, insurance in her name, control over major decisions) and that no disinterested witness can corroborate the oral promise's specific terms.

## 9. Bounded Uncertainty and Practical Directions

Key doctrinal uncertainties include: whether State X requires strict "unequivocal referability" of the acts to the oral contract or merely clear and convincing evidence of the promise; whether possession by a family member can ever satisfy the possession element without additional corroboration; and whether the oral trust direction to Ben triggers a separate Statute of Frauds for trusts requiring distinct part-performance analysis. Additionally, the quantum of "substantial" improvement varies by jurisdiction, and some courts discount improvements partially funded by the promisor.

Claire's counsel should take three concrete steps. First, secure testimony from disinterested witnesses — the tenant, contractors who built the addition, neighbors — who can corroborate both the oral promise and Claire's exclusive occupancy, establishing that her possession was referable to the agreement rather than a mere family arrangement. Second, compile a detailed accounting with documentary evidence (receipts, bank records, contractor invoices) demonstrating that Claire's $6,500 personal expenditure was independently substantial, separate from any contribution by Anna, and that the improvements permanently and materially enhanced the property's value. Third, plead both part performance and promissory estoppel as alternative theories, and request in the alternative a constructive trust, an equitable lien for the value of improvements, or restitution, thereby preserving all available remedies and ensuring relief even if the court applies a narrower version of part performance than *Neale* endorses.

# BENCH MEMO: Claire v. Heirs of Anna — Oral Promise to Convey Land

## 1. Jurisdiction Assumption

This analysis assumes State X follows traditional U.S. common-law equitable doctrine as articulated in *Neale v. Neales*, 76 U.S. (9 Wall.) 1 (1869), which recognized that part performance of an oral contract for the conveyance of land may take a case outside the Statute of Frauds when the acts of performance are unequivocally referable to the oral agreement and when denying enforcement would itself constitute a fraud. I assume State X has not enacted a statute abrogating equitable part-performance or limiting it to written memoranda. This choice matters because *Neale* represents the broadest federal articulation of part-performance doctrine, requiring possession plus valuable and permanent improvements referable to the contract, and some jurisdictions have narrowed or expanded these elements. If State X follows a stricter rule (e.g., requiring payment of purchase price as an additional element), the analysis at Point 9 would shift.

## 2. Bottom-Line Outcome

Claire's claim should succeed. The recommended remedy is specific performance directing the heirs to convey legal title to Claire, or in the alternative, imposition of a constructive trust on the property in Claire's favor. The combination of fifteen years of exclusive possession, marriage undertaken in reliance on the promise, $6,500 in personal funds expended on permanent improvements, and Anna's knowing acquiescence satisfies every element of the *Neale* part-performance doctrine. Damages under promissory estoppel should serve as the fallback remedy only if the court finds the evidence of the oral agreement's specific terms insufficiently clear to support specific performance.

## 3. Controlling Doctrine

The Statute of Frauds, specifically the land-conveyance provision requiring a signed writing for transfers of interests in real property, is dispositive on its face and would bar Claire's claim if applied mechanically. However, the equitable doctrine of part performance as stated in *Neale* controls the result. The Supreme Court in *Neale* held that where a party, in reliance on an oral promise to convey land, takes possession and makes valuable, lasting improvements with the knowledge and encouragement of the promisor, equity will enforce the promise because to permit the promisor (or her successors) to invoke the Statute would be to use the statute as an instrument of fraud rather than a shield against it. The legal standard requires: (a) an oral agreement sufficiently definite in its terms, (b) possession delivered and taken pursuant to that agreement, (c) valuable and permanent improvements made in reliance, and (d) circumstances making it impossible to restore the status quo ante through money damages alone.

## 4. Formation/Consideration

Claire's acts constitute valid consideration, not merely conditions of a gratuitous gift. Anna's promise was explicitly bargained for: Claire was to marry, relocate onto the property, and assume responsibility for it. Claire's marriage, her expenditure of $6,500 in personal savings on permanent improvements, her collection and management of rental income, and her fifteen years of occupancy and maintenance all constitute detrimental reliance and forbearance that goes well beyond a donative condition. A court should characterize Claire's marriage-and-move as the inducing consideration for a bilateral oral contract, and her subsequent improvements as both additional consideration and the acts of part performance that trigger equitable relief. Even if characterized as a conditional gift, the substantial and irreversible nature of Claire's reliance transforms the arrangement into an enforceable equitable obligation under *Neale*.

## 5. Statute of Frauds Gates

The writing requirement triggered is the land-conveyance provision of the Statute of Frauds. No signed writing from Anna exists, so the statute's bar is facially applicable. The dispositive aspect is the absence of any memorandum signed by the party to be charged (Anna). Part performance removes this bar because Claire's acts — taking exclusive possession, making $6,500 in permanent improvements, and occupying for fifteen years — are unequivocally referable to the oral promise and not explicable as the conduct of a mere licensee or tenant. Under *Neale*, these acts substitute for the writing by providing evidentiary certainty equivalent to a signed instrument.

## 6. Exceptions / Promissory Estoppel

Under classical *Neale*-style part performance, Claire's claim is strong: possession plus permanent, valuable improvements plus the promisor's knowledge and encouragement constitute the canonical fact pattern. Under modern promissory estoppel (Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 139), Claire also prevails because the promise induced reasonable and foreseeable reliance, and injustice can be avoided only by enforcement. The classical part-performance theory is stronger here for two reasons: first, it directly yields specific performance rather than the flexible and potentially limited remedy under § 139; second, the facts map almost perfectly onto *Neale*'s own facts, which involved family members, oral promises of land, possession, and improvements. Promissory estoppel serves as a valuable alternative but may limit recovery to reliance damages in some jurisdictions.

## 7. Defenses / Mistake

Defense (a), that Anna paid for some improvements, does not defeat Claire's claim. Anna's contributions are consistent with her role as the promisor facilitating the bargain and do not negate Claire's independent $6,500 expenditure or her reliance. At most, this affects the quantum of Claire's reliance but not its existence. Defense (b), that Anna retained some rental income, similarly fails. Anna held legal title and may have retained income as informal compensation or under a loose management arrangement; this does not contradict Claire's equitable ownership or the existence of the oral promise. Defense (c), that Claire breached a condition by using some rentals for personal expenses, is the weakest argument. No evidence suggests the oral promise conditioned conveyance on Claire's exclusive application of all rental income to property expenses. Even if such a condition existed, minor personal use of rental income over fifteen years would constitute an immaterial breach insufficient to forfeit Claire's entire equitable interest, particularly given the doctrine of substantial performance. None of these defenses is likely to defeat Claire's equity claim.

## 8. Strongest Counterargument for the Heirs

The heirs' best argument is that Anna's oral statement was a revocable, gratuitous promise — a mere expression of donative intent — rather than a binding contract, and that Claire's acts of improvement were those of a permissive licensee or family member voluntarily enhancing property she was allowed to occupy, not acts unequivocally referable to a contract. If the heirs can prove at trial that Anna never intended a binding obligation, that Claire understood she occupied the property at Anna's pleasure, and that the improvements were voluntary enhancements rather than bargained-for performance, then the acts of "part performance" lose their unequivocal referability to a contract and the Statute of Frauds bar remains intact. The heirs must produce testimony or documentary evidence (letters, statements to third parties, tax filings showing Anna claimed the property and improvements as her own) demonstrating that no mutual understanding of a binding conveyance existed.

## 9. Bounded Uncertainty and Practical Directions

Key doctrinal uncertainties include: whether State X requires all three elements of the *Neale* triad (possession, improvements, and payment of consideration) or accepts possession plus improvements alone; whether $6,500 in 1860s-equivalent dollars constitutes "substantial" improvement relative to the property's value; whether Claire's collection of rents strengthens her claim (showing dominion) or weakens it (suggesting she was merely a managing agent); and whether the oral direction to Ben as trustee creates a separate oral-trust theory under the constructive-trust doctrine independent of part performance.

Three concrete steps for Claire's counsel: First, secure and present testimony from neighbors, tenants, and any surviving witnesses who heard Anna describe the property as Claire's or who observed Claire exercising dominion as owner, thereby establishing the oral agreement's terms and Claire's possession as referable to the contract rather than mere license. Second, obtain appraisals and contractor testimony documenting the nature, permanence, and value of Claire's $6,500 improvements relative to the property's total value, to demonstrate that the improvements are irrecoverable through money damages and satisfy any jurisdiction's substantiality threshold. Third, plead both specific performance under part-performance doctrine and constructive trust as alternative equitable remedies, and include a fallback promissory-estoppel count for reliance damages, ensuring that the court has multiple doctrinal paths to relief regardless of which equitable theory it finds most persuasive under State X law.

# BENCH MEMO: CLAIRE v. HEIRS OF ANNA — ORAL PROMISE TO CONVEY LAND

**1. Jurisdiction Assumption.**

I assume State X follows traditional U.S. common-law equitable doctrine as articulated in *Neale v. Neales*, 76 U.S. (9 Wall.) 1 (1869), which permits a court of equity to enforce an oral agreement for the conveyance of land notwithstanding the Statute of Frauds where the claimant demonstrates part performance consisting of possession, payment of consideration, and valuable improvements made in reliance on the promise. This choice matters because *Neale* establishes the tripartite test (possession + payment/consideration + improvements) that distinguishes enforceable reliance from a mere unperformed oral gift. Some modern jurisdictions have codified narrower or broader versions of this exception; I note departures only where material.

**2. Bottom-Line Outcome.**

Claire's claim should succeed. The recommended remedy is specific performance directing the heirs to convey the lot to Claire, or alternatively imposition of a constructive trust on the property in Claire's favor. The facts satisfy every element of the *Neale* part-performance doctrine, and damages would be inadequate given the unique nature of real property and fifteen years of possession and improvement.

**3. Controlling Doctrine.**

The Statute of Frauds, requiring a writing signed by the party to be charged for any agreement transferring an interest in land, is dispositive on its face and would bar Claire's claim at law. However, the equitable doctrine of part performance as stated in *Neale* controls the result. The Supreme Court in *Neale* held that where a party, in reliance on an oral promise to convey land, takes possession, makes substantial and valuable improvements, and provides consideration, equity will decree specific performance because the acts of part performance are "unequivocally referable" to the oral agreement and it would be a fraud upon the promisee to permit the promisor (or successors) to invoke the statute. The legal standard requires that the acts of reliance be of such a character that they can be reasonably explained only by the existence of the alleged agreement.

**4. Formation/Consideration.**

Claire's acts constitute valid consideration, not a mere conditional gift. Anna's promise was explicitly bargained for: Claire was to marry and move onto the property. Claire performed both conditions and then expended $6,500 of her own savings on improvements—a substantial sum representing detrimental reliance that goes well beyond passive acceptance of a gift. The marriage itself, while sometimes treated as insufficient consideration standing alone, is here coupled with relocation, expenditure of personal funds, and fifteen years of active stewardship. A court should characterize Claire's acts as both consideration for a bilateral agreement and as the satisfaction of conditions precedent that ripened Anna's promise into an enforceable obligation. The combination of bargained-for acts and substantial detriment removes any characterization as a revocable license.

**5. Statute of Frauds Gates.**

Two SoF categories are triggered: the land-transfer provision and potentially the one-year provision (the arrangement contemplated indefinite duration). The land-transfer provision is dispositive. Part performance removes the bar because Claire satisfies all three *Neale* elements: (i) she took and maintained exclusive possession for fifteen years; (ii) she paid valuable consideration through her own funds for construction; and (iii) she made permanent, substantial improvements (addition, outbuildings, landscaping, utilities) that are unequivocally referable to an ownership interest rather than a mere tenancy.

**6. Exceptions / Promissory Estoppel.**

Under classical *Neale*-style part performance, Claire's claim is strong because the tripartite test is fully satisfied. Under modern promissory estoppel (Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 139), Claire also has a viable claim: Anna made a clear promise, Claire reasonably relied to her substantial detriment, and injustice can be avoided only by enforcement. The part-performance theory is stronger here because it directly yields specific performance as the standard remedy, whereas promissory estoppel under § 139 leaves remedy to judicial discretion and some courts limit it to reliance damages. Claire's counsel should plead both theories but lead with part performance.

**7. Defenses / Mistake.**

Defense (a): That Anna paid for some improvements actually reinforces Claire's claim—it shows Anna acknowledged the arrangement and invested in property she regarded as Claire's. Defense (b): Anna's retention of some rental income is consistent with Anna holding legal title as trustee during her lifetime and does not negate the oral agreement; at most it creates an accounting issue. Defense (c): Claire's use of some rental income for personal expenses is not a material breach of any identifiable condition. The oral promise contained no restriction on use of rentals, and even if it did, the doctrine of substantial performance and the disproportionality between the alleged breach and forfeiture of fifteen years of improvements would prevent this defense from defeating equity. None of these defenses is likely to prevail.

**8. Strongest Counterargument for the Heirs.**

The heirs' best argument is that Claire's possession and improvements are equally consistent with a revocable family license—a mother permitting a daughter to live on family land—and therefore the acts are not "unequivocally referable" to an oral contract to convey. To succeed, the heirs must prove at trial that Anna treated the property as her own throughout (e.g., she paid taxes, controlled major decisions, never told third parties Claire owned it), that Claire's improvements were modest relative to the property's value, and that the family arrangement lacked the specificity of a bargained-for exchange. This is a fact-intensive inquiry, but the $6,500 expenditure, the construction of multiple structures, and fifteen years of exclusive possession make this argument difficult to sustain.

**9. Bounded Uncertainty and Practical Directions.**

Key uncertainties include: whether State X requires all three *Neale* elements or treats possession plus improvements as sufficient; how "substantial" improvements must be relative to the property's value; whether the oral direction to Ben as trustee creates a separate oral-trust theory (some states have distinct rules for oral trusts of land); and whether State X recognizes § 139 promissory estoppel for land transfers at all. Three concrete steps for Claire's counsel: First, obtain and present documentary evidence of Claire's $6,500 expenditure (bank records, contractor receipts, permits) and secure testimony from the tenant confirming Claire acted as landlord, to establish the improvements are unequivocally referable to ownership. Second, plead in the alternative for a constructive trust based on unjust enrichment and fraud, ensuring that even if the court rejects part performance it may impose an equitable remedy to prevent the heirs from being unjustly enriched by Claire's improvements. Third, depose Ben regarding Anna's oral instructions to him as trustee, because his testimony as a party-opponent admission may establish both the existence and terms of the oral agreement, strengthening the referability prong and potentially supporting an independent oral-trust claim.

# BENCH MEMO: Claire v. Heirs of Anna — Oral Promise to Convey Land

## 1. Jurisdiction Assumption

This analysis assumes State X follows traditional U.S. common-law equitable doctrine as articulated in *Neale v. Neales*, 76 U.S. (9 Wall.) 1 (1869), which recognized that part performance of an oral contract for the conveyance of land can take a case out of the Statute of Frauds when the acts of performance are referable exclusively to the alleged oral agreement and would otherwise result in fraud upon the performing party. I assume State X has not enacted a statutory abolition of the part-performance exception and follows the majority rule permitting equity to enforce oral land contracts upon a showing of possession, payment, and valuable improvements. This choice matters because a minority of jurisdictions (following a stricter English rule) require all three elements and treat possession alone as insufficient, while some modern jurisdictions additionally recognize promissory estoppel as an independent basis to overcome the Statute of Frauds under Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 139.

## 2. Bottom-Line Outcome

Claire's claim should succeed. The recommended remedy is imposition of a constructive trust on the property in Claire's favor, with an order directing the heirs to convey legal title to her. Alternatively, specific performance of the oral agreement is warranted. A constructive trust is the cleanest vehicle because it avoids the conceptual difficulty of "enforcing" an oral contract and instead prevents the unjust enrichment that would result if the heirs retained property improved at Claire's substantial expense pursuant to an oral promise. If for any reason equitable title transfer is denied, damages under promissory estoppel should be awarded as a floor, measured at minimum by Claire's $6,500 expenditure plus the value of all improvements, adjusted for fifteen years of occupancy.

## 3. Controlling Doctrine

The Statute of Frauds facially bars enforcement of Anna's oral promise because it concerns a transfer of an interest in land. However, *Neale* holds that where a party, in reliance on an oral agreement, takes possession, makes valuable and lasting improvements, and changes her position such that restoration to the status quo ante is impossible, equity will enforce the agreement to prevent the Statute from becoming an instrument of fraud rather than a shield against it. The part-performance doctrine as applied in *Neale* is the controlling equitable principle. The Court in *Neale* emphasized that the combination of possession, improvements, and payment of consideration renders the acts "unequivocally referable" to the oral contract, thereby satisfying equity's demand for evidentiary corroboration substituting for a writing.

## 4. Formation/Consideration

Claire's acts constitute valid consideration, not merely conditions of a gratuitous gift. Anna's promise was expressly conditioned on Claire's marriage and relocation—acts that imposed real legal detriment on Claire (changing her domicile, committing her savings). The $6,500 expenditure and years of labor improving the property constitute additional bargained-for exchange. A court should characterize Claire's marriage-and-move as an inducement that ripened into binding consideration once performed, and the subsequent improvements as further performance under the agreement. Even if characterized as a conditional gift, the substantial reliance transforms the arrangement into an enforceable obligation under both part-performance and estoppel doctrines.

## 5. Statute of Frauds Gates

The writing requirement triggered is the land-conveyance provision (typically codified as requiring a signed memorandum for any contract for the sale or transfer of an interest in real property). The dispositive aspect is the complete absence of any signed writing by Anna. Part performance removes this bar because Claire satisfies the classic triad recognized in *Neale*: (1) she took exclusive possession with Anna's consent; (2) she made substantial, lasting, and valuable improvements ($6,500 in construction plus landscaping and utility work); and (3) she performed the conditions of the oral agreement (marriage, relocation). These acts are inexplicable except by reference to the oral promise, satisfying the "unequivocal referability" test.

## 6. Exceptions / Promissory Estoppel

Under classical *Neale*-style part performance, Claire's claim is strong because she has all three traditional elements: possession, improvements, and performance of conditions. Under modern promissory estoppel (Restatement § 139), Claire must show a promise reasonably expected to induce action, actual reliance, and injustice avoidable only by enforcement. Both routes are viable, but part performance is stronger here because the fifteen-year possession and $6,500-plus improvements provide concrete, corroborated evidence that satisfies even skeptical courts. Promissory estoppel serves as an independent fallback and is particularly useful if a court finds the oral arrangement was a conditional gift rather than a contract, since estoppel does not require traditional consideration.

## 7. Defenses / Mistake

Defense (a)—Anna paid for some improvements—does not defeat Claire's claim; Anna's contributions are consistent with her role as the promisor facilitating the arrangement and, if anything, corroborate the oral agreement's existence. Defense (b)—Anna retained some rental income—similarly corroborates Anna's continuing involvement but does not negate Claire's reliance; at most it creates an accounting issue. Defense (c)—Claire used some rentals for personal expenses—fails because the oral promise contained no express restriction on rental use; even if it did, diversion of modest rental income over fifteen years is a trivial breach that does not constitute a material failure of consideration sufficient to forfeit Claire's equity in the property. Courts applying equitable principles would not impose forfeiture of $6,500 in improvements and fifteen years of reliance over incidental personal use of rental income.

## 8. Strongest Counterargument for the Heirs

The heirs' best argument is that Anna's oral statement was a revocable, gratuitous promise to make a gift—not a contract—and that part performance cannot rescue a mere donative promise because there is no underlying enforceable agreement to which performance can attach. Under this theory, Claire's possession was a license, and her improvements were voluntary. To succeed, the heirs must prove at trial that Anna never intended a binding commitment, that Claire's marriage and move were not bargained-for conditions but independent life choices, and that Anna retained sufficient dominion (paying for improvements, collecting rents, supervising work) to demonstrate she never relinquished equitable ownership. If the heirs can establish this characterization, the part-performance exception arguably has no contract to enforce.

## 9. Bounded Uncertainty and Practical Directions

Key uncertainties include: whether State X requires all three elements of the part-performance triad or treats substantial improvements alone as sufficient; whether the jurisdiction recognizes part performance for oral gifts (as opposed to oral sales); and how "substantial" improvements must be relative to the property's value. Additionally, some jurisdictions require that possession be "exclusive," which could be complicated by Anna's supervisory involvement and rental-income retention.

Claire's counsel should take three concrete steps. First, secure and present detailed evidence of every dollar Claire spent on improvements, including receipts, contractor testimony, and before-and-after appraisals, to establish that the improvements are both substantial and permanently affixed, making monetary restitution inadequate. Second, obtain testimony from witnesses (neighbors, the tenant, family members) who heard Anna acknowledge the arrangement or observed Claire acting as owner, to corroborate the oral promise and demonstrate unequivocal referability. Third, plead both part performance and promissory estoppel as alternative theories, and include a constructive-trust count alongside specific performance, ensuring that the court has maximum doctrinal flexibility to grant relief regardless of whether it characterizes the arrangement as a contract or a relied-upon promise.

# BENCH MEMO: Claire v. Heirs of Anna — Oral Promise to Convey Land

## 1. Jurisdiction Assumption

I assume State X follows traditional U.S. common-law equitable doctrine as articulated in *Neale v. Neales*, 76 U.S. (9 Wall.) 1 (1869), which recognized that part performance of an oral agreement respecting land may take a case out of the Statute of Frauds when the acts of performance are exclusively referable to the alleged contract and would make it a fraud to allow the promisor (or her successors) to invoke the statute. This choice matters because *Neale* sets a relatively claimant-friendly standard: possession plus valuable and permanent improvements made in reliance on the oral agreement, with the knowledge and acquiescence of the promisor, are sufficient to override the writing requirement. Some modern jurisdictions impose additional gates (e.g., requiring payment of consideration or requiring that the acts be "unequivocally referable" to the contract rather than explainable on any other ground). I note departures only where they would change the outcome.

## 2. Bottom-Line Outcome

Claire's claim should succeed. The recommended remedy is the imposition of a constructive trust on the property in Claire's favor, with an order directing the heirs to convey legal title to her. Alternatively, specific performance of the oral promise is warranted. Between the two, a constructive trust is the cleaner vehicle because it avoids characterizing the arrangement as an executory contract and instead addresses the unconscionability of the heirs' unjust retention of property improved at Claire's expense and in reliance on Anna's promise.

## 3. Controlling Doctrine

The Statute of Frauds—specifically the provision requiring a signed writing for conveyances of interests in land—is dispositive on its face and would bar enforcement of the oral promise standing alone. However, the equitable doctrine of part performance, as applied in *Neale*, controls the result. In *Neale*, the Supreme Court held that where a party, in reliance on an oral promise to convey land, takes possession, makes valuable and lasting improvements, and alters her position so substantially that restoration to the status quo ante is impracticable, equity will enforce the promise notwithstanding the absence of a writing. The rationale is that the Statute of Frauds, enacted to prevent fraud, must not itself become an instrument of fraud. Claire's fifteen years of possession, $6,500 in improvements (a significant sum, particularly at the time), construction of outbuildings, and management of the property satisfy every element *Neale* requires.

## 4. Formation/Consideration

Claire's acts constitute both consideration and reliance. Her marriage (at Anna's inducement), her relocation onto the property, and her expenditure of $6,500 in personal savings on permanent improvements are classic bargained-for detriments. This is not a mere conditional gift or revocable license. Anna's promise was bilateral: she promised to convey if Claire married and moved onto the property; Claire performed both conditions and then went further by substantially improving the land. A court should characterize Claire's marriage-and-move as conditions that ripened into consideration once performed, and the improvements as additional consideration and reliance that independently support enforcement.

## 5. Statute of Frauds Gates

Two SoF categories are potentially triggered: the land-conveyance provision and, if the arrangement is characterized as a trust, the requirement in many jurisdictions that express trusts of land be evidenced by a writing. The land-conveyance provision is the primary bar. Part performance removes it because Claire can show the classic triad recognized in *Neale* and its progeny: (i) actual, exclusive, and continuous possession for fifteen years; (ii) valuable, permanent improvements ($6,500 plus landscaping and utilities); and (iii) acts referable solely to the alleged oral agreement, not explicable as a mere tenancy or license. The combination makes it inequitable to permit the heirs to shelter behind the statute.

## 6. Exceptions / Promissory Estoppel

Under classical *Neale*-style part performance, Claire's claim is strong because her possession and improvements are precisely the kind of conduct the doctrine was designed to protect. Under modern promissory estoppel (Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 139), Claire can also prevail: Anna's promise was clear, reliance was foreseeable and reasonable, and injustice can be avoided only by enforcement. Part performance is the stronger theory here because it directly yields specific performance or a constructive trust, whereas promissory estoppel in some jurisdictions limits relief to reliance damages. Claire should plead both but lead with part performance.

## 7. Defenses

Defense (a)—Anna paid for some improvements—does not defeat Claire's claim; it actually reinforces that Anna treated the arrangement as real and invested in property she considered Claire's beneficial interest. Defense (b)—Anna retained some rental income—is similarly unavailing; joint management and occasional retention of rents by a legal titleholder is consistent with the trustee-like role Anna occupied and does not negate Claire's equitable ownership. Defense (c)—Claire used some rentals for personal expenses—is the weakest. The oral promise contained no express restriction on rental use, and even if it did, diversion of modest rental income over fifteen years is a trivial breach that does not work a forfeiture of Claire's substantial equity.
