**5. Statute of Frauds Gates**

The writing requirement triggered is the land-conveyance provision of the Statute of Frauds (typically codified as requiring a memorandum signed by the party to be charged for any contract for the sale or transfer of an interest in land). No trust instrument signed by Anna exists either, so the Statute of Frauds for express trusts in land is also implicated. Part performance removes the bar through the confluence of three acts identified in *Neale*: (i) taking and maintaining possession, (ii) making valuable and permanent improvements, and (iii) paying consideration. Claire satisfies all three. The improvements are not merely referable to a tenancy — their scale and nature (a large addition, outbuildings, utility upgrades) are explicable only by reference to an ownership interest, which is the evidentiary function the Statute of Frauds is designed to serve.

**6. Exceptions / Promissory Estoppel**

Under classical *Neale*-style part performance, Claire's claim is strong: possession plus permanent improvements plus consideration paid is the paradigmatic case. Under modern promissory estoppel (Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 139), Claire also prevails because Anna made a promise she reasonably expected to induce reliance, Claire relied to her substantial detriment, and injustice can be avoided only by enforcement. The part-performance theory is stronger here because it yields specific performance as of right, whereas promissory estoppel under § 139 grants courts discretion to limit the remedy to reliance damages. Given the uniqueness of real property and the impossibility of restoring Claire to the status quo, part performance is the superior vehicle.

**7. Defenses / Mistake**

Defense (a), that Anna paid for some improvements, does not defeat Claire's claim; it is consistent with Anna's role as the legal titleholder facilitating the promised conveyance and may even corroborate the oral agreement. Defense (b), that Anna retained some rental income, similarly fails because partial retention of rents by a titleholder-promisor during the interim period does not negate the promise; it reflects the practical reality that legal title had not yet passed. Defense (c), that Claire used some rental income for personal expenses, is the weakest argument. The oral promise contained no express condition restricting Claire's use of rents, and even if it did, minor diversion of rental income over fifteen years is not a material breach sufficient to forfeit Claire's entire equitable interest, particularly given the doctrine of substantial performance and the disproportionality of forfeiture. None of these defenses is likely to defeat Claire's equity claim.

**8. Strongest Counterargument for the Heirs**

The heirs' best argument is that Claire's acts of possession and improvement are equally referable to a family license or tenancy rather than an oral contract to convey, and therefore fail the evidentiary threshold required for part performance. Under the "unequivocal referability" test applied in some jurisdictions, the acts of part performance must be explicable only by reference to the alleged oral contract. The heirs must prove at trial that Claire's occupancy was understood by both parties as a revocable family arrangement, that the improvements were gifts or self-interested enhancements by a tenant, and that no witnesses can corroborate the oral promise. If the heirs can show that Claire's conduct is equally consistent with a rent-free license, the part-performance exception collapses and the Statute of Frauds bars the claim.

**9. Bounded Uncertainty and Practical Directions**

Key uncertainties include: whether State X applies the strict "unequivocal referability" test or the more liberal "corroborative evidence" approach to part performance; whether $6,500 in 1850s-era dollars (or whenever the facts are set) constitutes "substantial" improvements; and whether Claire's collection of rents constitutes an independent indicium of an ownership interest or is neutral. Additionally, some jurisdictions require all three *Neale* elements while others treat any two as sufficient.

Claire's counsel should take three concrete steps. First, secure testimony from any surviving witnesses — neighbors, the tenant, family members — who heard Anna articulate the promise or acknowledge Claire's ownership interest, as corroboration of the oral agreement dramatically strengthens the part-performance claim. Second, obtain documentary evidence (receipts, bank records, contractor invoices) proving the quantum and permanence of Claire's $6,500 expenditure, and commission an appraiser to establish that the improvements are inconsistent with a mere tenancy or license. Third, plead both part performance and promissory estoppel as alternative theories, and additionally plead constructive trust and unjust enrichment as equitable fallbacks, so that even if the court rejects specific performance, Claire recovers at minimum the value of her improvements and reliance expenditures.

# BENCH MEMO: Claire v. Heirs of Anna — Oral Promise to Convey Land

## 1. Jurisdiction Assumption

This analysis assumes State X follows traditional U.S. common-law equitable doctrine as articulated in *Neale v. Neales*, 76 U.S. (9 Wall.) 1 (1869), which recognized that part performance of an oral contract for the conveyance of land may take the case out of the Statute of Frauds when the acts of performance are referable exclusively to the oral agreement and are of such a character that it would be a fraud to allow the promisor's successors to repudiate the promise. This choice matters because *Neale* establishes a relatively generous standard: possession, coupled with valuable and permanent improvements made in reliance on the oral promise, with the knowledge and encouragement of the promisor, is the paradigmatic case for equitable relief. Some jurisdictions require all three elements (possession, payment of consideration, and valuable improvements), while others accept two. I assume State X follows the tripartite *Neale* standard, which Claire satisfies.

## 2. Bottom-Line Outcome

Claire's claim should succeed. The recommended remedy is imposition of a constructive trust on the property in Claire's favor, with an order directing the heirs to convey legal title to her. Specific performance of the oral promise is functionally equivalent here, but framing relief as a constructive trust avoids the technical objection that the court is "enforcing" an oral contract and instead rests on equity's power to prevent unjust enrichment and fraud. In the alternative, if a court were reluctant to grant title, promissory estoppel damages measured by the full value of Claire's reliance expenditures ($6,500 plus the value of fifteen years of improvements, labor, and landscaping) would be appropriate, though this is the inferior remedy.

## 3. Controlling Doctrine

The Statute of Frauds, requiring a signed writing for transfers of interests in land, is dispositive on its face and would bar enforcement of Anna's oral promise standing alone. The equitable doctrine of part performance, as articulated in *Neale*, controls the result. In *Neale*, the Supreme Court held that where a party, in reliance on an oral agreement, takes possession of land, makes valuable and permanent improvements, and does so with the knowledge and acquiescence of the promisor, equity will enforce the agreement notwithstanding the absence of a writing. The rationale is that the Statute of Frauds, intended to prevent fraud, must not itself become an instrument of fraud. Claire's fifteen years of possession, $6,500 expenditure on a large addition and outbuildings, and continuous improvements with Anna's active knowledge and praise satisfy this standard.

## 4. Formation/Consideration

Claire's acts constitute valid consideration, not merely a condition of a gratuitous gift. Anna's promise was expressly conditioned on Claire's marriage and relocation — acts that imposed real detriment on Claire (changing her living situation, committing her resources). The subsequent $6,500 expenditure and years of labor constitute additional consideration flowing from Claire to Anna in exchange for the promise of title. A court should characterize Claire's marriage-and-relocation as bargained-for inducement and her improvements as executed consideration, distinguishing this from a mere revocable license or conditional gift. The mutuality of obligation is evidenced by Anna's direction to Ben to act as trustee, indicating Anna understood herself to be bound.

## 5. Statute of Frauds Gates

Two SoF categories are triggered: the land-conveyance provision and potentially the trust provision (oral direction to Ben as trustee). The land-conveyance writing requirement is the primary bar. Part performance removes this bar when three elements converge: (i) actual, exclusive, continuous possession by the promisee; (ii) payment of valuable consideration or purchase price; and (iii) making of permanent, valuable improvements. Claire satisfies all three. Her possession was open, continuous for fifteen years, and exclusive in character (she collected rents, managed the property). Her $6,500 outlay and labor constitute consideration. The improvements — addition, outbuildings, landscaping, utilities — are permanent and substantial. These acts are "unequivocally referable" to the oral agreement, as required under *Neale*, because they are inexplicable absent the promised conveyance.

## 6. Exceptions / Promissory Estoppel

Under classical *Neale*-style part performance, Claire's claim is strong because she satisfies every traditional element. Under modern promissory estoppel (Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 139), Claire must show a promise, reasonable and foreseeable reliance, and injustice avoidable only by enforcement. This too is satisfied. Part performance is the stronger theory because it yields specific performance or a constructive trust — title to the land — whereas promissory estoppel in many jurisdictions limits relief to reliance damages rather than expectation. Given that Claire seeks the land itself, part performance is the preferred and more historically grounded avenue.

## 7. Defenses / Mistake

Defense (a) — Anna paid for some improvements: This actually strengthens Claire's case, as it demonstrates Anna's acquiescence in and ratification of the oral arrangement. Anna's contributions are consistent with a donor/promisor facilitating the promised conveyance, not with retention of ownership rights adverse to Claire. Defense (b) — Anna retained some rental income: Partial retention of rents by Anna is not inconsistent with the oral promise; Anna held legal title and may have understood herself as trustee entitled to some management compensation, or the parties may have informally shared income. This does not negate Claire's exclusive possession for part-performance purposes. Defense (c) — Claire used some rentals for personal expenses: This is the weakest defense. The oral promise contained no express condition restricting Claire's use of rental income. Even if such a condition were implied, diversion of some rental income for personal use over fifteen years is a trivial deviation that does not constitute a material breach sufficient to forfeit Claire's equity in the property. Equity does not require perfection.

## 8. Strongest Counterargument for the Heirs

The heirs' best argument is that Anna's oral statement was a gratuitous, revocable expression of donative intent — not an enforceable contract — and that Claire's possession was merely a permissive family arrangement (a license), with her improvements constituting voluntary gifts to her mother's property. Under this theory, there was no mutual assent to a bargained-for exchange, and Claire's acts are referable to the familial relationship rather than exclusively to a contract. To succeed, the heirs must prove at trial that Anna never manifested intent to be contractually bound, that no bargain was struck, and that Claire's improvements were understood by both parties as voluntary contributions to family property. Evidence of Anna directing Ben as trustee and Anna's repeated praise of Claire's work cuts strongly against this characterization, but the heirs would need testimony or documents showing Anna treated the property as her own throughout (e.g., claiming it on tax returns, making unilateral decisions inconsistent with Claire's ownership).

## 9. Bounded Uncertainty and Practical Directions

Key uncertainties include: (i) whether State X requires improvements to exceed a specific dollar threshold to be "substantial," which is fact-intensive; (ii) whether State X requires exclusive possession or merely open possession, since Anna occasionally supervised work; (iii) whether the oral trust direction to Ben independently creates an enforceable trust under a separate exception; and (iv) whether State X has adopted § 139 promissory estoppel for land transactions as a separate SoF exception.

Three concrete steps for Claire's counsel: First, obtain and present detailed documentary evidence of Claire's expenditures — receipts, contractor invoices, bank records showing the $6,500 and all subsequent outlays — to establish the quantum of improvements beyond dispute. Second, secure testimony from the tenant, neighbors, and any witnesses to Anna's oral statements (including the direction to Ben) to establish both the terms of the promise and Anna's continuous acknowledgment of Claire's ownership interest, making the acts "unequivocally referable" to the contract. Third, plead in the alternative — specific performance, constructive trust, and promissory estoppel damages — to preserve all remedial avenues and ensure that even if the court rejects part performance on technical grounds, the reliance-based claim survives.

# BENCH MEMO: Claire v. Heirs of Anna — Oral Promise to Convey Land

## 1. Jurisdiction Assumption

I assume State X follows traditional U.S. common-law equitable doctrine as articulated in *Neale v. Neales*, 76 U.S. (9 Wall.) 1 (1869), which recognized that an oral agreement to convey land, otherwise barred by the Statute of Frauds, may be specifically enforced in equity where the promisee has partly performed by taking possession, making valuable and permanent improvements, and changing position in reliance on the promise. This choice matters because *Neale* established the federal baseline that part performance sufficient to take a case out of the Statute of Frauds requires acts that are unequivocally referable to the oral agreement and that would make it a fraud to allow the promisor (or her successors) to invoke the statute. Some jurisdictions require all three classic elements (possession, payment, improvements); others accept two. I assume State X requires the *Neale* triad but also recognizes modern promissory estoppel as an independent basis for relief under Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 139.

## 2. Bottom-Line Outcome

Claire's claim should succeed. The recommended remedy is imposition of a constructive trust on the land in Claire's favor, with an order directing the heirs to execute a deed conveying fee simple to Claire. Alternatively, specific performance of the oral promise is warranted. A constructive trust is the cleaner vehicle because it avoids the conceptual difficulty of specifically enforcing a promise made by a now-deceased promisor who cannot be ordered to act, and it directly addresses the unjust enrichment that would result if the heirs retained land improved by $6,500 of Claire's funds plus fifteen years of labor and occupancy.

## 3. Controlling Doctrine

The Statute of Frauds, requiring a signed writing for conveyances of interests in land, is dispositive on its face and would bar enforcement of Anna's purely oral promise. However, the equitable doctrine of part performance as articulated in *Neale* controls the result. The *Neale* Court held that where the promisee, in reliance on the oral agreement, has taken actual possession, made substantial and permanent improvements, and so changed her position that restoring the status quo ante is impossible without injustice, equity will decree performance because enforcing the statute would itself operate as an instrument of fraud. Claire satisfies every element of the *Neale* standard: she took exclusive possession upon marriage, expended $6,500 in permanent improvements (addition, outbuildings, landscaping, utilities), and occupied the property for fifteen years.

## 4. Formation/Consideration

Claire's acts constitute valid consideration, not merely conditions of a gratuitous gift. Anna's promise was bargained-for: Claire was to marry, move onto the property, and occupy it, and in exchange Anna would hold title for Claire's benefit. Claire's marriage, relocation, and expenditure of personal savings are classic forms of detrimental reliance that also function as consideration. A court should characterize Claire's marriage and move as induced performance constituting both consideration and the conditions triggering Anna's obligation. The $6,500 expenditure and fifteen years of occupancy further demonstrate that this was a bilateral arrangement, not a revocable license. Even if characterized as a conditional gift, the conditions were fully performed, making the promise irrevocable.

## 5. Statute of Frauds Gates

Two SoF categories are triggered: the land-conveyance provision and, potentially, the one-year provision (since the arrangement contemplated indefinite/permanent transfer). The land-conveyance requirement is dispositive. Part performance removes the bar because Claire's acts—exclusive possession, substantial permanent improvements, and payment of her own funds—are unequivocally referable to the oral agreement and not explicable by any other relationship. Under *Neale*, these acts make it unconscionable to permit the statute to shield the heirs.

## 6. Exceptions / Promissory Estoppel

Under classical *Neale*-style part performance, Claire's claim is strong: possession plus improvements plus expenditure satisfies the triad. Under modern promissory estoppel (Restatement § 139), Claire must show a promise reasonably expected to induce action, actual reliance, and injustice avoidable only by enforcement. Both routes reach the same result, but classical part performance is stronger here because the facts map precisely onto the *Neale* framework, the doctrine is well-established for land cases, and it yields specific performance or a constructive trust rather than the potentially limited "justice requires" remedy of § 139, which some courts restrict to reliance damages.

## 7. Defenses / Mistake

Defense (a)—Anna paid for some improvements: This actually reinforces Claire's claim, as it shows Anna's recognition of the arrangement and her participation in improving property she intended for Claire. It does not negate Claire's own $6,500 expenditure. Defense (b)—Anna retained some rental income: Occasional retention of rents by Anna is consistent with Anna holding legal title as trustee or owner-in-transition; it does not disprove the oral agreement and may reflect Anna's reserved life interest or informal arrangement. Defense (c)—Claire used some rentals for personal expenses: This is the weakest defense. The oral promise contained no express restriction on rental use, and even if it did, diversion of some rental income for personal expenses is a minor breach at most, not a material breach sufficient to forfeit fifteen years of reliance. Equity would not impose forfeiture for such a trivial deviation. None of these defenses is likely to defeat Claire's equity claim.

## 8. Strongest Counterargument for the Heirs

The heirs' best argument is that Anna's oral statement was a revocable, gratuitous expression of intent—not a binding promise—and that Claire's acts of improvement were those of a permissive licensee or family member living rent-free, not unequivocally referable to an enforceable contract. Under this theory, the improvements are explained by the familial relationship and the benefit of free housing, and possession was permissive rather than contractual. To succeed, the heirs must prove at trial that no definite promise with identifiable terms was made, that Claire's occupancy was understood by both parties as a license revocable at will, and that the improvements were voluntary gifts to Anna's property rather than reliance expenditures. If the heirs can show ambiguity in the terms of the alleged promise—particularly that Anna never specified an irrevocable transfer—they undermine the "unequivocal referability" requirement central to *Neale*.

## 9. Bounded Uncertainty and Practical Directions

Key uncertainties include: whether State X requires all three *Neale* elements or accepts a subset; whether $6,500 in 1860s-era dollars (or whenever the facts are set) constitutes "substantial" improvement relative to the land's value; whether Claire's collection of rents constitutes sufficient indicia of ownership-possession or merely agency; and whether a constructive trust may be imposed against heirs who are bona fide successors rather than the original promisor.

Three concrete steps for Claire's counsel: First, secure and present detailed evidence of the improvements—receipts, contractor testimony, before-and-after valuations, and testimony from the tenant confirming Claire collected rents as owner—to establish unequivocal referability to the oral promise. Second, obtain testimony from disinterested witnesses (neighbors, the tenant, family friends) who heard Anna acknowledge the arrangement or describe Claire as the property's intended owner, to corroborate the promise's existence and terms, thereby satisfying any Dead Man's Statute concerns that State X may impose. Third, plead both part performance and promissory estoppel as alternative theories, and request both specific performance and a constructive trust as alternative remedies, preserving maximum flexibility if the court finds one equitable theory inapplicable but accepts the other.

# BENCH MEMO: Claire v. Heirs of Anna — Oral Promise to Convey Land

## 1. Jurisdiction Assumption

I assume State X follows traditional U.S. common-law equitable doctrine as articulated in *Neale v. Neales*, 76 U.S. (9 Wall.) 1 (1869), which recognized that part performance of an oral agreement concerning land can remove the bar of the Statute of Frauds when the acts of performance are unequivocally referable to the alleged agreement and when denying enforcement would itself constitute a fraud. This choice matters because *Neale* established a federal equity baseline that many states adopted: the combination of possession, payment, and valuable improvements, taken together, can substitute for the missing writing. Some modern jurisdictions have expanded this through Restatement-based promissory estoppel (Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 139), while others retain a narrower classical formulation. The analysis proceeds under the *Neale* framework but flags where modern estoppel doctrine could supplement it.

## 2. Bottom-Line Outcome

Claire's claim should succeed. The recommended remedy is imposition of a constructive trust on the property in Claire's favor, with the court ordering the heirs to convey legal title. Specific performance of the oral promise is the functional equivalent and equally appropriate. The fifteen-year course of possession, $6,500 in personal expenditures for permanent improvements, and Anna's knowing acquiescence—indeed active encouragement—satisfy every element of the part-performance exception. If a court were reluctant to grant equitable title, damages under promissory estoppel would be the fallback, but given the nature of the improvements (buildings, outbuildings, utilities), money damages are inadequate, which itself supports equitable relief.

## 3. Controlling Doctrine

The Statute of Frauds for conveyances of interests in land is dispositive on its face: no signed writing from Anna exists. However, *Neale* held that where a party, in reliance on an oral agreement, has taken possession, made valuable and permanent improvements, and changed her position such that restoration to the status quo ante is impossible, equity will enforce the promise notwithstanding the absence of a writing. The legal standard requires acts "unequivocally referable" to the oral contract—not reasonably explicable on any other ground—coupled with a showing that non-enforcement would work a fraud on the performing party. Part performance, not promissory estoppel, is the doctrine most likely to control because Claire's acts satisfy the classical triad: possession, payment/improvements, and irreversible change of position.

## 4. Formation/Consideration

Claire's acts constitute valid consideration, not merely conditions of a gratuitous gift. Anna's promise was explicitly bargained for: Claire was to marry, move onto the property, and occupy it. Claire's marriage, relocation, expenditure of $6,500 in personal savings, and fifteen years of stewardship represent substantial detrimental reliance that a court should characterize as both consideration and induced performance. Even if characterized as a conditional gift, the conditions were fully performed, and equity treats fully performed conditions in oral land-transfer promises as enforceable under part performance. The marriage itself, as a legal act undertaken in reliance, has historically been treated as particularly weighty consideration, a point *Neale* implicitly recognized in its broader equitable framework.

## 5. Statute of Frauds Gates

The operative SoF provision is the land-conveyance clause (typically codified as requiring a writing signed by the party to be charged for any transfer of an interest in real property). The dispositive aspect is Anna's failure to execute any deed, memorandum, or signed instrument. Part performance removes this bar by demonstrating that Claire's possession was exclusive and continuous, her improvements were permanent and substantial ($6,500 plus labor, outbuildings, utilities, landscaping), and these acts are inexplicable absent the oral agreement. No tenant or licensee would invest $6,500 in permanent structures on another's land without an ownership interest. This unequivocal referability is the linchpin.

## 6. Exceptions / Promissory Estoppel

Under *Neale*-style classical part performance, Claire's claim is strong: she satisfies all three traditional elements (possession, improvements, irreparable change of position). Under modern promissory estoppel (Restatement § 139), Claire must show a promise, reasonable and foreseeable reliance, and injustice avoidable only by enforcement. Both routes reach the same result, but classical part performance is stronger here because it directly addresses land conveyances and does not require the court to adopt the more contested § 139 framework. Part performance also more naturally yields specific performance, whereas promissory estoppel in some jurisdictions limits relief to reliance damages. Claire's counsel should plead both but lead with part performance.

## 7. Defenses / Mistake

Defense (a)—Anna paid for some improvements—does not defeat Claire's claim. Anna's contributions are consistent with her role as the promisor facilitating the arrangement; they reinforce rather than undermine the existence of the agreement. Defense (b)—Anna retained some rental income—similarly reflects Anna's continuing legal title as trustee-equivalent during her lifetime and does not negate Claire's equitable interest. Defense (c)—Claire used some rentals for personal expenses—is the weakest. The oral promise contained no express restriction on rental use, and even if it did, minor deviations over fifteen years do not constitute a material breach sufficient to forfeit an equitable interest, particularly where the property was substantially improved. Equity abhors forfeiture, and none of these defenses should defeat Claire's claim.

## 8. Strongest Counterargument for the Heirs

The heirs' best argument is that Claire's occupancy and improvements are explicable as a gratuitous license from a mother to a daughter—a family accommodation—rather than acts unequivocally referable to an oral contract of conveyance. If the acts are equivocal, part performance fails under *Neale*. To prevail, the heirs must prove at trial that family members commonly make such improvements on parents' property without any expectation of ownership, that Anna's statements were aspirational rather than promissory, and that the scale of improvements ($6,500) was modest relative to the property's value, making a license explanation plausible. This is a genuine factual contest, though the fifteen-year duration, construction of multiple outbuildings, and Anna's explicit oral direction to Ben as trustee substantially weaken it.

## 9. Bounded Uncertainty and Practical Directions

Key uncertainties include: (i) whether State X requires all three elements of the classical triad or treats possession plus improvements as sufficient; (ii) the threshold for "substantial" improvements (some courts require improvements disproportionate to rental value); (iii) whether Ben's designated trusteeship creates an independent basis for a resulting or constructive trust apart from part performance; and (iv) whether State X recognizes § 139 promissory estoppel for land transfers.

Three concrete steps for Claire's counsel: First, secure testimony from neighbors, contractors, and any surviving witnesses (including Ben in his capacity as designated trustee) establishing Anna's oral promise and its specific terms, corroborating that Claire's occupancy was as owner, not licensee. Second, obtain appraisals and receipts documenting that the $6,500 in improvements (plus labor) substantially exceeded what any tenant or licensee would invest, establishing unequivocal referability. Third, plead in the alternative—specific performance of the oral contract, imposition of a constructive trust, and damages under promissory estoppel—to preserve all remedial avenues and ensure that even a court skeptical of one theory can grant relief under another.

Neales*, 76 U.S. (9 Wall.) 1 (1869), which recognized that an oral agreement to convey land, otherwise barred by the Statute of Frauds, may be specifically enforced in equity where the promisee has partly performed by taking possession, making valuable and permanent improvements, and changing position in reliance on the promise. This choice matters because *Neale* sets a relatively generous standard for part-performance — requiring possession plus substantial improvements referable to the oral agreement — rather than the narrower rule in some jurisdictions that demand all three of possession, payment of consideration, and improvements. I assume State X has not adopted a statutory override eliminating the part-performance exception, nor has it adopted the Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 139 promissory estoppel exception by statute, though its courts would recognize it as persuasive authority.

## 2. Bottom-Line Outcome

Claire's claim should succeed. The recommended remedy is specific performance compelling the heirs to convey title, or in the alternative, imposition of a constructive trust on the property in Claire's favor. The facts present a textbook application of the part-performance doctrine under *Neale*: fifteen years of exclusive possession, $6,500 in personal funds expended on permanent improvements, and continuous reliance all undertaken with Anna's knowledge and encouragement. Damages under promissory estoppel would be an adequate fallback but would undercompensate Claire given the property's appreciated value and the irreplaceable nature of the improvements.

## 3. Controlling Doctrine

The Statute of Frauds — specifically the land-conveyance provision requiring a signed writing for transfers of interests in real property — is dispositive on its face and would bar enforcement of Anna's oral promise. However, the equitable doctrine of part performance as articulated in *Neale* most likely controls the result. The *Neale* Court held that where a party, in reliance on an oral promise to convey land, enters into possession and makes valuable, permanent improvements that cannot be adequately compensated by money damages, it would be a fraud upon that party to permit the promisor (or her successors) to invoke the Statute of Frauds as a shield. The standard requires acts of performance "unequivocally referable" to the oral contract, not explicable by any other relationship.

## 4. Formation/Consideration

Claire's acts constitute valid consideration, not merely conditions of a gratuitous gift. Anna's promise was bargained-for: Claire married, relocated onto the property, and expended $6,500 in improvements — acts that conferred a direct benefit on Anna's estate and constituted a detriment to Claire. The marriage itself, while not independently sufficient consideration in modern law, combined with the relocation and expenditure, forms a classic bilateral exchange. A court should characterize Claire's marriage-and-move as the inducing consideration for the promise and the subsequent improvements as further performance of the bargain, not merely conditions precedent to a revocable license. The fifteen-year course of dealing, with Anna's active encouragement, confirms a contractual rather than gratuitous relationship.

## 5. Statute of Frauds Gates

The writing requirement triggered is the land-conveyance provision (and potentially the trust provision, since Anna directed Ben to hold as trustee). The dispositive aspect is the absence of any signed memorandum by Anna. Part-performance removes this bar because Claire satisfies all three traditional elements recognized in *Neale*-line jurisdictions: (i) actual, exclusive possession for fifteen years; (ii) payment of valuable consideration through personal funds; and (iii) permanent, substantial improvements ($6,500 in construction plus landscaping and utilities). These acts are unequivocally referable to the oral agreement and not to a mere landlord-tenant or license relationship.

## 6. Exceptions / Promissory Estoppel

Under classical part-performance per *Neale*, Claire's claim is strong: possession plus permanent improvements is the paradigm case. Under modern promissory estoppel (Restatement (Second) § 139), Claire also prevails — Anna's promise was clear, reliance was foreseeable, and injustice can be avoided only by enforcement. Part-performance is the stronger theory here because it directly yields specific performance, whereas promissory estoppel in many jurisdictions limits relief to reliance damages. The facts — fifteen years of possession, substantial construction, and Anna's acquiescence — make the classical doctrine dispositive without needing to invoke the more flexible but remedially uncertain estoppel theory.

## 7. Defenses / Mistake

Defense (a): Anna paid for some improvements. This does not defeat Claire's claim; it reinforces that Anna treated the arrangement as a joint undertaking consistent with the oral promise. At most, the heirs could seek an equitable offset, but co-investment by the promisor strengthens, not weakens, the inference of an agreement. Defense (b): Anna retained some rental income. Under *Neale*, the critical inquiry is Claire's reliance and improvements, not whether the promisor also derived interim benefits. Anna's retention of some rents is consistent with her nominal legal title and does not negate the oral promise. Defense (c): Claire used some rentals for personal expenses. This is the weakest defense. The oral promise contained no express condition restricting rental use; even if it did, minor diversion of rental income over fifteen years is de minimis and would not constitute a material breach sufficient to forfeit Claire's equitable interest, particularly given the doctrine of substantial performance.

## 8. Strongest Counterargument for the Heirs

The heirs' best argument is that Claire's possession and improvements are equally consistent with a revocable family license — a mother permitting a daughter to live on and improve family property — rather than an enforceable contract to convey. Under the "unequivocal referability" test, if the heirs can prove at trial that families in similar circumstances routinely allow children to occupy and improve property without any promise of ownership, and that Anna's statements were aspirational rather than contractual, the acts of part-performance become ambiguous and insufficient to overcome the Statute of Frauds. The heirs must produce evidence that Anna treated the property as her own throughout (e.g., retaining rents, paying taxes, making independent decisions about the property) and that no witnesses corroborate the specific oral promise.

## 9. Bounded Uncertainty and Practical Directions

Key uncertainties include: (i) whether State X requires all three elements of part-performance or only possession plus improvements; (ii) the quantum of improvement deemed "substantial" — $6,500 fifteen years ago is significant but a court could question sufficiency relative to total property value; (iii) whether the unequivocal-referability standard is applied strictly (some jurisdictions reject part-performance where possession could be explained as familial accommodation); and (iv) whether Ben's failure to act as trustee creates an independent basis for a constructive trust versus merely corroborating the oral promise.

Claire's counsel should take three concrete steps. First, secure testimony from disinterested witnesses (the tenant, contractors, neighbors) who can corroborate both the specific terms of Anna's oral promise and Claire's exclusive possession and improvement activity, thereby satisfying the clear-and-convincing evidence standard many equity courts require. Second, compile detailed documentary evidence — receipts, permits, bank records, tax filings — establishing the precise dollar amount and permanence of Claire's improvements and demonstrating that the expenditures are inexplicable absent a promised ownership interest. Third, plead alternative theories in the complaint — specific performance based on part-performance, constructive trust based on unjust enrichment, and promissory estoppel for damages — to preserve all remedial avenues and ensure that even if the court applies a stricter part-performance standard, Claire retains a viable path to relief.

# BENCH MEMO: CLAIRE v. HEIRS OF ANNA — ORAL PROMISE TO CONVEY LAND

**1. Jurisdiction Assumption**

I assume State X follows traditional U.S. common-law equitable principles as articulated in *Neale v. Neales*, 76 U.S. (9 Wall.) 1 (1869), which recognized that equity will enforce an oral agreement to convey land—notwithstanding the Statute of Frauds—where the promisee has taken possession, made valuable and permanent improvements, and changed position in reliance on the promise such that non-enforcement would work a fraud. I assume State X has not enacted a statutory abolition of the part-performance doctrine and follows the majority common-law rule that part performance sufficient in quality and quantity can remove the Statute of Frauds bar. This choice matters because jurisdictions vary on whether possession alone suffices or whether possession plus improvements plus payment of consideration are all required; under *Neale*, the Supreme Court demanded a confluence of possession, improvements, and reliance acts referable exclusively to the oral agreement.

**2. Bottom-Line Outcome**

Claire's claim should succeed. The recommended remedy is imposition of a constructive trust on the property in Claire's favor, with an order directing the heirs to convey legal title to her. Specific performance of the oral promise is the functional equivalent and equally appropriate. If a court were reluctant to order conveyance, a constructive trust achieves the same result on unjust-enrichment grounds reinforced by the oral promise. Damages under promissory estoppel should be pleaded as an alternative but are inferior because monetary relief cannot adequately compensate for a unique parcel of land improved over fifteen years.

**3. Controlling Doctrine**

The Statute of Frauds, requiring a signed writing for the transfer of an interest in land, is dispositive on its face and would bar enforcement of Anna's oral promise. However, the equitable doctrine of part performance, as applied in *Neale*, controls the result. In *Neale*, the Court held that where a party enters into possession under an oral agreement, expends substantial sums in permanent improvements, and the acts of performance are unequivocally referable to the contract rather than to any other relationship, equity will decree specific performance to prevent the statute from becoming an instrument of fraud. Claire's fifteen years of possession, $6,500 expenditure on permanent structures, and management of the property satisfy all three *Neale* requirements.

**4. Formation/Consideration**

Claire's acts constitute valid consideration, not merely conditions of a gratuitous gift. Anna's promise was explicitly bargained for: Claire was to marry and move onto the property, which she did, thereby incurring legal detriment (binding herself in marriage, uprooting her living arrangements, and committing personal savings). The $6,500 in improvements and the ongoing maintenance further constitute substantial detrimental reliance that goes beyond a mere license. A court should characterize Claire's marriage-and-relocation as bargained-for inducement and her expenditures as both consideration and reliance, distinguishing this from a revocable conditional gift because Claire's performance was requested by Anna as the quid pro quo for the conveyance.

**5. Statute of Frauds Gates**

The writing requirement triggered is the land-conveyance provision of the Statute of Frauds (typically § 4 of the original English statute or its State X equivalent). No signed memorandum exists. Part performance removes the bar because Claire can demonstrate all three classic elements: (a) exclusive possession taken under the oral agreement, (b) payment of valuable consideration in the form of $6,500 in permanent, substantial improvements, and (c) acts unequivocally referable to the oral contract—building additions and outbuildings on property she did not own makes no sense absent the promised conveyance. Under *Neale*, these acts collectively estop the promisor's successors from invoking the statute.

**6. Exceptions / Promissory Estoppel**

Under classical *Neale*-style part performance, Claire's claim is strong: possession for fifteen years, permanent structural improvements, and exclusive management of the property satisfy the doctrine fully. Under modern promissory estoppel (Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 139), Claire can also recover because Anna's promise foreseeably induced substantial reliance, and injustice can be avoided only by enforcement. The part-performance theory is stronger here because it directly yields specific performance of the land contract, whereas promissory estoppel under § 139 gives courts discretion to limit relief to reliance damages. Given the unique nature of real property and the fifteen-year occupancy, part performance is the superior vehicle.

**7. Defenses / Mistake**

Defense (a)—Anna paid for some improvements: This actually reinforces Claire's claim because it shows Anna acknowledged the arrangement and acted consistently with it. At most, it raises an offset question, not a bar. Defense (b)—Anna retained some rental income: Partial retention of rents by an oral trustee or promisor does not defeat the underlying agreement; it may reflect Anna's reserved life interest or informal cost-sharing and is consistent with her supervisory role. Defense (c)—Claire used some rentals for personal expenses: This is the weakest defense. The oral promise contained no express condition restricting rental use, and even if it did, diversion of modest rental income for personal expenses over fifteen years is a trivial breach that does not justify forfeiture of the entire estate interest. Equity abhors forfeiture, and no court following *Neale* would deny Claire relief on this basis absent a material, willful breach of an express and essential condition.

**8. Strongest Counterargument for the Heirs**

The heirs' best argument is that Anna's oral statement was a revocable expression of donative intent—a conditional gift promise—rather than an enforceable bilateral contract, and that Claire's acts of improvement were those of a permissive licensee or family member, not unequivocally referable to a binding agreement. If the heirs can prove at trial that Anna retained dominion over the property (collected rents, paid taxes, directed improvements, and held herself out as owner to third parties throughout the fifteen years), they can argue that Claire's possession was never exclusive and her improvements were consistent with a family accommodation rather than contract performance. This would undermine the "unequivocal referability" requirement of *Neale* and potentially restore the Statute of Frauds bar.

**9. Bounded Uncertainty and Practical Directions**

Key doctrinal uncertainties include: (i) whether State X requires all three *Neale* elements or accepts possession plus improvements alone; (ii) the quantum of improvement that qualifies as "substantial" (some jurisdictions require improvements disproportionate to the value recoverable at law); and (iii) whether Anna's retention of some rents and payment of some improvement costs negates the exclusivity of Claire's possession. Some jurisdictions also question whether marriage alone constitutes sufficient part performance.

Three concrete steps for Claire's counsel: First, secure and present documentary evidence—receipts, contractor invoices, bank records, and photographs—establishing that Claire personally funded the $6,500 in improvements and that these improvements are permanent, substantial, and referable only to the oral promise. Second, obtain testimony from the tenant, neighbors, and any witnesses to Anna's oral statements (including Ben's acknowledgment of the trustee designation) to corroborate both the existence and terms of the oral agreement and Claire's exclusive possessory conduct. Third, plead both part performance and promissory estoppel as alternative theories, and request both specific performance and a constructive trust in the prayer for relief, preserving reliance damages as a fallback, to ensure the court has maximum flexibility to grant equitable relief under whichever doctrinal framework State X favors.

# BENCH MEMO: Claire v. Heirs of Anna — Oral Promise to Convey Land

## 1. Jurisdiction Assumption

This analysis assumes State X follows traditional U.S. common-law equitable principles as articulated in *Neale v. Neales*, 76 U.S. (9 Wall.) 1 (1869), which recognized that part performance of an oral contract for the conveyance of land may remove the bar of the Statute of Frauds when the acts of performance are referable exclusively to the oral agreement and would make it a fraud to permit the promisor (or her heirs) to invoke the statute. This choice matters because *Neale* represents the classical federal articulation of the part-performance exception, requiring a combination of possession, payment or expenditure, and valuable improvements — all three of which are present here. Some jurisdictions have codified narrower or broader versions of this exception; I assume State X has not departed materially from the *Neale* framework. If State X follows the minority rule requiring that possession alone is insufficient without payment of purchase money specifically, the analysis at Point 9 would shift.

## 2. Bottom-Line Outcome

Claire's claim should succeed. The recommended remedy is imposition of a constructive trust on the property in Claire's favor, with an order directing the heirs to convey legal title. Specific performance of the oral promise is the functional equivalent and equally appropriate. A constructive trust is marginally preferable because it avoids the conceptual objection that equity is "enforcing" an oral contract and instead frames relief as preventing the unjust enrichment of the heirs. Damages under promissory estoppel should be preserved as an alternative but are inferior because they cannot adequately compensate fifteen years of possession, improvement, and reliance.

## 3. Controlling Doctrine

The Statute of Frauds, requiring a signed writing for transfers of interests in land, is dispositive on its face and would bar Claire's claim if applied mechanically. However, the equitable doctrine of part performance as stated in *Neale* controls the result. The Supreme Court in *Neale* held that where a party, in reliance on an oral agreement, takes possession, makes valuable and permanent improvements, and changes her position so substantially that restoration to the status quo ante is impossible, it would be a fraud to allow the other party to shelter behind the statute. The legal standard is threefold: (a) possession delivered and taken under the oral agreement, (b) valuable and permanent improvements made in reliance thereon, and (c) such change of position that non-enforcement would itself work a fraud. Claire satisfies all three elements convincingly.

## 4. Formation/Consideration

Claire's acts constitute valid consideration, not merely conditions of a gratuitous gift. Anna's promise was explicitly bargained for: Claire was to marry, move onto the property, and occupy and improve it. Claire's marriage, her expenditure of $6,500 in personal savings for construction, her ongoing maintenance and improvement of the property, and her management of the tenant all constitute legal detriment undertaken at Anna's inducement. A court should characterize these acts as consideration supporting an enforceable bilateral agreement, or at minimum as the requested performance of a unilateral contract that became binding upon Claire's full performance. The marriage alone, as a change in legal status undertaken in reliance on the promise, has historically been recognized as sufficient consideration in equity.

## 5. Statute of Frauds Gates

Two SoF categories are triggered: the land-conveyance provision (requiring a writing for any transfer of an interest in real property) and potentially the one-year provision (the arrangement contemplated performance over an indefinite period). The land-conveyance provision is dispositive. Part performance removes the bar because Claire's acts — taking exclusive possession, spending $6,500 on permanent improvements, and occupying the property for fifteen years — are unequivocally referable to the oral agreement and not explicable on any other ground. Under *Neale*, such acts make it unconscionable to permit the statute to serve as an instrument of fraud.

## 6. Exceptions / Promissory Estoppel

Under classical *Neale*-style part performance, Claire's claim is strong: she possesses the trifecta of possession, permanent improvements, and substantial change of position. Under modern promissory estoppel (Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 139), Claire also has a viable claim because her reliance was reasonable, foreseeable, and substantial, and injustice can be avoided only by enforcement. The part-performance doctrine is stronger here because it directly yields specific performance or a constructive trust, whereas promissory estoppel under § 139 gives courts discretion to limit relief to reliance damages. The evidentiary fit is also better: Claire's acts are classically "referable" to the oral agreement in the way *Neale* demands.

## 7. Defenses / Mistake

Defense (a) — Anna paid for some improvements: This actually reinforces Claire's claim, as it shows Anna acknowledged and participated in the arrangement. Anna's contributions are consistent with a mother assisting the agreed-upon improvement of property she intended to convey, not with retaining ownership. Defense (b) — Anna retained some rental income: Partial retention of rents by Anna during her lifetime is ambiguous but does not defeat the claim. It may reflect informal family arrangements or Anna's retained life interest, and it is consistent with Anna holding legal title as de facto trustee. Defense (c) — Claire used some rentals for personal expenses: This is the weakest defense. The oral promise contained no express restriction on rental use; the promise was to "give the lot and all buildings." Even if such a condition existed, Claire's use of some rental income for personal expenses over fifteen years is a trivially immaterial deviation that does not constitute a material breach warranting forfeiture of the entire equitable interest, particularly given the doctrine of disproportionate forfeiture.

## 8. Strongest Counterargument for the Heirs

The strongest argument is that Anna's oral statement was a gratuitous, revocable expression of donative intent — not a binding contract — and that Claire's occupancy was merely a permissive family license that Anna could have revoked at any time. Under this theory, there was no bargained-for exchange; the marriage was Claire's personal decision, not consideration; and the improvements were voluntary enhancements by a licensee. To succeed, the heirs must prove at trial that Anna never manifested contractual intent (as opposed to aspirational generosity), that Claire understood her occupancy was precarious, and that no quid pro quo was discussed. If the heirs can show that Anna's statements were consistently framed as future hopes rather than present commitments, and that Claire never demanded assurance of title, this recharacterization could defeat both the contract and the part-performance claim.

## 9. Bounded Uncertainty and Practical Directions

Key doctrinal uncertainties include: (i) whether State X requires all three *Neale* elements or treats possession plus improvements as sufficient; (ii) whether "substantial" improvements have a quantitative threshold relative to land value; (iii) whether Anna's partial retention of rents undermines the exclusivity of Claire's possession; and (iv) whether State X recognizes promissory estoppel as an independent basis to overcome the SoF for land transfers, or limits relief to part performance.

Claire's counsel should take three concrete steps. First, secure and present testimony from witnesses (neighbors, the tenant, Ben himself in his earlier trustee capacity, contractors) establishing that Claire's occupancy and improvements were understood by all parties as flowing from Anna's specific oral promise, thereby proving the acts are "referable" to the agreement. Second, compile complete financial documentation — receipts, bank records, contractor invoices — quantifying Claire's $6,500 expenditure and all subsequent improvements to demonstrate the impossibility of adequate restoration through money damages alone. Third, plead in the alternative: assert part performance for specific performance or constructive trust as the primary claim, promissory estoppel under § 139 as a secondary claim, and unjust enrichment or quantum meruit as a fallback, ensuring that even if the court rejects the contractual theory, Claire recovers at minimum the value of her improvements and reliance expenditures.
