The heirs would need to present evidence—testimony from family members, Anna's statements to third parties, the absence of any discussion of conveyance mechanics—showing that no meeting of minds occurred and that the arrangement was understood by both parties as gratuitous and revocable.

## 9. Bounded Uncertainty and Practical Directions

Key doctrinal uncertainties include: whether State X requires all three elements of part performance or treats possession plus one additional element as sufficient; whether $6,500 in improvements (in the relevant time period) qualifies as "substantial" relative to the property's value; whether Claire's collection of rents strengthens her claim (showing dominion consistent with ownership) or weakens it (suggesting she was merely a managing agent); and whether State X permits specific performance under promissory estoppel or limits that doctrine to damages.

Three concrete steps Claire's counsel should take: First, retain an appraiser to establish the value of Claire's improvements relative to the property's total value at the time of improvement and at present, demonstrating that the improvements were substantial, permanent, and disproportionate to what any tenant or licensee would undertake. Second, gather all documentary and testimonial evidence of Anna's statements to neighbors, friends, and family acknowledging Claire's ownership interest—letters, holiday cards, conversations witnessed by the tenant or by Ben—to establish the oral agreement's existence and terms beyond Claire's self-serving testimony. Third, plead both part performance and constructive trust as alternative equitable theories, ensuring that even if the court finds the part-performance elements marginally insufficient, it can impose a constructive trust to prevent unjust enrichment by the heirs who would otherwise receive a windfall of Claire's fifteen years of labor and capital investment.

# BENCH MEMO: Claire v. Heirs of Anna — Oral Promise to Convey Land

## 1. Jurisdiction Assumption

This analysis assumes State X follows traditional U.S. common-law equitable doctrine as articulated in *Neale v. Neales*, 76 U.S. (9 Wall.) 1 (1869), which recognized that part performance of an oral contract for the conveyance of land may take the case out of the Statute of Frauds when the acts of performance are referable exclusively to the oral agreement and would render enforcement of the statute a vehicle for fraud. I assume State X has not enacted a statutory abrogation of the part-performance exception and follows the majority common-law rule requiring possession plus valuable improvements (the "tripartite" test: possession, payment of consideration or improvements, and acts exclusively referable to the contract). This choice matters because a minority of jurisdictions require only two of three elements, while a handful reject part performance entirely for land transfers, which would materially alter the outcome.

## 2. Bottom-Line Outcome

Claire's claim should succeed. The recommended remedy is imposition of a constructive trust on the property in Claire's favor, with an order directing the heirs to convey legal title. Specific performance of the oral promise is the functional equivalent and equally appropriate. If the court is reluctant to grant equitable title, a constructive trust is the more doctrinally precise vehicle because Anna's promise created an intended but unwritten trust with Ben as trustee. Damages under promissory estoppel serve as an adequate fallback but would under-compensate Claire given fifteen years of possession, improvement, and reliance.

## 3. Controlling Doctrine

The Statute of Frauds, requiring a signed writing for transfers of interests in land, is dispositive on its face and would bar Claire's claim absent an equitable exception. The controlling equitable doctrine is part performance. Under *Neale*, the Supreme Court held that where an oral agreement for land is followed by possession, valuable and permanent improvements, and conduct referable solely to the agreement, equity will decree specific performance notwithstanding the absence of a writing, because to permit the promisor (or her heirs) to invoke the statute would itself perpetrate a fraud. The standard requires acts "unequivocally referable" to the oral contract that make it impossible to restore the parties to the status quo ante through monetary compensation alone.

## 4. Formation/Consideration

Claire's acts constitute valid consideration, not merely conditions of a gratuitous gift. Anna's promise was explicitly bargained-for: conveyance in exchange for Claire's marriage, relocation onto the property, and assumption of occupancy and improvement responsibilities. Claire's marriage, her expenditure of $6,500 in personal savings, her construction of permanent additions and outbuildings, and her management of the property (including tenant relations) all constitute detrimental reliance and bargained-for exchange. A court should characterize Claire's marriage and move as inducement forming the consideration for a bilateral oral contract, and her subsequent expenditures as performance of that contract. Even if characterized as a conditional gift, the conditions were fully performed, ripening Claire's equitable interest.

## 5. Statute of Frauds Gates

Two SoF categories are triggered: the land-transfer provision and, arguably, the provision governing agreements not to be performed within one year (the arrangement contemplated indefinite duration). The land-transfer provision is dispositive. Part performance removes the bar because Claire satisfies all three traditional elements: (a) she took exclusive possession with Anna's consent and maintained it for fifteen years; (b) she made valuable, permanent improvements totaling at least $6,500 plus labor; and (c) these acts are referable exclusively to the oral promise — no other explanation accounts for a daughter building a large addition and outbuildings on property she does not own.

## 6. Exceptions / Promissory Estoppel

Under *Neale*-style classical part performance, Claire's claim is strong because her possession, improvements, and expenditures are precisely the type of conduct the doctrine was designed to protect. Under modern promissory estoppel (Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 139), Claire also prevails: Anna's promise was clear, reliance was foreseeable and substantial, and injustice can be avoided only by enforcement. Classical part performance is the stronger theory because it directly yields specific performance as of right, whereas promissory estoppel under § 139 gives the court discretion to limit the remedy to reliance damages. Given the permanence of Claire's improvements and the impossibility of adequate monetary restoration, part performance is the superior vehicle.

## 7. Defenses / Mistake

Defense (a): That Anna paid for some improvements actually reinforces Claire's claim — it shows Anna acknowledged the arrangement and invested in property she intended Claire to own. It does not negate Claire's independent expenditures. Defense (b): Anna's retention of some rental income is consistent with Anna holding legal title as trustee or life-estate holder; it does not disprove the oral agreement and may reflect Anna's reserved life interest. Defense (c): Claire's use of some rentals for personal expenses is not a material breach defeating equity. The oral promise contained no express condition restricting rental use, and even if implied, de minimis or incidental personal use over fifteen years does not constitute the kind of inequitable conduct that would bar relief in equity. None of these defenses is likely to defeat Claire's claim.

## 8. Strongest Counterargument for the Heirs

The heirs' best argument is that Anna's promise was a revocable, gratuitous conditional gift — not a contract — and that Claire's acts of improvement were those of a permissive licensee or family member, not acts "unequivocally referable" to an enforceable agreement. If the heirs can prove at trial that Anna retained dominion and control (collecting rents, directing improvements, paying for construction), that Claire never treated herself as owner (never paid taxes, never insured the property in her name), and that the improvements are explicable by the familial relationship rather than a bargain, then the acts lose their "exclusive referability" to an oral contract, and the part-performance exception fails. The heirs must produce evidence that Anna's conduct was consistent with ownership retained, not ownership promised.

## 9. Bounded Uncertainty and Practical Directions

Key uncertainties include: whether State X requires all three elements of part performance or only two; whether "substantial" improvements must exceed a particular threshold relative to land value; whether possession must be "exclusive" (Claire's sharing occupancy with a tenant or Anna's supervisory visits could be contested); and whether *Neale*'s referability standard is "solely and unequivocally" referable or merely "reasonably" referable to the oral contract. Additionally, some jurisdictions distinguish oral trusts of land from oral contracts to convey, applying different evidentiary standards.

Claire's counsel should take three concrete steps. First, secure and present documentary evidence of Claire's independent expenditures — canceled checks, receipts, contractor invoices, and bank records showing the $6,500 outlay and any additional spending — to establish the quantum of improvement beyond dispute. Second, obtain testimony from the tenant, neighbors, and any witnesses to Anna's oral statements (including Ben, who was directed to act as trustee) to corroborate the terms of the promise and its exclusive referability, and file a detailed verified complaint reciting each element of part performance with specificity. Third, move for discovery of Anna's estate records, tax filings, and any correspondence to demonstrate that Anna never treated the property as her own beneficial asset during the fifteen-year period, and that Claire bore the burdens of ownership — thereby negating the heirs' characterization of Claire as a mere licensee.

# BENCH MEMO: Claire v. Heirs of Anna — Oral Promise to Convey Land

## 1. Jurisdiction Assumption

I assume State X follows traditional U.S. common-law equitable doctrine as articulated in **Neale v. Neales, 76 U.S. (9 Wall.) 1 (1869)**, which holds that an oral agreement to convey land, otherwise barred by the Statute of Frauds, may be specifically enforced in equity where the promisee has partly performed by taking possession, making valuable and substantial improvements, and acting in reliance on the promise under circumstances where mere restitution of money would be inadequate. This choice matters because Neale establishes a relatively claimant-friendly standard: the combination of possession plus substantial improvements plus circumstances making restitution inadequate is sufficient to remove the Statute of Frauds bar, without requiring the more restrictive three-prong test some modern jurisdictions impose (requiring acts "unequivocally referable" to the contract). If State X follows a stricter "unequivocal referability" standard, Claire's burden increases marginally but likely remains met given the fifteen-year duration and scale of improvements.

## 2. Bottom-Line Outcome

Claire's claim should succeed. The recommended remedy is **specific performance via imposition of a constructive trust** on the property in Claire's favor, with the heirs directed to convey legal title. The facts — fifteen years of exclusive possession, $6,500 in personal savings for construction, substantial improvements to structures and utilities, all with Anna's knowledge and encouragement — squarely satisfy the Neale framework. Alternatively, if a court finds any equitable impediment to full conveyance, damages under promissory estoppel provide a fallback, but specific performance is both the primary and strongest remedy.

## 3. Controlling Doctrine

The **Statute of Frauds for conveyances of interests in land** is dispositive on its face: no signed writing exists memorializing Anna's promise. However, the equitable doctrine of **part performance** as recognized in Neale controls the result. In Neale, the Supreme Court held that where a party enters possession under an oral agreement, expends substantial sums in permanent improvements, and the circumstances are such that restoration to the status quo ante is practically impossible, equity will enforce the oral promise notwithstanding the absence of a writing. The rationale is that the Statute of Frauds was enacted to prevent fraud, not to enable it; allowing the promisor's heirs to retain both the land and the improvements would itself constitute the fraud the statute was designed to prevent.

## 4. Formation/Consideration

Claire's acts constitute valid consideration, not merely conditions of a gratuitous gift. Anna's promise was explicitly bargained-for: conveyance in exchange for Claire's marriage, relocation, and occupation of the property. Claire's marriage (a legal detriment and change of position), her expenditure of $6,500 in savings, her labor in improving the property, and her fifteen-year commitment to maintaining and managing it (including tenant relations) all constitute substantial consideration. A court should characterize Claire's acts as both consideration supporting a bilateral agreement and detrimental reliance supporting estoppel. The marriage alone, as a classic forbearance/change of position, distinguishes this from a mere revocable license or conditional gift.

## 5. Statute of Frauds Gates

The applicable SoF provision is the **land conveyance writing requirement** — typically requiring a memorandum signed by the party to be charged describing the property and the essential terms. No such writing exists. The dispositive aspect is the complete absence of Anna's signature on any document. Part performance removes the bar because Claire satisfies all three traditional elements: (a) taking and maintaining exclusive possession for fifteen years, (b) making substantial, permanent improvements ($6,500 in construction plus landscaping and utility work), and (c) paying value referable to the agreement. Under Neale, these acts — particularly the improvements that cannot be restored in kind — make it unconscionable to permit the Statute to shield the heirs.

## 6. Exceptions / Promissory Estoppel

Under **classical part performance** per Neale, Claire's claim is strong: possession, improvements, and irreversible change of position are all present. Under **modern promissory estoppel** (Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 139), Claire must show a promise reasonably expected to induce action, actual reliance, and injustice avoidable only by enforcement. Both routes reach the same result, but classical part performance is the stronger theory because it is the established equitable basis for specific performance of land contracts and does not require the court to engage in the more discretionary "injustice" balancing of § 139. Promissory estoppel is valuable as an alternative, particularly if a court limits part-performance relief, but it more commonly yields reliance damages rather than specific performance. Claire's counsel should plead both but lead with part performance.

## 7. Defenses / Mistake

**(a) Anna paid for some improvements.** This is largely irrelevant. Even if Anna contributed, Claire independently spent $6,500 — a substantial sum — and performed labor. Anna's contributions are consistent with her role as the promisee's mother and informal supervisor, reinforcing rather than undermining the existence of the agreement. **(b) Anna retained some rental income.** Partial retention of rents by Anna during her lifetime is consistent with Anna holding legal title as informal trustee or with informal family arrangements. It does not negate Claire's possession or the oral agreement; at most it creates a minor accounting issue. **(c) Claire used some rentals for personal expenses.** This defense fails because the oral promise contained no express condition restricting rental use. Even if it did, the alleged breach is trivial relative to Claire's fifteen years of substantial performance. Equity does not forfeit an entire interest for a minor, unspecified deviation. None of these defenses defeats Claire's equity claim.

## 8. Strongest Counterargument for the Heirs

The heirs' strongest argument is that **Anna's oral statement was a revocable, gratuitous promise — an intention to make a gift — rather than an enforceable bilateral contract**, and that Claire's acts were conditions of a license rather than consideration for a bargain. Under this theory, part performance cannot rescue the claim because there was no "agreement" to perform in the first place; Anna simply allowed her daughter to live on the property and make improvements, retaining full authority to revoke. To succeed, the heirs must prove at trial that (i) the oral exchange lacked bargained-for consideration (i.e., Anna did not seek or require Claire's marriage or relocation as the price of conveyance), (ii) Anna's statements were expressions of future intent rather than present commitment, and (iii) Anna's conduct — retaining rents, paying for some improvements, never executing a deed — was inconsistent with a binding obligation. This is a factually intensive argument, but the fifteen-year duration, Anna's supervisory involvement, and Claire's massive expenditures make it difficult to sustain.

## 9. Bounded Uncertainty and Practical Directions

**Doctrinal uncertainties** include: (i) whether State X requires improvements to be "unequivocally referable" to the oral contract rather than explainable by a landlord-tenant or family relationship; (ii) whether possession alone, without improvements, would suffice (here moot given the improvements, but relevant to the standard applied); (iii) the quantum of improvement required to be "substantial"; and (iv) whether State X recognizes promissory estoppel as an independent basis for specific performance of land transfers or limits it to damages.

**Three concrete steps for Claire's counsel:** First, secure and present detailed documentary evidence of Claire's $6,500 expenditure — receipts, bank records, contractor testimony, and appraisals showing the current enhanced value of improvements — to establish that restitution in money would be inadequate and that the improvements are permanent and substantial. Second, obtain testimony from neighbors, the tenant, and any surviving witnesses (including Ben) regarding Anna's oral statements, Claire's exclusive possession, and Anna's acknowledgment of Claire's ownership interest, to corroborate the existence and terms of the oral agreement and defeat the "mere gift" characterization. Third, plead both part performance and promissory estoppel as alternative equitable theories, and request both specific performance and a constructive trust in the prayer for relief, preserving the damages alternative, so the court has maximum flexibility to grant relief under whichever doctrinal framework State X most readily accepts.

# BENCH MEMO: CLAIRE v. HEIRS OF ANNA — ORAL PROMISE TO CONVEY LAND

**1. Jurisdiction Assumption**

This analysis assumes State X follows traditional U.S. common-law equitable doctrine as articulated in *Neale v. Neales*, 76 U.S. (9 Wall.) 1 (1869), which recognized that equity will enforce an oral agreement to convey land, notwithstanding the Statute of Frauds, where the promisee has taken possession, made valuable and permanent improvements, and performed the agreed-upon conditions in reliance on the promise. I assume State X has not enacted a statutory abolition of the part-performance exception and follows the majority rule that possession plus substantial improvements (not possession alone) satisfies the doctrine. This choice matters because a minority of jurisdictions (following the English rule) require all three classic indicia — possession, payment of consideration, and valuable improvements — while others accept possession plus either payment or improvements. Under the *Neale* framework, Claire's combination of acts is particularly strong.

**2. Bottom-Line Outcome**

Claire's claim should succeed. The recommended remedy is imposition of a constructive trust on the property in Claire's favor, with an order directing the heirs to convey legal title. Specific performance of the oral promise and a constructive trust are functionally equivalent here; the constructive trust framing is cleaner because Anna died intestate and title passed by operation of law to heirs who never held a contractual obligation. Alternatively, if a court finds the equitable claim somehow deficient, damages under promissory estoppel should be awarded measured by Claire's reliance expenditures plus the enhanced value of improvements.

**3. Controlling Doctrine**

The Statute of Frauds, requiring a signed writing for transfers of interests in land, is dispositive on its face and would bar enforcement of the oral promise standing alone. However, the equitable doctrine of part performance, as applied in *Neale*, controls the result. In *Neale*, the Supreme Court held that where a party enters possession under an oral agreement, expends substantial sums in permanent improvements, and performs the conditions of the bargain, equity will not permit the statute to be used as an instrument of fraud. The standard requires acts of performance that are unequivocally referable to the oral agreement — meaning the acts make sense only by reference to the promised conveyance — and that the promisee would suffer an injustice not remediable by money damages if the promise were not enforced.

**4. Formation/Consideration**

Claire's acts constitute valid consideration, not merely conditions of a gratuitous gift. Anna's promise was expressly conditional on Claire's marriage and relocation — acts that imposed legal detriment on Claire (changing her living situation, committing personal savings). The $6,500 expenditure on permanent improvements, undertaken in reliance on the promise, transforms this from a revocable license or conditional gift into an enforceable bargain. A court should characterize Claire's marriage-and-move as the bargained-for inducement and her subsequent improvements as both further consideration and the performance that triggers the part-performance exception. Even if characterized as a conditional gift, the substantial detrimental reliance converts it into an enforceable obligation under either part-performance or promissory estoppel.

**5. Statute of Frauds Gates**

Two SoF categories are triggered: the land-conveyance provision and, arguably, the provision governing agreements not performable within one year (given the open-ended nature of the arrangement). The land-conveyance provision is dispositive. Part performance removes the bar because Claire satisfies all three classical indicia: (a) she took exclusive possession with Anna's consent; (b) she paid substantial consideration through her own funds; and (c) she made permanent, valuable improvements. These acts are unequivocally referable to the oral promise — a mere licensee or tenant would not invest $6,500 in permanent construction.

**6. Exceptions / Promissory Estoppel**

Under *Neale*-style classical part performance, Claire's claim is strong: fifteen years of possession, permanent improvements, and conduct consistent only with an ownership interest. Under modern promissory estoppel (Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 139), Claire also prevails because Anna's promise was clear, reliance was foreseeable, actual reliance was substantial and detrimental, and injustice can be avoided only by enforcement. The classical part-performance doctrine is the stronger vehicle because it yields specific performance as of right, whereas promissory estoppel in many jurisdictions limits relief to reliance damages. Claire should plead both but lead with part performance.

**7. Defenses / Mistake**

Defense (a) — Anna paid for some improvements: This actually reinforces Claire's claim, showing Anna acknowledged Claire's possessory interest and treated the property as Claire's. At most it creates a minor offset. Defense (b) — Anna retained some rental income: Consistent with Anna holding legal title as informal trustee; it does not negate Claire's equitable interest and may reflect Anna collecting management fees or reimbursement. Defense (c) — Claire used some rentals for personal expenses: This is the weakest defense. The oral promise contained no express restriction on rental use, and even if it did, diversion of modest rental income is not a material breach sufficient to forfeit fifteen years of improvements and possession. Equity abhors forfeiture, and no court following *Neale* would deny relief over incidental personal use of rents.

**8. Strongest Counterargument for the Heirs**

The heirs' best argument is that the oral promise was a revocable conditional gift — not a contract — and that Claire's acts of improvement were those of a permissive occupant or licensee, not unequivocally referable to an agreement to convey. If the heirs can prove at trial that Anna consistently treated the property as her own (e.g., claiming it on tax returns, insuring it solely in her name, telling third parties it remained hers), and that Claire's occupancy was understood by both parties as a license revocable at will, then the improvements become voluntary betterments by a licensee, and the part-performance doctrine fails for lack of unequivocal referability. The heirs must produce testimony or documents showing Anna never relinquished dominion and that Claire's occupancy was explicable without reference to a conveyance promise.

**9. Bounded Uncertainty and Practical Directions**

Key uncertainties include: whether State X requires all three part-performance indicia or only two; whether fifteen years of delay triggers laches concerns; whether "substantial" improvements is measured by dollar amount or proportionality to land value; and whether Ben's role as oral trustee creates a separate trust theory under the constructive-fraud doctrine. Claire's counsel should take three concrete steps. First, secure affidavits or deposition testimony from neighbors, the tenant, and family members confirming that Anna publicly acknowledged Claire as the beneficial owner and that Claire's occupancy was understood as permanent and ownership-based. Second, obtain appraisals documenting the before-and-after value of the property to quantify the improvements as substantial and permanent, demonstrating irreparable injury if relief is denied. Third, plead in the alternative — specific performance under part performance, constructive trust under unjust enrichment, and damages under promissory estoppel — to preserve all remedial avenues and ensure that even a court skeptical of one theory can grant relief under another.

Defense (b): Anna's retention of some rental income is ambiguous but is best explained as Anna exercising residual legal-title prerogatives consistent with an informal trust arrangement, not as evidence negating the promise. Defense (c): Claire's use of some rentals for personal expenses does not constitute breach of a condition because the oral agreement contained no restriction on rental use; moreover, an owner is entitled to use rental income freely. Even if a condition existed, the breach would be immaterial given fifteen years of faithful occupancy and improvement, and equity would not forfeit Claire's entire interest over minor rental diversions. None of these defenses should defeat Claire's equity claim.

## 8. Strongest Counterargument for the Heirs

The heirs' best argument is that Claire's possession and improvements are equally consistent with a revocable family license or informal life estate rather than an enforceable agreement to convey fee simple, and therefore the acts of part performance are not "unequivocally referable" to the alleged oral contract. Under the strict "unequivocal referability" test applied in some jurisdictions, possession by a family member and improvements funded partly by the parent could be explained without reference to an ownership agreement. To succeed, the heirs must prove at trial that Anna's conduct (paying for some improvements, retaining some rents, never relinquishing control) is more consistent with a gratuitous license than a binding promise, and that no witness can corroborate the specific terms of the alleged oral conveyance.

## 9. Bounded Uncertainty and Practical Directions

Key doctrinal uncertainties include: whether State X applies the strict "unequivocal referability" test or the more flexible "corroborative evidence" standard for part performance; whether possession by a family member alone suffices or requires additional indicia of ownership such as tax payments; and whether the oral trust direction to Ben independently supports a constructive or resulting trust theory even absent full part-performance proof.

Three concrete steps for Claire's counsel: First, obtain and introduce evidence of Claire's exclusive possession and control — tax records, utility accounts in Claire's name, insurance policies, and testimony from the tenant confirming Claire as landlord — to establish that her occupancy was as owner, not licensee. Second, secure testimony from disinterested witnesses (neighbors, contractors, the tenant) who heard Anna acknowledge Claire's ownership or the agreement's terms, thereby corroborating the oral promise and satisfying the evidentiary function of the Statute of Frauds. Third, plead in the alternative for a constructive trust based on unjust enrichment and for promissory estoppel damages, ensuring that even if the court applies a strict referability standard that defeats specific performance, Claire recovers the full value of her improvements and reliance expenditures.

# BENCH MEMO: CLAIRE v. HEIRS OF ANNA — ORAL PROMISE TO CONVEY LAND

**1. Jurisdiction Assumption**

This analysis assumes State X follows traditional U.S. common-law equitable doctrine as articulated in *Neale v. Neales*, 76 U.S. (9 Wall.) 1 (1869), which holds that an oral agreement to convey land, though facially barred by the Statute of Frauds, may be specifically enforced in equity where the promisee has partly performed in reliance on the promise through acts that are unequivocally referable to the agreement and would make it a fraud to allow the promisor (or heirs) to invoke the statute. This choice matters because *Neale* sets a relatively generous standard for part performance—requiring possession, valuable and permanent improvements, and reliance that cannot be adequately compensated by money damages—and because jurisdictions diverging from *Neale* may require stricter evidentiary showings or reject part performance as an exception altogether. I assume State X has not legislatively abolished the equitable part-performance doctrine.

**2. Bottom-Line Outcome**

Claire's claim should succeed. The recommended remedy is specific performance compelling the heirs to convey legal title to Claire, or in the alternative, imposition of a constructive trust on the property in Claire's favor. The facts satisfy every element recognized in *Neale*: an oral promise of definite content, exclusive possession for fifteen years, and substantial, permanent improvements made in reliance. Damages under promissory estoppel would be an inadequate substitute given the uniqueness of real property and the magnitude of Claire's reliance expenditures.

**3. Controlling Doctrine**

The Statute of Frauds—specifically the land-conveyance provision requiring a signed writing for any transfer of an interest in real property—is dispositive on its face and would bar enforcement of Anna's oral promise. However, the equitable doctrine of part performance, as applied in *Neale*, controls the result. The Supreme Court in *Neale* held that where a party, in reliance on an oral agreement, takes possession, makes valuable and lasting improvements, and changes her position so substantially that restoration to the status quo ante is impossible, equity will enforce the agreement notwithstanding the absence of a writing. The standard requires acts "unequivocally referable" to the oral contract—not explicable by any other relationship—and a showing that denying enforcement would itself work a fraud.

**4. Formation/Consideration**

Claire's acts constitute valid consideration, not merely a condition of a gratuitous gift. Anna's promise was expressly conditioned on Claire's marriage and relocation onto the property—acts that imposed real legal detriment on Claire (change of residence, commitment of $6,500 in savings, assumption of property management responsibilities). Under bargain theory, Claire's marriage, move, and expenditure of personal funds for permanent improvements constitute a bargained-for exchange. Even if characterized as a conditional gift, the substantial and irreversible nature of Claire's reliance transforms the arrangement into one equity will enforce. A court should characterize Claire's acts as both consideration and detrimental reliance—the marriage and move being inducement, and the $6,500 expenditure plus fifteen years of stewardship being classic consideration that removes any "mere license" characterization.

**5. Statute of Frauds Gates**

Two SoF provisions are triggered: the land-conveyance clause and potentially the one-year provision (since performance extended over fifteen years, though the agreement was performable within a lifetime). The land-conveyance clause is dispositive. Part performance removes the bar through three independently sufficient indicia recognized in *Neale*: (a) Claire took and maintained exclusive possession for fifteen years; (b) she made valuable, permanent improvements ($6,500 in construction plus landscaping and utility work); and (c) these acts are unequivocally referable to the oral agreement rather than to a landlord-tenant or familial hospitality arrangement. The combination of all three makes the case for removing the SoF bar compelling.

**6. Exceptions: Part Performance vs. Promissory Estoppel**

Under *Neale*-style classical part performance, Claire's claim is strong: possession plus permanent improvements plus payment of her own funds constitutes the traditional trifecta. Under modern promissory estoppel (Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 139), Claire must show a promise, reasonable and foreseeable reliance, and injustice avoidable only by enforcement. Both routes reach the same result, but part performance is the stronger theory because it is the historically recognized equitable exception to the land SoF, it yields specific performance rather than mere reliance damages, and Claire's facts map precisely onto *Neale*'s requirements. Promissory estoppel serves as a valuable alternative if the court finds the acts not sufficiently "unequivocal," but its remedial flexibility (potentially limiting relief to damages) makes it the secondary theory.

**7. Defenses**

Defense (a)—Anna paid for some improvements: This actually strengthens Claire's position. Anna's contributions corroborate the existence of the oral agreement and show Anna treated Claire as the beneficial owner. At most, the heirs could seek an equitable offset, but Anna's payments are consistent with a mother supporting the promised conveyance. Defense (b)—Anna retained some rental income: Partial retention of rents by Anna during her lifetime is explicable as Anna exercising residual legal-title prerogatives before formal conveyance or as informal family financial sharing. It does not negate Claire's possession or improvements, and *Neale* does not require the promisee to have captured every economic benefit. Defense (c)—Claire used some rentals for personal expenses: This is the weakest defense. No express condition required Claire to segregate rental income for property-only purposes. The oral promise was to "give the lot and all buildings"; rental-income management was an incident of possession, not a condition precedent. None of these defenses is likely to defeat Claire's equity claim.

**8. Strongest Counterargument for the Heirs**

The heirs' best argument is that Claire's acts are not "unequivocally referable" to an oral contract but are instead consistent with a gratuitous familial license—a mother allowing a married daughter to live on family property and improve it as a matter of family accommodation. To succeed, the heirs must prove at trial that: (i) Anna's statements and conduct were consistent with a revocable license rather than a promise to convey; (ii) Claire's improvements were of a type and scale that family members routinely make on property they occupy by permission; and (iii) Anna's retention of rental income and partial payment for improvements demonstrate that Anna always regarded herself as full owner. If the heirs can establish these facts, a court could find the acts equivocal and decline to take the case outside the Statute of Frauds.

**9. Bounded Uncertainty and Practical Directions**

Key doctrinal uncertainties include: whether State X requires all three part-performance elements (possession, improvements, and payment of consideration) or treats any one as sufficient; whether "substantial" improvement is measured by dollar amount, proportion of property value, or permanence; and whether a jurisdiction following *Neale* requires corroborating evidence of the oral promise beyond the claimant's own testimony. Additionally, some jurisdictions hold that possession by a family member is inherently equivocal, which could undermine Claire's showing.

Claire's counsel should take three concrete steps. First, secure corroborating testimony from Ben (who was orally directed to act as trustee), neighbors, contractors, and any witnesses to Anna's statements praising Claire's work and acknowledging Claire's ownership interest—this addresses the corroboration concern directly. Second, obtain and present documentary evidence of Claire's $6,500 expenditure (bank records, contractor receipts, building permits) and commission an appraisal showing the current value of improvements relative to the unimproved lot, establishing that monetary damages would be inadequate and that the improvements are permanent and substantial. Third, plead both part performance and promissory estoppel as alternative theories, and include a constructive-trust count alongside specific performance, so the court has maximum remedial flexibility regardless of which equitable framework it adopts.

# BENCH MEMO: Claire v. Heirs of Anna — Oral Promise to Convey Land

## 1. Jurisdiction Assumption

This analysis assumes State X follows traditional U.S. common-law equitable doctrine as articulated in *Neale v. Neales*, 76 U.S. (9 Wall.) 1 (1869), which held that an oral agreement to convey land, otherwise barred by the Statute of Frauds, may be specifically enforced in equity where the promisee has taken possession, made valuable and substantial improvements, and performed the essential conditions of the bargain. This choice matters because *Neale* represents the mainstream American equity rule on part performance—broader than the English rule (which required only possession plus payment)—and because jurisdictions diverge on whether possession alone suffices or whether improvements are independently required. I assume State X requires the tripartite showing from *Neale*: possession, valuable improvements, and performance referable to the oral contract.

## 2. Bottom-Line Outcome

Claire's claim should succeed. The recommended remedy is specific performance directing the heirs to convey legal title to Claire, or alternatively the imposition of a constructive trust over the property in Claire's favor. The facts satisfy every element of the *Neale* part-performance doctrine. Damages under promissory estoppel would be an inferior but viable fallback if the court finds any element of part performance technically deficient.

## 3. Controlling Doctrine

The Statute of Frauds, requiring a signed writing for conveyances of interests in land, is dispositive on its face and would bar Claire's claim at law. However, the equitable doctrine of part performance, as articulated in *Neale*, controls the result. In *Neale*, the Supreme Court held that where a party, in reliance on an oral promise to convey land, takes possession, makes substantial and valuable improvements, and fulfills the conditions of the agreement, it would be a fraud upon that party to permit the promisor (or heirs) to invoke the Statute of Frauds to defeat the claim. The legal standard requires acts of reliance so substantial and so clearly referable to the oral agreement that enforcing the statute would itself work a fraud.

## 4. Formation/Consideration

Claire's acts constitute valid consideration, not merely a condition of a gratuitous gift. Anna's promise was explicitly bargained-for: Claire was to marry, move onto the property, and treat it as her own. Claire's marriage, relocation, expenditure of $6,500 in personal savings for improvements, and fifteen years of stewardship constitute a classic bilateral exchange—forbearance and affirmative action in exchange for a promise to convey. Even if characterized as a conditional gift, Claire's expenditure of substantial personal funds in reliance on the promise transforms the arrangement into an enforceable bargain under equity. The court should characterize Claire's marriage and move as inducement and her substantial expenditures as both consideration and detrimental reliance.

## 5. Statute of Frauds Gates

The SoF provision triggered is the land-conveyance clause (and potentially the trust provision, since Anna directed Ben to hold as trustee). The dispositive barrier is the absence of any signed writing evidencing the conveyance or trust. Part performance removes this bar because Claire satisfies all three *Neale* elements: (a) she took exclusive possession by moving onto the property; (b) she made valuable, permanent improvements ($6,500 in construction plus landscaping and utilities); and (c) her acts are uniquely referable to the oral agreement—no reasonable explanation exists for a daughter spending personal savings to build additions on her mother's land absent such a promise.

## 6. Exceptions / Promissory Estoppel

Under classical *Neale*-style part performance, Claire's claim is strong: fifteen years of possession, major capital improvements, and acts clearly referable to the oral promise. Under modern promissory estoppel (Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 139), Claire also prevails: a promise reasonably expected to induce action, actual substantial reliance, and injustice avoidable only by enforcement. The part-performance doctrine is stronger here because it yields specific performance as of right, whereas promissory estoppel in many jurisdictions limits relief to reliance damages. Given Claire's overwhelming equitable position, classical part performance is the superior vehicle.

## 7. Defenses / Mistake

Defense (a)—Anna paid for some improvements—does not defeat Claire's claim; it is consistent with Anna's acknowledged role as beneficial owner-in-transition who assisted the very improvements she encouraged. If anything, Anna's participation corroborates the oral agreement. Defense (b)—Anna retained some rental income—similarly corroborates Anna's ongoing involvement but does not negate Claire's possession or improvements; partial retention of rents during a transitional period is consistent with the arrangement. Defense (c)—Claire used some rentals for personal expenses—is the weakest argument. No condition of the oral agreement required Claire to segregate rental income for property-only purposes; the promise was to "give the lot and all buildings" upon marriage and occupancy, not to impose a fiduciary accounting obligation. None of these defenses is likely to defeat Claire's equity claim.

## 8. Strongest Counterargument for the Heirs

The heirs' strongest argument is that Claire's acts are not uniquely referable to the oral contract but are instead consistent with a mere family license to occupy—a daughter living on her mother's property and making improvements as a grateful child, not as a contractual promisee. To succeed, the heirs must prove at trial that the improvements were modest relative to the property's value, that Claire's occupancy was permissive and revocable, and that no witnesses can corroborate the specific oral promise. If the court finds the acts equally consistent with a gratuitous arrangement, the referability requirement fails and the Statute of Frauds bars the claim.

## 9. Bounded Uncertainty and Practical Directions

Key uncertainties include: whether State X requires all three *Neale* elements or treats possession plus either payment or improvements as sufficient; whether $6,500 constitutes "substantial" improvement relative to the property's total value; whether Claire's collection of rents constitutes the kind of dominion that evidences equitable ownership; and whether § 139 promissory estoppel is recognized in State X as an independent basis for specific performance rather than merely damages.

Three concrete steps for Claire's counsel: First, secure testimony from disinterested witnesses (the tenant, neighbors, contractors) corroborating both the oral promise and Claire's exclusive possession and improvement activity, to establish referability beyond family generosity. Second, obtain appraisals showing the before-and-after value of the property to demonstrate that Claire's $6,500 expenditure plus sweat equity constituted a substantial, permanent, and irreversible enhancement—making restitution inadequate and specific performance necessary. Third, plead both part performance and promissory estoppel as alternative equitable theories, and request in the alternative a constructive trust, to preserve maximum flexibility should the court find one doctrine unavailable under State X law.

# BENCH MEMO: Claire v. Heirs of Anna — Oral Promise to Convey Land

## 1. Jurisdiction Assumption

This analysis assumes State X follows traditional U.S. common-law equitable principles as articulated in *Neale v. Neales*, 76 U.S. (9 Wall.) 1 (1869), which recognized that part performance of an oral agreement concerning land may remove the bar of the Statute of Frauds where the acts of performance are referable exclusively to the alleged contract and where denying enforcement would itself constitute a fraud. I assume State X has not enacted a statutory abrogation of the part-performance doctrine and follows the majority American rule that possession plus valuable improvements plus payment of consideration suffice. This matters because a minority of jurisdictions (following the English rule in *Maddison v. Alderson*) require that the acts of performance be "unequivocally referable" to the contract, which would raise a higher threshold. I assume State X applies the more widely adopted American standard consistent with *Neale*.

## 2. Bottom-Line Outcome

Claire's claim should succeed. The recommended remedy is imposition of a constructive trust on the property in Claire's favor, with an order directing the heirs to convey legal title to her. Alternatively, specific performance of the oral agreement is warranted. Between the two, a constructive trust is the cleaner equitable vehicle because it avoids the conceptual difficulty of "enforcing" an oral contract and instead remedies the unjust enrichment and fraud that would result from the heirs retaining property improved at Claire's expense under an oral promise. If for any reason equitable title transfer is denied, damages under promissory estoppel should be awarded at minimum to compensate Claire's $6,500 expenditure and the value of fifteen years of improvements.

## 3. Controlling Doctrine

The Statute of Frauds, requiring a signed writing for conveyances of interests in land, is dispositive on its face and would bar Claire's claim if applied mechanically. However, the equitable doctrine of part performance, as articulated in *Neale*, controls the result. The Supreme Court in *Neale* held that where a party, in reliance on an oral promise to convey land, takes possession, makes valuable and permanent improvements, and changes her position so substantially that restoration to the status quo ante is impossible, equity will not permit the Statute of Frauds to be used as an instrument of fraud. The legal standard requires: (a) an oral agreement sufficiently definite in its terms, (b) possession taken pursuant to that agreement, (c) valuable improvements made in reliance, and (d) circumstances making it inequitable to deny enforcement.

## 4. Formation/Consideration

Claire's acts constitute valid consideration, not merely conditions of a gratuitous gift. Anna's promise was bargained-for: Claire was to marry, relocate onto the property, and occupy it — acts that imposed real detriment on Claire and conferred real benefit on Anna (property maintenance, improvements, oversight of tenants). The $6,500 expenditure on construction and the fifteen years of labor on landscaping and utilities far exceed what any licensee or donee would undertake. A court should characterize Claire's marriage-and-relocation as the inducing consideration and her improvements as further executed consideration, together establishing a bilateral oral contract rather than a revocable conditional gift.

## 5. Statute of Frauds Gates

Two SoF categories are triggered: the land-conveyance provision and potentially the one-year provision (since performance contemplated an indefinite/lifetime arrangement). The land-conveyance requirement is the dispositive bar. Part performance removes it because Claire satisfies all three traditional indicia recognized in *Neale*: (1) she took exclusive possession, (2) she made substantial, permanent improvements ($6,500 in construction plus landscaping and utility work), and (3) she paid valuable consideration. These acts are referable to no plausible relationship other than the alleged oral agreement, satisfying even the stricter "unequivocal referability" test.

## 6. Exceptions / Promissory Estoppel

Under classical *Neale*-style part performance, Claire's claim is strong because she satisfies every element: possession, improvements, and reliance making rescission inequitable. Under modern promissory estoppel (Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 139), Claire also prevails: Anna made a promise she reasonably expected to induce action, Claire reasonably relied, and injustice can be avoided only by enforcement. The part-performance doctrine is the stronger vehicle here because it yields specific performance or a constructive trust — full title — whereas promissory estoppel in many jurisdictions limits recovery to reliance damages. Given the strength of Claire's improvement evidence, counsel should lead with part performance and plead estoppel as an alternative.

## 7. Defenses / Mistake

Defense (a) — Anna paid for some improvements: This actually reinforces Claire's claim, as it shows Anna treated the arrangement as real and invested in "Claire's" property. At most it creates a minor offset or confirms joint understanding. Defense (b) — Anna retained some rental income: Partial retention of rents by Anna during her lifetime is consistent with Anna holding legal title as trustee or as an accommodation; it does not negate Claire's equitable ownership, particularly since Claire also collected and used rents with Anna's knowledge. Defense (c) — Claire used some rentals for personal expenses: This is the weakest defense. No condition in the oral agreement restricted Claire's use of rental proceeds; as equitable owner, Claire was entitled to the rents. Even if a condition existed, minor diversion of rental income over fifteen years is not a material breach warranting forfeiture of the entire property interest, and equity abhors forfeitures. None of these defenses is likely to defeat Claire's claim.

## 8. Strongest Counterargument for the Heirs

The heirs' best argument is that Anna's oral statement was a revocable, gratuitous expression of intent — an unenforceable promise to make a gift — rather than a contractual undertaking supported by consideration, and that Claire's improvements were those of a permissive family occupant, not a contract purchaser. Under this theory, the acts of "part performance" are equally referable to a family license as to a contract, failing the referability requirement. To succeed, the heirs must prove at trial that: (1) Claire paid no rent or below-market consideration, (2) Anna retained dominion and control (collecting rents, directing improvements, paying taxes), and (3) the family's course of dealing was consistent with Anna remaining full owner. If the heirs can establish that Claire's occupancy looked indistinguishable from a family living arrangement rather than a contractual one, the part-performance exception may not apply.

## 9. Bounded Uncertainty and Practical Directions

Key doctrinal uncertainties include: whether State X requires all three indicia of part performance or treats any two as sufficient; whether the "unequivocal referability" standard applies (which could weaken Claire's claim if occupancy is attributable to family relationship); whether constructive trust requires proof of fraud or merely unjust enrichment; and whether $6,500 plus sweat equity over fifteen years meets the jurisdiction's threshold for "substantial" improvements.

Three concrete steps for Claire's counsel: First, obtain and present detailed evidence — receipts, contractor testimony, photographs, and appraisals — quantifying the before-and-after value of improvements to demonstrate they are substantial, permanent, and exclusively referable to the oral agreement rather than casual family generosity. Second, secure testimony from Ben (who was orally directed to act as trustee) and any other witnesses — neighbors, the tenant, family members — who can corroborate the specific terms of Anna's oral promise, establishing definiteness of the agreement and Anna's intent to create a trust. Third, plead both part performance and promissory estoppel as independent grounds, and add a constructive-trust count alongside specific performance, so that the court has maximum flexibility to grant equitable relief under whichever doctrinal framework it finds most appropriate under State X law.
