To maximize success, Claire's counsel should: (1) obtain and present testimony from neighbors, contractors, and the tenant confirming that Claire occupied the property as owner, directed all improvements, and was publicly understood to be the owner — establishing unequivocal referability; (2) secure documentary evidence of Claire's $6,500 expenditure (bank records, receipts, contractor invoices) and an appraisal showing the improvements' current value relative to the unimproved lot, demonstrating that money damages would be inadequate; and (3) plead the constructive trust theory as an independent equitable ground, arguing that allowing the heirs to retain the benefit of Claire's improvements would constitute unjust enrichment, thereby giving the court an alternative equitable basis even if it finds the part-performance showing marginally insufficient under *Neale*.

# BENCH MEMO: Claire v. Heirs of Anna — Oral Promise to Convey Land

## 1. Jurisdiction Assumption

I assume State X follows traditional U.S. common-law equitable doctrine as articulated in *Neale v. Neales*, 76 U.S. (9 Wall.) 1 (1869), which holds that an oral agreement to convey land, otherwise barred by the Statute of Frauds, may be specifically enforced in equity where the promisee has taken possession, made valuable and permanent improvements, and changed position in reliance on the promise such that refusing enforcement would itself constitute a fraud. This choice matters because *Neale* represents the mainstream nineteenth-century equitable part-performance doctrine that most American jurisdictions have adopted in some form, though modern states vary on whether possession alone suffices or whether improvements and payment are additionally required. I assume State X requires the traditional *Neale* triad: possession, valuable improvements, and reliance rendering the promisee's position otherwise irremediable by money damages.

## 2. Bottom-Line Outcome

Claire's claim should succeed. The recommended remedy is imposition of a constructive trust on the property in Claire's favor, with the court ordering the heirs to convey legal title to Claire. Specific performance of the oral promise is the functional equivalent and equally appropriate. A constructive trust is slightly preferable because it directly addresses Ben's fiduciary position as the orally designated trustee and avoids the conceptual difficulty of "enforcing" a contract that was never reduced to writing. Damages under promissory estoppel should be preserved as an alternative but are inferior because they cannot adequately compensate fifteen years of possession, improvements, and life choices organized around ownership.

## 3. Controlling Doctrine

The Statute of Frauds for conveyances of interests in land is dispositive on its face: no signed writing from Anna exists, and the oral promise is squarely within the statute. However, the equitable doctrine of part performance as articulated in *Neale* controls the result. The Supreme Court in *Neale* held that where a party, in reliance on an oral agreement, takes possession of land, makes permanent and valuable improvements, and alters her position so substantially that mere restitution of expenditures would be inadequate, equity will enforce the promise notwithstanding the absence of a writing. The rationale is that the Statute of Frauds, enacted to prevent fraud, must not itself become an instrument of fraud. Claire satisfies every element of *Neale*'s standard.

## 4. Formation/Consideration

Claire's acts constitute valid consideration, not mere conditions of a gratuitous gift. Anna's promise was explicitly bargained for: Claire was to marry, move onto the property, and occupy it. Claire's marriage, relocation, expenditure of $6,500 in personal savings, construction of improvements, and management of the property (including rent collection) constitute substantial detrimental reliance and bargained-for consideration. Even if characterized as a conditional gift, Claire's performance of the conditions coupled with Anna's acquiescence and encouragement transforms the arrangement into an enforceable equitable obligation. The expenditure of $6,500 — a significant sum representing Claire's own savings — and fifteen years of occupancy and management go far beyond a mere license.

## 5. Statute of Frauds Gates

The writing requirement triggered is the land-conveyance provision of the Statute of Frauds (derived from the English Statute of Frauds, 29 Car. II c. 3, § 4), requiring a memorandum signed by the party to be charged for any transfer of an interest in land. No trust instrument, deed, or signed memorandum from Anna exists. Part performance removes the bar because Claire can demonstrate all three traditional elements: (a) exclusive possession of the property for fifteen years; (b) valuable, permanent improvements ($6,500 in construction plus landscaping and utility work); and (c) acts referable uniquely to the alleged oral agreement rather than to any other relationship, since Claire's expenditures are explicable only by an understanding of ownership.

## 6. Exceptions / Promissory Estoppel

Under classical *Neale*-style part performance, Claire's claim is strong: possession, improvements, and change of position are all present and well-documented over fifteen years. Under modern promissory estoppel (Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 139), Claire also has a viable claim because Anna's promise was reasonably foreseeable to induce reliance, Claire relied to her substantial detriment, and injustice can be avoided only by enforcement. The classical part-performance theory is stronger here because it is specifically designed for land transactions, is well-established in equity, and leads directly to the remedy Claire seeks (specific performance or constructive trust). Promissory estoppel, while available, is sometimes limited to reliance damages rather than expectation relief, and some jurisdictions are reluctant to apply § 139 to grant specific performance of land contracts.

## 7. Defenses / Mistake

Defense (a) — Anna paid for some improvements: This actually strengthens Claire's case. Anna's financial participation corroborates the oral agreement and demonstrates Anna treated Claire as the beneficial owner. At most, it reduces the net amount of Claire's personal expenditure but does not negate the substantial improvements Claire independently funded. Defense (b) — Anna retained some rental income: Partial retention of rents during the earlier years is consistent with Anna's role as legal title holder managing the transition. It does not vitiate Claire's equitable claim, especially since Claire also collected and used rents with Anna's knowledge, demonstrating shared understanding of Claire's beneficial ownership. Defense (c) — Claire used some rentals for personal expenses: This is the weakest defense. The oral promise contained no express restriction on use of rental proceeds. Even if an implied condition existed, using some rental income for personal expenses is a trivial deviation that does not constitute a material breach sufficient to forfeit fifteen years of reliance and improvement. Equity abhors forfeiture, and no court following *Neale* would deny relief on this basis.

## 8. Strongest Counterargument for the Heirs

The heirs' strongest argument is that the oral arrangement was a revocable gratuitous license — not a contract — and that Anna's acts (retaining some rents, paying for some improvements, supervising work) demonstrate she never relinquished dominion, meaning Claire's possession was permissive and precarious rather than referable to an ownership agreement. To succeed, the heirs must prove at trial that Claire's occupancy and improvements are equally consistent with a family accommodation (a daughter living on her mother's land) as with an oral conveyance, thereby defeating the "unequivocal referability" requirement that some jurisdictions impose on part-performance claims. If the heirs can show that Claire's acts are ambiguous — explicable by the mother-daughter relationship rather than by an ownership promise — the part-performance exception collapses and the Statute of Frauds bars the claim.

## 9. Bounded Uncertainty and Practical Directions

Key doctrinal uncertainties include: (i) whether State X requires "unequivocal referability" of the acts of performance to the oral contract (a stricter standard than *Neale* itself, adopted in some jurisdictions, which could allow the heirs to argue Claire's acts are explained by familial generosity); (ii) whether the quantum of $6,500 in improvements qualifies as "substantial" given fifteen years of occupancy and potential appreciation; and (iii) whether State X recognizes constructive trusts based on oral promises or requires an independent basis in fraud or unjust enrichment beyond the broken promise itself.

Three concrete steps for Claire's counsel: First, secure testimony from witnesses (neighbors, contractors, the tenant) who can establish that Anna represented Claire as the owner and that the improvements were understood by all parties as incident to Claire's ownership — this defeats the referability challenge. Second, obtain appraisals and financial records documenting the current value of Claire's improvements relative to the property's total value, establishing that restitutionary damages would be grossly inadequate and that only specific performance achieves justice. Third, plead both constructive trust (grounded in unjust enrichment and Anna's fiduciary-like undertaking through Ben as trustee) and specific performance of the oral contract as alternative theories, ensuring that even if the court rejects part performance under a strict referability standard, the constructive trust claim survives as an independent equitable remedy not subject to the same Statute of Frauds limitations.

Claire's claim should succeed. The facts satisfy every element of the *Neale* part-performance doctrine. Damages under promissory estoppel are available as a fallback but are inferior because they cannot restore the bargained-for interest in land and would be difficult to calculate given fifteen years of intertwined expenditures.

## 3. Controlling Doctrine

The Statute of Frauds, specifically the land-conveyance provision requiring a signed writing by the party to be charged, is dispositive on its face and renders the oral promise unenforceable at law. However, the equitable doctrine of part performance as stated in *Neale* controls the result. The Supreme Court in *Neale* held that where acts of part performance are "unequivocally referable" to the alleged oral contract and the promisee would suffer irreparable fraud if the promise were not enforced, equity will decree specific performance notwithstanding the absence of a writing. The standard requires possession, substantial improvements referable to the contract, and change of position such that mere monetary restitution would be inadequate.

## 4. Formation/Consideration

Claire's acts constitute valid consideration, not merely conditions of a gratuitous gift. Anna's promise was explicitly bargained for: Claire was to marry, move onto the property, and occupy it. Claire's marriage, relocation, expenditure of $6,500 in personal savings for construction, collection and management of rental income, and fifteen years of maintenance constitute a classic bilateral exchange—Claire gave up alternative living arrangements and invested heavily in reliance on the promise. A court should characterize the marriage-plus-relocation-plus-improvement as both inducement and consideration, distinguishing this from a mere conditional gift because Anna received the tangible benefit of property improvement and management, and Claire suffered a detriment she would not have incurred absent the promise.

## 6. Exceptions / Promissory Estoppel

Under classical *Neale* part performance, Claire's claim is strong: possession, substantial improvements, and irreparable injury are all present. Under modern promissory estoppel (Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 139), Claire can also show a promise reasonably expected to induce action, actual substantial reliance, and injustice avoidable only by enforcement. The part-performance doctrine is stronger here because it directly yields specific performance of the land conveyance, whereas promissory estoppel under § 139 grants the court discretion to limit relief to reliance damages. Given that Claire seeks the land itself, the classical doctrine is both doctrinally cleaner and remedially superior.

Defense (a)—Anna paid for some improvements: This actually reinforces Claire's claim, as it shows Anna acknowledged the arrangement and invested in the property consistent with the oral promise. Defense (c)—Claire used some rentals for personal expenses: This is the weakest defense. The oral promise contained no express condition restricting rental use, and even if it did, de minimis or incidental personal use of rental income over fifteen years does not constitute a material breach sufficient to forfeit Claire's equity. ## 8. Strongest Counterargument for the Heirs

The strongest argument is that Claire's occupancy and improvements are explicable as a familial license—a mother permitting her married daughter to live on family property and improve it—rather than acts "unequivocally referable" to an oral contract of conveyance. ## 9. Bounded Uncertainty and Practical Directions

Key uncertainties include: whether State X requires all three *Neale* elements or accepts possession plus one; whether $6,500 in 1860s-equivalent dollars constitutes "substantial" improvement (likely yes, but jurisdictions vary on thresholds); whether Claire's failure to assert formal ownership during Anna's lifetime undermines the "unequivocal referability" requirement; and whether the oral trust direction to Ben creates a separate enforceable obligation under the constructive-trust doctrine independent of part performance.

Three concrete steps for Claire's counsel: First, secure testimony from the tenant, neighbors, and any surviving witnesses who heard Anna describe Claire as the owner or beneficiary, directly corroborating the oral promise and defeating the "familial license" characterization. # BENCH MEMO: Claire v. Heirs of Anna — Oral Promise to Convey Land

## 1. Jurisdiction Assumption

I assume State X follows traditional U.S. common-law equitable doctrine as articulated in *Neale v. Neales*, 76 U.S. (9 Wall.) 1 (1869), which recognized that part performance of an oral contract for the conveyance of land may take the case out of the Statute of Frauds where the acts of performance are unequivocally referable to the alleged agreement and where enforcement is necessary to prevent fraud. This choice matters because *Neale* represents the classical federal articulation of the part-performance exception—requiring possession, valuable improvements, and conduct explicable only by reference to the oral agreement. Some modern jurisdictions have expanded this through Restatement-based promissory estoppel (Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 139), which lowers the threshold. I assume State X has not legislatively abolished the equitable part-performance exception, as most states have not.

## 2. Bottom-Line Outcome

Claire's claim should succeed. The recommended remedy is imposition of a constructive trust on the property in Claire's favor, with the court ordering the heirs to convey legal title. Alternatively, specific performance of the oral promise is warranted. A constructive trust is the cleaner vehicle because it avoids the conceptual difficulty of specifically enforcing a contract that Anna could have performed only during her lifetime; equity simply declares that the heirs hold title as constructive trustees. Damages under promissory estoppel serve as an adequate fallback but would undercompensate Claire given fifteen years of possession, improvements, and reliance.

## 3. Controlling Doctrine

The Statute of Frauds—specifically the land-conveyance provision requiring a signed writing—is dispositive on its face and would bar Claire's claim absent an equitable exception. The controlling equitable doctrine is part performance. Under *Neale*, the Supreme Court held that where a party, in reliance on an oral agreement to convey land, takes possession, makes valuable and permanent improvements, and the circumstances are such that no other explanation for the conduct is plausible, equity will enforce the agreement to prevent the statute itself from becoming an instrument of fraud. The standard requires acts that are (a) exclusively referable to the oral contract, (b) substantial and irreversible, and (c) performed with the knowledge and acquiescence of the promisor.

## 4. Formation/Consideration

Claire's acts constitute valid consideration, not merely conditions of a gratuitous promise. Anna's promise was expressly conditioned on Claire's marriage and relocation—both of which impose legal detriment (change of residence, assumption of marital obligations altering legal status). More significantly, Claire's expenditure of $6,500 in personal savings for permanent improvements constitutes classic bargained-for exchange or, at minimum, detrimental reliance that transforms what might appear to be a conditional gift into an enforceable bilateral arrangement. A court should characterize Claire's marriage-and-move as the inducing consideration and her improvements as additional performance confirming the bargain's existence. Even if characterized as a conditional gift, the substantial reliance expenditures trigger equitable enforcement under *Neale*.

## 5. Statute of Frauds Gates

Two SoF categories are triggered: the land-conveyance provision (requiring a writing signed by the party to be charged for any transfer of an interest in real property) and potentially the one-year provision if the agreement contemplated performance over an indefinite period, though the latter is likely inapplicable since the agreement was capable of full performance within one year. The land-conveyance writing requirement is the dispositive bar. Part performance removes this bar because Claire satisfies all three traditional indicia recognized in *Neale*: (1) she took exclusive possession of the property; (2) she made substantial, permanent, and valuable improvements ($6,500 in construction plus landscaping and utility work); and (3) these acts were performed with Anna's full knowledge, acquiescence, and even active encouragement. The improvements are unequivocally referable to the oral promise—no rational person would spend $6,500 improving another's property absent an agreement to receive title.

## 6. Exceptions / Promissory Estoppel

Under classical *Neale*-style part performance, Claire's claim is strong: possession plus substantial improvements plus the promisor's knowledge satisfies the tripartite test. Under modern promissory estoppel (Restatement § 139), Claire must show a promise that the promisor should reasonably have expected to induce action, actual reliance, and injustice avoidable only by enforcement. Both routes reach Claire's claim, but classical part performance is the stronger theory here because the facts map precisely onto *Neale*'s framework—fifteen years of open, notorious possession with major capital improvements. Promissory estoppel is the useful alternative because it does not require that the acts be "unequivocally referable" to the contract, a standard some jurisdictions apply stringently. Claire's counsel should plead both.

## 7. Defenses / Mistake

Defense (a)—Anna paid for some improvements: This actually strengthens Claire's claim by demonstrating Anna's recognition of the arrangement. Anna's contributions are consistent with a mother assisting a daughter who holds equitable title, not with Anna retaining ownership. Defense (b)—Anna retained some rental income: If Anna occasionally collected rents, this could suggest she retained a possessory or ownership interest. However, the facts show Claire primarily collected and used rental income, and any retention by Anna is equally consistent with Anna exercising oversight as informal trustee or simply receiving reimbursement. This defense is weak absent evidence of a systematic pattern inconsistent with Claire's equitable ownership. Defense (c)—Claire used rentals for personal expenses: This is the weakest defense. The oral promise contained no express restriction on rental-income use, and even if it did, using some rental proceeds for personal expenses is a minor deviation that does not constitute a material breach warranting forfeiture of fifteen years of reliance and investment. Equity abhors forfeiture, and no court applying *Neale* principles would deny relief on this basis.

## 8. Strongest Counterargument for the Heirs

The heirs' best argument is that the oral arrangement was not a contract at all but an unenforceable gratuitous license—Anna permitted Claire to live on the property and improve it as a family accommodation, with no intent to create a binding obligation to convey title. To succeed, the heirs must prove at trial that Claire's acts of possession and improvement are equally consistent with a rent-free family living arrangement as with an oral contract, thereby defeating the "unequivocal referability" requirement under *Neale*. Specifically, they would need evidence that other family members received similar accommodations without promises of title, that Claire acknowledged she had no ownership expectation (in correspondence, tax filings, or testimony), or that the improvements were modest enough to be consistent with a licensee beautifying her temporary home. The $6,500 expenditure and fifteen-year duration make this argument difficult but not impossible.

## 9. Bounded Uncertainty and Practical Directions

Key doctrinal uncertainties include: (i) whether State X requires all three *Neale* elements or treats possession alone as sufficient (a minority position); (ii) how "substantial" improvements must be relative to property value—if the lot was worth $100,000, $6,500 may seem modest; (iii) whether State X permits constructive trusts based on oral promises or requires an independent basis such as unjust enrichment; and (iv) whether Ben's failure to act as trustee creates a separate basis for relief under resulting-trust doctrine.

Three concrete steps for Claire's counsel: First, secure and present detailed evidence of the $6,500 expenditure through receipts, contractor testimony, bank records, and before-and-after property appraisals to establish that improvements were permanent, substantial, and enhanced the property's market value well beyond the cash outlay. Second, obtain testimony from witnesses—neighbors, the tenant, family members—who heard Anna acknowledge the promise or describe Claire as the property's owner, thereby corroborating the oral agreement's existence and defeating the "mere license" characterization. Third, plead alternative theories in the complaint—specific performance via part performance, constructive trust, resulting trust (based on Claire's capital contributions), and promissory estoppel for damages—to preserve all avenues and ensure that even if the court rejects one equitable theory, others remain available.

Defense (a): Anna paid for some improvements. This does not defeat Claire's equity; it reinforces that Anna treated Claire as the beneficial owner and acted consistently with the oral promise. At most it reduces the quantum of Claire's out-of-pocket reliance, but *Neale* does not require that the claimant fund every improvement alone. Defense (b): Anna retained some rental income. Occasional retention of rents by the legal titleholder is consistent with a trust arrangement and does not negate Claire's possession or beneficial ownership. Defense (c): Claire used some rentals for personal expenses. This is the weakest defense. The oral promise contained no express restriction on rental use, and even if it did, diversion of modest rental income is not a material breach sufficient to forfeit fifteen years of reliance and improvement. Equity would treat this as de minimis or subject to an accounting, not forfeiture. None of these defenses is likely to defeat Claire's claim.

## 8. Strongest Counterargument for the Heirs

The heirs' best argument is that Claire's acts are referable to a gratuitous family license or informal living arrangement rather than to an enforceable oral contract — that is, the acts fail the "unequivocal referability" test. If the heirs can prove at trial that similarly situated family members routinely made improvements to Anna's properties without any expectation of ownership, or that Claire acknowledged she held no ownership interest (e.g., in tax filings, insurance applications, or statements to third parties), the court could find that possession and improvements are equally consistent with a family accommodation as with an oral conveyance, thereby failing to take the case outside the Statute of Frauds.

## 9. Bounded Uncertainty and Practical Directions

Key uncertainties include: whether State X requires all three *Neale* elements or treats possession alone as sufficient; whether "substantial" improvements require a threshold ratio to land value; whether the oral direction to Ben as trustee creates a separate oral-trust theory (some states have a Statute of Frauds for express trusts); and whether promissory estoppel in State X permits specific performance or only damages.

Three concrete steps for Claire's counsel: First, secure testimony from disinterested witnesses (the tenant, neighbors, contractors) confirming that Anna represented Claire as the owner or beneficial holder and that improvements were made pursuant to the promise, establishing unequivocal referability. Second, compile documentary evidence — receipts, permits, bank records — quantifying Claire's $6,500 expenditure and demonstrating that improvements are permanent and inseparable from the realty, foreclosing any argument that money damages are adequate. Third, plead both part performance and constructive trust as alternative equitable theories, and plead promissory estoppel as a fallback legal claim, preserving all avenues in case the court applies a narrower part-performance standard than *Neale*.

# BENCH MEMO: CLAIRE v. HEIRS OF ANNA — ORAL PROMISE TO CONVEY LAND

**1. Jurisdiction Assumption**

This analysis assumes State X follows traditional U.S. common-law equitable principles as articulated in *Neale v. Neales*, 76 U.S. (9 Wall.) 1 (1869), which recognized that equity will enforce an oral agreement to convey land, notwithstanding the Statute of Frauds, where the promisee has partly performed by taking possession, making valuable and permanent improvements, and acting in reliance on the oral promise. I assume State X has not enacted a statutory abolition of the part-performance exception and follows the majority rule that part performance must render the promisee's position unjust to restore through money damages alone. This choice matters because some jurisdictions require all three elements (possession, payment of consideration, and permanent improvements) while others accept a subset; the *Neale* framework demands the confluence of acts that are "unequivocally referable" to the oral contract and that make rescission unconscionable.

**2. Bottom-Line Outcome**

Claire's claim should succeed. The recommended remedy is specific performance compelling the heirs to convey the lot and buildings to Claire, or in the alternative, imposition of a constructive trust over the property in Claire's favor. The facts — fifteen years of exclusive possession, $6,500 in personal savings expended on permanent improvements, marriage undertaken in reliance, and Anna's knowledge and encouragement — present a textbook part-performance case under *Neale*. Damages under promissory estoppel serve as a viable fallback but are inferior given the uniqueness of real property.

**3. Controlling Doctrine**

The Statute of Frauds, requiring a writing signed by the party to be charged for any conveyance of an interest in land, is dispositive on its face and would bar Claire's claim if applied mechanically. However, the equitable doctrine of part performance, as articulated in *Neale*, controls the result. The Supreme Court in *Neale* held that where a party has been induced by an oral promise to take possession, expend money in lasting improvements, and alter her position so substantially that mere restitution of money would be inadequate, equity will decree specific performance to prevent the Statute of Frauds from becoming an instrument of fraud. The legal standard requires acts of reliance that are unequivocally referable to the oral agreement and that would cause irreparable injury if the promise were repudiated.

**4. Formation/Consideration**

Claire's acts constitute valid consideration, not merely the satisfaction of a condition precedent to a gratuitous gift. Anna's promise was explicitly bargained for: Claire was to marry, relocate onto the property, and maintain it. Claire's marriage, her expenditure of $6,500 in savings, her labor in improvements, and her management of the tenancy all constitute legal detriment undertaken at Anna's request. A court should characterize Claire's marriage and substantial expenditures as consideration supporting an enforceable bilateral agreement rather than a mere conditional gift, because Anna's promise induced Claire to change her legal and financial position irreversibly. Even if characterized as a conditional gift, the reliance element independently supports equitable enforcement under part-performance doctrine.

**5. Statute of Frauds Gates**

Two SoF categories are triggered: the requirement of a writing for conveyances of interests in land and potentially the requirement for declarations of trust. The land-conveyance writing requirement is the dispositive bar. Part performance removes this bar because Claire satisfies all three traditional elements recognized in *Neale*: (i) taking and maintaining exclusive possession for fifteen years, (ii) making substantial, permanent, and valuable improvements with her own funds, and (iii) acting in reliance on the specific oral promise. These acts are unequivocally referable to the alleged oral agreement and inexplicable as mere tenant or licensee conduct.

**6. Exceptions / Promissory Estoppel**

Under classical *Neale*-style part performance, Claire's claim is strong: possession plus permanent improvements plus reliance satisfies the traditional test. Under modern promissory estoppel (Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 139), Claire also prevails because Anna's promise was clear, reliance was reasonable and foreseeable, and injustice can be avoided only by enforcement. The part-performance doctrine is the stronger vehicle because it directly yields specific performance as a remedy, whereas promissory estoppel in some jurisdictions limits relief to reliance damages. Claire's counsel should plead both but lead with part performance.

**7. Defenses / Mistake**

Defense (a) — that Anna paid for some improvements — is unlikely to defeat the claim because Claire independently spent $6,500 of her own savings; Anna's additional contributions are consistent with her role as donor/promisor and actually corroborate the oral agreement's existence. Defense (b) — Anna retained some rental income — similarly corroborates Anna's continuing involvement but does not negate Claire's possession or improvements; at most it creates an accounting issue. Defense (c) — Claire's diversion of some rental income to personal expenses — fails because no evidence suggests this was a material condition of the oral promise, and minor deviations in the use of incidental rental income over fifteen years do not constitute the kind of breach that would forfeit equitable relief. None of these defenses is likely to defeat Claire's equity claim.

**8. Strongest Counterargument for the Heirs**

The heirs' best argument is that Claire's acts are referable to a gratuitous family license rather than an oral contract — that Anna simply allowed her daughter to live on and improve the property as a permissive occupant, and that no enforceable agreement ever existed. To prevail, the heirs must prove at trial that Anna never manifested contractual intent, that Claire's occupancy was understood by both parties as precarious and revocable, and that the improvements were voluntary gifts to the property rather than bargained-for reliance. They would need testimony or documentary evidence showing Anna treated the arrangement as informal hospitality rather than a binding commitment, and that Claire's expenditures were made without any expectation of ownership.

**9. Bounded Uncertainty and Practical Directions**

Key uncertainties include: whether State X requires all three part-performance elements or accepts possession plus improvements alone; whether $6,500 in 1850s-era dollars (or the relevant period) constitutes "substantial" improvement; whether Claire's receipt and use of rents strengthens or weakens her claim to exclusive possession; and whether the oral direction to Ben as trustee creates a separate constructive-trust theory independent of part performance.

Three concrete steps for Claire's counsel: First, secure and present detailed evidence of every dollar Claire spent on improvements — receipts, contractor testimony, building permits, and before-and-after valuations — to establish the quantum of irreversible reliance beyond dispute. Second, obtain testimony from neighbors, the tenant, and any surviving witnesses that Anna consistently represented Claire as the owner or intended owner of the property, thereby establishing that Claire's acts were unequivocally referable to the oral promise rather than a mere license. Third, plead alternative theories — specific performance under part performance, constructive trust based on Anna's oral direction to Ben as trustee, and promissory estoppel for damages — to preserve all avenues of relief and ensure that even if the court rejects one theory, the equitable claim survives under another.

# BENCH MEMO: Claire v. Heirs of Anna — Oral Promise to Convey Land

## 1. Jurisdiction Assumption

I assume State X follows traditional U.S. common-law equitable doctrine as articulated in *Neale v. Neales*, 76 U.S. (9 Wall.) 1 (1869), which holds that an oral agreement concerning land, otherwise barred by the Statute of Frauds, may be specifically enforced in equity where the claimant demonstrates part performance consisting of possession, payment of consideration, and valuable improvements made in reliance on the promise. This choice matters because *Neale* sets a relatively claimant-friendly standard: it does not require all three elements in rigid sequence but treats them as cumulative indicia of an enforceable oral agreement that equity will not allow the Statute of Frauds to be used as an instrument of fraud. If State X had adopted a narrower rule—requiring, for instance, that possession alone never suffices or that only written trusts are cognizable—the analysis could shift. I assume no such departure.

## 2. Bottom-Line Outcome

Claire's claim should succeed. The recommended remedy is imposition of a constructive trust on the property in Claire's favor, with an order directing the heirs to convey legal title. Specific performance of the oral promise is the functional equivalent. If a court were reluctant to grant equitable title, damages under promissory estoppel would be the fallback, but on these facts the equitable remedy is both appropriate and superior because money damages cannot adequately replicate fifteen years of possession, improvement, and reliance.

## 3. Controlling Doctrine

The Statute of Frauds, requiring a signed writing for conveyances of interests in land, is dispositive on its face and would bar Claire's claim absent an equitable exception. The controlling exception is the doctrine of part performance as applied in *Neale*. The Supreme Court there held that where a party, in reliance on an oral agreement, takes possession of land, pays valuable consideration, and makes lasting and valuable improvements, enforcing the Statute of Frauds would itself perpetrate a fraud. The oral agreement is therefore taken out of the statute and enforced in equity. Claire's acts—marriage, entry into possession, expenditure of $6,500 in personal savings on permanent improvements, and fifteen years of occupancy—satisfy each prong of the *Neale* standard.

## 4. Formation and Consideration

Claire's acts constitute valid consideration, not merely conditions of a gratuitous gift. Anna's promise was bargained-for: Claire married, relocated onto the property, and expended substantial personal funds on improvements—acts she would not have undertaken but for the promise. Marriage has long been recognized as valuable consideration in Anglo-American law, and the expenditure of $6,500 in savings on permanent structures goes well beyond a donee's passive receipt of a gift. A court should characterize Claire's marriage-and-improvement as classic bilateral consideration that transformed Anna's promise from a revocable conditional gift into an enforceable contract. Even if characterized as a unilateral contract (promise for performance), Claire fully performed.

## 5. Statute of Frauds Gates

Two SoF categories are triggered: the land-conveyance provision and, potentially, the provision governing the creation of express trusts in land (Anna directed Ben to hold as trustee). Both require a signed writing. The dispositive barrier is the land-conveyance writing requirement. Part performance removes that bar because Claire can show the classic triad recognized in *Neale*: (i) exclusive possession for fifteen years; (ii) payment of valuable consideration through personal funds; and (iii) permanent, substantial improvements visible on the land. These acts are "unequivocally referable" to the oral agreement and render it inequitable to invoke the statute.

## 6. Exceptions — Part Performance vs. Promissory Estoppel

Under classical *Neale*-style part performance, Claire's claim is strong: possession plus improvements plus consideration is the paradigm case. Under modern promissory estoppel (Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 139), Claire also prevails because Anna's promise was reasonably foreseeable as inducing reliance, Claire relied to her substantial detriment, and injustice can be avoided only by enforcement. Part performance is the stronger theory here because it directly yields specific performance and is well-established for land cases; promissory estoppel in some jurisdictions limits relief to reliance damages rather than expectation. Claire's counsel should plead both but lead with part performance.

## 7. Defenses

(a) Anna paid for some improvements: This does not defeat Claire's claim; it actually reinforces the existence of the agreement, since Anna's participation is consistent with her role as the promisor facilitating the arrangement. At most it reduces the quantum of Claire's out-of-pocket reliance, but it does not negate possession or Claire's own $6,500 expenditure. (b) Anna retained some rental income: Retention of rents by Anna during her lifetime is consistent with Anna holding legal title as constructive trustee; it does not disprove Claire's equitable interest and may reflect Anna's understanding she remained legal owner pending formal conveyance. (c) Claire used some rentals for personal expenses: This is not a material breach of any condition. The oral promise contained no express restriction on rental use, and even if it did, de minimis personal use over fifteen years does not constitute the kind of breach warranting forfeiture of an equitable interest. Equity abhors forfeitures, and a court would likely find this immaterial.

## 8. Strongest Counterargument for the Heirs

The heirs' best argument is that Anna's statement was a revocable, gratuitous expression of intent—not a binding contract—and that Claire's improvements were those of a permissive licensee, not a contract purchaser. If the heirs can prove at trial that Anna retained dominion and control (collecting rents, directing improvements, paying for construction), that Claire never treated herself as owner (never paid taxes, never insured the property in her name), and that the "promise" lacked definite terms (no specific parcel description, no timeline), a court could conclude the acts of part performance are equivocally referable to a family license rather than a contract, thus failing the *Neale* standard.

## 9. Bounded Uncertainty and Practical Directions

Key uncertainties include: whether State X requires all three *Neale* elements or treats them as flexible factors; whether "substantial" improvement has a monetary threshold relative to land value; whether Claire's failure to pay property taxes undermines her possession claim; and whether State X recognizes promissory estoppel as independently sufficient to overcome the SoF for land (some jurisdictions do not).

Claire's counsel should take three concrete steps. First, secure and present documentary evidence of Claire's $6,500 expenditure—receipts, bank records, contractor testimony—and obtain appraisals showing the improvements' current value to demonstrate substantiality beyond dispute. Second, gather testimony from the tenant, neighbors, and Ben himself establishing that Claire exercised exclusive dominion over the property for fifteen years, including maintenance decisions, tenant selection, and tax or insurance payments if any, to establish unequivocal possession referable to ownership. Third, plead both part performance and promissory estoppel as alternative equitable theories, and request in the alternative a constructive trust, to give the court maximum doctrinal flexibility regardless of which exception it recognizes.

Claire's claim should succeed. The recommended remedy is imposition of a constructive trust on the property in Claire's favor, with an order directing the heirs to convey legal title to her. Specific performance of the oral promise is the functional equivalent and equally appropriate. A damages-only remedy under promissory estoppel is inadequate given the nature of the improvements and Claire's fifteen years of possession; equity demands the land-based remedy.

## 3. Controlling Doctrine

The Statute of Frauds facially bars enforcement of Anna's oral promise because it involves a transfer of an interest in land without a signed writing. However, the equitable doctrine of part performance, as applied in *Neale*, controls the result. In *Neale*, the Supreme Court held that where a party, in reliance on an oral agreement, takes possession of land and makes valuable, permanent improvements with the knowledge and acquiescence of the promisor, equity will enforce the agreement notwithstanding the absence of a writing, because to permit the promisor (or her successors) to invoke the statute would be to use it as an instrument of fraud rather than a shield against it. Claire's fifteen years of possession, $6,500 in improvements (substantial for the era/context), construction of outbuildings, and landscaping/utility work satisfy this standard.

## 4. Formation/Consideration

Claire's acts constitute valid consideration, not merely conditions of a gratuitous gift. Anna's promise was explicitly bargained for: Claire was to marry and move onto the property, and she did both. The marriage itself, as an act of forbearance and life-altering commitment undertaken at Anna's inducement, constitutes classic consideration (consistent with traditional doctrine treating marriage settlements seriously). Claire's subsequent expenditure of $6,500 in personal savings on permanent improvements further cements the bargain character. The writing requirement is triggered under the land-conveyance provision of the Statute of Frauds (the classic "Section 4" category requiring a memorandum signed by the party to be charged for contracts transferring interests in real property). The combination is dispositive under *Neale* because permitting the heirs to retain both the land and Claire's improvements would constitute the very fraud the Statute was never intended to facilitate.

Under classical *Neale*-style part performance, Claire's claim is strong: possession plus substantial improvements plus the promisor's knowledge and acquiescence form the canonical trio. Under modern promissory estoppel (Restatement § 139), Claire must show a promise foreseeably inducing reliance, actual substantial reliance, and injustice avoidable only by enforcement. Defense (a): Anna paid for some improvements. This does not defeat Claire's claim; it reinforces that Anna treated Claire as the beneficial owner and acted consistently with the oral promise. Occasional retention of rents by the legal title holder is consistent with Anna's role as nominal titleholder/trustee and does not establish that Claire lacked possessory rights. Defense (c): Claire used some rentals for personal expenses. This is the weakest defense. The oral promise contained no express condition restricting rental use, and even if it did, minor diversions over fifteen years do not constitute a material breach sufficient to forfeit Claire's entire equitable interest, especially given the doctrine of substantial performance and the disproportionate forfeiture that would result. None of these defenses is likely to defeat Claire's equity claim.

The heirs' best argument is that Anna's promise was a conditional gift (not a contract), that Claire's improvements were made as a licensee with no enforceable agreement, and that the acts of "part performance" are equally referable to a family living arrangement as to an oral land contract—thus failing the exclusivity-of-reference requirement some jurisdictions impose. To succeed, the heirs must prove at trial that Claire's possession and improvements are reasonably explicable by the mother-daughter relationship and informal family accommodation rather than by a specific oral promise to convey, thereby defeating the evidentiary function of the part-performance doctrine.

## 9. Bounded Uncertainty and Practical Directions

Key uncertainties include: whether State X requires strict "exclusive referability" of the acts to the oral contract (some jurisdictions have relaxed this); whether $6,500 in improvements qualifies as "substantial" relative to the property's value; whether Claire's possession was sufficiently "exclusive" given Anna's occasional supervisory involvement; and whether State X permits specific performance under promissory estoppel or limits it to reliance damages.

Three concrete steps for Claire's counsel: First, secure testimony from disinterested witnesses (the tenant, neighbors, contractors) who can corroborate Anna's oral promise and Claire's exclusive possession, satisfying the evidentiary function underlying the part-performance exception. Second, obtain an appraisal establishing that Claire's $6,500 expenditure and labor resulted in improvements whose current value is substantial relative to the unimproved lot, making restitution inadequate and specific performance necessary. Defense (b): Anna's retention of some rental income is similarly consistent with Anna holding legal title as promised (with Ben as nominal trustee) and exercising residual management rights during her lifetime. It does not negate the conveyance promise. Defense (c): Claire's use of some rental income for personal expenses is not a material breach of any condition. The oral promise contained no restriction on rental-income use; even if it did, the doctrine of substantial performance and the disproportionality between fifteen years of faithful occupancy and minor personal expenditures would prevent forfeiture. None of these defenses is likely to defeat Claire's equity claim.

## 8. Strongest Counterargument for the Heirs

The heirs' strongest argument is that Claire's acts are not "unequivocally referable" to an oral contract to convey but are equally consistent with a revocable license to occupy family property—a common intra-family arrangement. If the heirs can prove at trial that Anna retained dominion (collecting rents, supervising work, paying for improvements), they can argue Claire's possession was permissive and dependent, not contractual. To succeed, the heirs must demonstrate that Anna exercised control inconsistent with a completed conveyance and that Claire's improvements were modest enough to be compensable in damages, negating the inadequacy-of-legal-remedy predicate for specific performance.

## 9. Bounded Uncertainty and Practical Directions

Key uncertainties include: whether State X requires all three *Neale* elements or treats possession plus improvements as sufficient; whether "substantial" improvements are measured absolutely or relative to the property's value; whether the oral direction to Ben as trustee creates an independent express-trust claim subject to its own SoF rules; and whether State X has adopted § 139 promissory estoppel as an independent SoF exception. Claire's counsel should take three concrete steps: First, secure and present documentary evidence (receipts, contractor testimony, photographs, tax records) quantifying Claire's $6,500 expenditure and demonstrating the permanent, substantial character of improvements relative to the property's pre-improvement value. Second, obtain testimony from neighbors, the tenant, and any surviving witnesses to Anna's oral statements acknowledging Claire's ownership interest, thereby establishing the unequivocal referability of Claire's possession to the oral contract rather than a mere license. Third, plead in the alternative—specific performance under part performance, constructive trust under unjust enrichment, and damages under promissory estoppel—to preserve all remedial avenues and ensure that even if the court rejects one theory, Claire retains a path to relief under the others.

# BENCH MEMO: CLAIRE v. HEIRS OF ANNA — ORAL PROMISE TO CONVEY LAND

**1. Jurisdiction Assumption**

This analysis assumes State X follows traditional U.S. common-law equitable principles as articulated in *Neale v. Neales*, 76 U.S. (9 Wall.) 1 (1869), which held that an oral agreement to convey land, otherwise barred by the Statute of Frauds, may be specifically enforced in equity where the promisee has partly performed by taking possession, making valuable and permanent improvements, and changing position in reliance on the promise. I assume State X has not enacted a statutory abrogation of the part-performance doctrine and follows the majority rule that part performance must consist of acts unequivocally referable to the oral agreement—not merely consistent with some other explanation. This choice matters because jurisdictions vary on whether possession alone suffices or whether substantial improvements are additionally required; under *Neale*, the Supreme Court demanded the combination of possession, improvements, and reliance-based change of position, which is the most analytically complete framework and favorable to Claire.

**2. Bottom-Line Outcome**

Claire's claim should succeed. The recommended remedy is imposition of a constructive trust on the property in Claire's favor, with an order directing the heirs to execute and deliver a deed. Specific performance of the oral promise is the functionally equivalent alternative. If for any reason equity declines specific performance, damages measured by Claire's full reliance expenditures plus the enhanced value of improvements should be awarded under promissory estoppel. The constructive trust is preferable because it vindicates the oral trust arrangement Anna established and avoids the unjust enrichment that would otherwise accrue to the heirs.

**3. Controlling Doctrine**

The Statute of Frauds, requiring a signed writing for transfers of interests in land, is dispositive on its face and would bar enforcement of the oral promise standing alone. However, the equitable doctrine of part performance, as crystallized in *Neale*, controls the result. In *Neale*, the Court held that where a party, in reliance on an oral agreement, takes possession of land, makes substantial and permanent improvements, and materially changes position such that restoration to the status quo ante is impossible, equity will enforce the oral agreement notwithstanding the absence of a writing. The rationale is that the Statute of Frauds, designed to prevent fraud, must not itself become an instrument of fraud. Claire's fifteen years of possession, $6,500 in improvements (substantial for the era-equivalent value), construction of multiple structures, and marriage undertaken in reliance satisfy every element *Neale* requires.

**4. Formation/Consideration**

Claire's acts constitute valid consideration, not merely conditions of a gratuitous gift. Anna's promise was explicitly bargained for: Claire was to marry, move onto the property, and occupy it. Claire's marriage, relocation, and expenditure of $6,500 in personal savings on permanent improvements constitute legal detriment and bargained-for exchange. Even if characterized as a conditional gift, Claire's substantial improvements transform the arrangement into one equity will enforce because the improvements were induced by and referable to the promise. A court should characterize Claire's marriage and move as conditions precedent that she fully performed, and her expenditures as both consideration and detrimental reliance.

**5. Statute of Frauds Gates**

Two SoF categories are triggered: the requirement of a writing for conveyances of interests in land and, potentially, the provision governing agreements not performable within one year (given the indefinite duration). The land-conveyance provision is dispositive. Part performance removes the bar because Claire can demonstrate all three traditional elements: (i) actual, exclusive, continuous possession for fifteen years; (ii) substantial, permanent improvements ($6,500 in construction plus landscaping and utility work); and (iii) payment or expenditure referable solely to the oral agreement. These acts are "unequivocally referable" to an agreement to convey, not explicable as mere tenancy or license.

**6. Exceptions / Promissory Estoppel**

Under classical *Neale*-style part performance, Claire's claim is strong: possession plus permanent improvements plus reliance constitutes the paradigmatic case. Under modern promissory estoppel (Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 139), Claire also prevails because Anna's promise was clear, reliance was foreseeable, Claire's reliance was actual and substantial, and injustice can be avoided only by enforcement. The part-performance doctrine is stronger here because it directly yields specific performance or a constructive trust—the remedy Claire most needs—whereas promissory estoppel in some jurisdictions limits relief to reliance damages. Counsel should plead both but lead with part performance.

**7. Defenses / Mistake**

Defense (a)—Anna paid for some improvements—does not defeat the claim; Anna's contributions are consistent with her role as the property's titleholder acting in furtherance of the oral arrangement and, if anything, corroborate the agreement's existence. Defense (b)—Anna retained some rental income—similarly fails because Anna held legal title and her retention of some rents is consistent with a trustee-like arrangement; moreover, Claire also collected and used rents, demonstrating possessory dominion. Defense (c)—Claire used some rentals for personal expenses—is the weakest; the oral promise contained no express restriction on rental use, and even if it did, minor diversion of rents over fifteen years does not constitute a material breach sufficient to forfeit Claire's equity in the property. Equity abhors forfeiture, and none of these defenses should defeat Claire's claim.

**8. Strongest Counterargument for the Heirs**

The heirs' best argument is that Claire's possession and improvements are equally consistent with a revocable license or family accommodation rather than an enforceable agreement to convey, and therefore her acts are not "unequivocally referable" to an oral contract. To succeed, the heirs must prove at trial that Anna retained dominion and control inconsistent with a conveyance—for example, that Anna collected the majority of rents, directed improvements as owner, treated the property as her own on tax returns, or told third parties she had not given the land to Claire. If the heirs can establish that Claire's occupancy looked indistinguishable from a permissive family arrangement, the unequivocal-referability requirement fails and the Statute of Frauds bar stands.

**9. Bounded Uncertainty and Practical Directions**

Key uncertainties include: (i) whether State X requires all three part-performance elements or treats possession plus one additional element as sufficient; (ii) the quantum of improvement that qualifies as "substantial" (some courts require improvements disproportionate to rental value); (iii) whether the oral direction to Ben as trustee creates a separate basis for relief under an oral trust theory, which some jurisdictions recognize independently of part performance; and (iv) whether Claire's collection of rents constitutes sufficient "acts of ownership" or undermines her claim by suggesting a tenancy.

Claire's counsel should take three concrete steps. First, secure and present detailed evidence of every improvement—receipts, contractor testimony, before-and-after photographs, and an appraiser's testimony on current enhanced value—to establish that the improvements are permanent, substantial, and referable only to an ownership interest. Second, obtain testimony from neighbors, the tenant, and any surviving witnesses that Anna repeatedly acknowledged Claire as the beneficial owner and that Claire's occupancy was understood in the community as ownership, not license—this directly rebuts the unequivocal-referability challenge. Third, plead both constructive trust and promissory estoppel as alternative theories, ensuring that even if the court applies a narrow part-performance standard, Claire preserves a damages remedy under § 139 estoppel principles, and move for discovery on Anna's tax filings and any correspondence referencing the arrangement to establish documentary corroboration short of a signed conveyance.

# BENCH MEMO: Claire v. Heirs of Anna — Oral Promise to Convey Land

## 1. Jurisdiction Assumption

This analysis assumes State X follows traditional U.S. common-law equitable principles as articulated in *Neale v. Neales*, 76 U.S. (9 Wall.) 1 (1869), which held that an oral agreement to convey land, otherwise barred by the Statute of Frauds, may be specifically enforced in equity where the promisee has taken possession, made valuable and substantial improvements, and changed position in reliance on the promise. I assume State X has not enacted a statutory abrogation of the part-performance doctrine and follows the majority American rule that possession plus substantial improvements (not possession alone) suffices to remove the Statute of Frauds bar. This choice matters because a minority of jurisdictions require all three traditional elements — possession, payment of consideration, and valuable improvements — while others accept possession plus any one additional act of reliance. The strength of Claire's claim varies accordingly.

## 2. Bottom-Line Outcome

Claire's claim should succeed. The recommended remedy is imposition of a constructive trust on the property in Claire's favor, with an order directing the heirs to convey legal title. Specific performance of the oral promise is functionally equivalent here and equally appropriate. If the court finds any equitable impediment to full conveyance, damages under promissory estoppel provide a viable alternative, but the facts strongly favor the in-kind remedy.

## 3. Controlling Doctrine

The Statute of Frauds, applicable to agreements for the transfer of interests in land, is dispositive on its face: no signed writing exists. However, the equitable doctrine of part performance, as set forth in *Neale*, controls the result. The Supreme Court in *Neale* held that where a party, in reliance on an oral agreement, takes possession of land and makes permanent, valuable improvements with the knowledge and acquiescence of the promisor, enforcing the Statute of Frauds would itself perpetrate a fraud. The acts of reliance must be "unequivocally referable" to the oral agreement and of such character that the party cannot be restored to the status quo ante by monetary compensation alone. Claire's fifteen years of possession, $6,500 in improvements (substantial for the era and context), construction of outbuildings, and management of the property satisfy this standard.

## 4. Formation/Consideration

Claire's acts constitute valid consideration, not merely conditions of a gratuitous gift. Anna's promise was expressly conditioned on Claire's marriage and relocation — acts that imposed real legal detriment (changing domicile, foregoing alternative living arrangements, committing personal savings). The $6,500 expenditure and ongoing improvement work represent bargained-for exchange, not mere donative conditions, because Anna's promise induced Claire's performance and Anna received the benefit of enhanced property value. A court should characterize Claire's marriage-and-move as the inducing consideration and the improvements as further executed consideration, distinguishing this from a revocable license or conditional gift where the promisee incurs no substantial detriment.

## 5. Statute of Frauds Gates

The relevant SoF provision requires a writing signed by the party to be charged (Anna) for any contract for the sale or transfer of an interest in land. The oral trust direction to Ben likewise fails under the Statute of Frauds applicable to express trusts in land in most jurisdictions. The dispositive aspect is the complete absence of any writing. Part performance removes the bar because Claire can demonstrate all elements recognized in *Neale*: (a) actual, exclusive possession for fifteen years; (b) substantial, permanent improvements; and (c) reliance that cannot be adequately compensated by damages alone, since the improvements are integrated into the realty and Claire's life was restructured around the property.

## 6. Exceptions / Promissory Estoppel

Under classical *Neale*-style part performance, Claire's claim is strong: possession, improvements, and unequivocal referability to the oral promise are all present. Under modern promissory estoppel (Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 139), Claire must show a promise reasonably expected to induce action, actual reliance, and injustice avoidable only by enforcement. Both routes reach the same result, but classical part performance is stronger here because the facts align precisely with *Neale*'s paradigm — fifteen years of open possession and permanent improvements with the promisor's knowledge. Promissory estoppel is the superior fallback in jurisdictions that have narrowed part performance to require payment of purchase price, which is absent here.

## 7. Defenses / Mistake

(a) Anna's partial payment for improvements actually strengthens Claire's position: it corroborates the oral agreement's existence and Anna's acknowledgment of Claire's equitable interest. (b) Anna's retention of some rental income is consistent with a life-estate or ongoing management arrangement and does not negate the promise; it may reflect Anna's retained interest during her lifetime, which terminated at death. (c) Claire's use of some rentals for personal expenses is not a material breach of any discernible condition. The oral promise contained no restriction on rental-income use; even if it did, the doctrine of substantial performance and the disproportionality between the alleged breach and forfeiture of fifteen years' reliance would prevent this defense from defeating equity. None of these defenses is likely to succeed.

## 8. Strongest Counterargument for the Heirs

The heirs' best argument is that Claire's acts are explicable as those of a permissive licensee or family member living rent-free, not as acts "unequivocally referable" to an oral contract to convey. If the heirs can prove at trial that Anna treated the property as her own throughout (e.g., paid taxes, insured it in her name, directed major decisions, and that Claire's improvements were gifts to her mother's property), the referability requirement fails and the Statute of Frauds bar stands. The heirs must produce evidence that Claire acknowledged Anna's ownership and never claimed an independent right.

## 9. Bounded Uncertainty and Practical Directions

Key uncertainties include: whether State X requires payment of purchase price as a separate element of part performance (some jurisdictions do, which would weaken Claire's claim since no price was paid); whether fifteen years of possession creates an independent adverse-possession claim that might moot the SoF issue; and whether the "unequivocal referability" standard is applied strictly (acts must be inexplicable absent the contract) or liberally (acts must corroborate the contract's existence).

Claire's counsel should take three concrete steps: First, secure testimony from witnesses (neighbors, the tenant, Ben himself) confirming Anna's oral statements acknowledging Claire's ownership interest, to establish referability beyond Claire's self-serving testimony. Second, compile documentary evidence — receipts, permits, tax records, insurance documents — showing Claire paid for improvements in her own name and exercised dominion inconsistent with mere licensee status. Third, plead both part performance and promissory estoppel as alternative equitable theories, and additionally plead constructive trust based on unjust enrichment, ensuring that if the court rejects the oral-contract theory, Claire retains a restitutionary claim for the value of her improvements that avoids the Statute of Frauds entirely.

The Statute of Frauds, requiring a signed writing for conveyances of interests in land, is dispositive on its face and would bar Claire's claim if applied mechanically. The Supreme Court in *Neale* held that where a party, in reliance on an oral promise to convey land, takes possession, makes substantial and permanent improvements, and materially changes position such that restoration to the status quo ante is impossible, equity will enforce the promise notwithstanding the absence of a writing. The rationale is that the Statute of Frauds, designed to prevent fraud, must not be converted into an instrument of fraud. Claire's fifteen years of possession, $6,500 in improvements (substantial for the era and context), construction of permanent structures, and marriage undertaken in reliance satisfy every element of the *Neale* standard.

Claire's acts constitute valid consideration, not merely conditions of a gratuitous gift. Anna's promise was bargained-for: Claire was to marry, relocate onto the property, and improve it, and in exchange Anna would hold title for Claire's benefit. Claire's marriage, her expenditure of $6,500 in personal savings on permanent improvements, her assumption of management responsibilities including rent collection, and her fifteen-year occupation collectively constitute detrimental reliance and bilateral consideration. A court should characterize these acts as consideration and induced reliance rather than a mere license, because Anna's promise was explicitly conditioned on Claire's performance of specific acts that Claire then fully performed. The presence of a designated trustee (Ben) further evidences Anna's intent to create a binding beneficial arrangement, not a revocable license.

## 5. Statute of Frauds Gates

Two SoF categories are triggered: the land-conveyance provision (requiring a writing for transfers of interests in real property) and potentially the trust provision (oral declaration of trust in land). Part performance removes this bar under *Neale* when three elements converge: (i) actual, exclusive, and continuous possession referable to the oral agreement; (ii) valuable, permanent improvements; and (iii) change of position making restoration of the status quo impracticable. Claire satisfies all three. Her possession was continuous for fifteen years, the improvements were permanent structures (addition, outbuildings, utilities), and she cannot be made whole by mere money damages given the nature and duration of her reliance.

Under classical *Neale*-style part performance, Claire's claim is strong: possession plus substantial improvements plus exclusive reliance is the paradigm case. Defense (c)—Claire used some rentals for personal expenses—is the weakest objection. The oral promise contained no express condition restricting rental use; even if it did, de minimis or incidental personal use of income over fifteen years does not constitute a material breach sufficient to forfeit Claire's entire equitable interest. Equity abhors forfeiture, and no court following *Neale* would deny relief on this basis absent evidence of gross misappropriation.

## 8. Strongest Counterargument for the Heirs

The heirs' best argument is that Anna's oral statement was a revocable, conditional gift promise—not an enforceable contract—and that Claire's acts of improvement were those of a permissive occupant or licensee, not referable exclusively to an oral contract. Under this theory, Claire's possession and improvements are equally consistent with a mother allowing a daughter to live on family property, and the part-performance doctrine should not apply because the acts are not "unequivocally referable" to an oral contract (the strict referability test followed in some jurisdictions). ## 9. Bounded Uncertainty and Practical Directions

Key uncertainties include: whether State X applies the strict "unequivocal referability" test (which could require Claire to show her acts are explicable only by the oral promise) versus the broader *Neale* standard; whether $6,500 in improvements qualifies as "substantial" relative to the property's value; and whether Claire's rent collection constitutes the type of dominion evidencing equitable ownership or merely permissive use. Third, plead both constructive trust and specific performance in the alternative, with a fallback claim for unjust enrichment/quantum meruit damages, ensuring that even under the most restrictive reading of the part-performance doctrine, Claire recovers at minimum the value of her improvements and reliance expenditures.
