3. The dispositive doctrine on the face of the pleadings is the Statute of Frauds rule requiring a signed writing for transfers of interests in land; the promise also arguably falls within the separate category for agreements made upon consideration of marriage. The equitable doctrine most likely controlling the result is part performance, as described in Neale: equity may enforce a parol land arrangement when the promisee, in reliance on it, takes possession, makes valuable and permanent improvements, and otherwise acts in a manner clearly referable to the alleged agreement, such that nonenforcement would perpetrate a fraud. Claire’s occupancy, substantial expenditures, utility and landscaping work, outbuildings, and exercise of rental rights fit that pattern.

4. A court should not treat this as a mere revocable license or naked conditional gift if it credits Claire’s evidence. Marriage alone is a difficult form of “consideration” because the Statute of Frauds has a special marriage provision, and many courts refuse to treat marriage itself as sufficient part performance. But the better characterization is that Anna made an inducement-backed family settlement: Claire married, moved onto the land, and spent significant personal funds improving it because Anna promised beneficial ownership. Even if a court doubts classical bargain consideration, Claire’s acts are at least reliance induced by the promise, which is enough for equity under Neale-style reasoning. So her conduct is best viewed as reliance and part performance strongly evidencing the agreement, rather than compliance with a mere condition attached to a gift.

5. The writing requirement is triggered first by the land-transfer provision, because Anna promised to give Claire the lot and hold title for her benefit, which is either an outright conveyance or an express trust in land. It is also potentially triggered by the marriage provision because the promise was made before marriage and conditioned on marriage. The land-transfer aspect is the core issue because Claire seeks title or a trust in land. Her response is that part performance removes the land bar: she took possession, made substantial and permanent improvements with her own money, managed at least some rents, and did so with Anna’s knowledge and encouragement. Those are precisely the acts equity traditionally treats as making the oral agreement enforceable.

6. Claire can reach relief most strongly under classical part performance, and secondarily under modern promissory estoppel. Part performance is stronger because her requested remedy is the land itself and her facts closely resemble Neale’s core pattern of possession plus valuable improvements. Modern promissory estoppel could also support relief if Anna should reasonably have expected reliance and injustice can be avoided only by enforcement or compensation; that theory is especially useful if State X is skeptical about using part performance where the promise was made in consideration of marriage. The uncertainty is that some courts do not allow promissory estoppel to circumvent the Statute of Frauds in land cases except for reliance damages. Thus, if title relief falters, estoppel should at least support recovery of Claire’s expenditures and possibly the value added to the land.

7. The heirs’ defenses are not likely to defeat Claire’s equity claim on this record. The fact that Anna paid for some improvements cuts against exclusivity of Claire’s investment, but it is not fatal if Claire can prove that her own expenditures were substantial, permanent, and made in reliance on ownership. Neale does not require that every dollar come from the promisee. Anna’s retention of some rental income likewise weakens but does not destroy Claire’s case; it may show the arrangement was informal or shared, yet Claire still occupied the property, improved it, and exercised some incidents of beneficial ownership. The alleged “breach” based on Claire’s using some rents for personal expenses is the weakest defense unless the heirs can prove a definite condition of the oral trust that rents were restricted to property expenses. Absent a clearly proven term, using rents personally is consistent with equitable ownership, not a disqualifying breach. At most, it might justify an accounting, not forfeiture.

8. The heirs’ best doctrinal argument is that this was an oral antenuptial agreement made upon consideration of marriage, and State X should treat that Statute of Frauds category as independently barring enforcement, with marriage and later occupancy/improvements insufficient to avoid the statute. To make that argument succeed, they must prove both that State X does not extend Neale-style part performance to the marriage clause and that Claire’s later conduct is equally explainable as permissive family occupancy, not acts unequivocally referable to a promise of ownership. Evidence that Anna substantially financed the work, controlled rents, and never relinquished beneficial ownership would be central.

9. The main uncertainties are whether State X allows part performance to overcome not only the land provision but also the marriage provision; how “substantial” and “unequivocally referable” the improvements must be; whether possession alone plus improvements suffices without exclusive receipt of rents; and whether the court prefers constructive trust, specific performance, or only reliance damages when title was never formally transferred to a trustee. To maximize success, Claire’s counsel should first plead in the alternative for specific performance, constructive trust, and reliance damages so a narrower view of the statute does not defeat the entire case. Second, counsel should develop exact proof tying Claire’s possession and expenditures to Anna’s promise: dates, invoices, source of funds, testimony that Claire would not have married/moved/invested absent the promise, and evidence of Anna’s knowledge and praise. Third, counsel should sharpen the “unequivocally referable” showing by documenting Claire’s management of the property and rental arrangements, while neutralizing the heirs’ defenses through an accounting that segregates Anna’s contributions and explains Claire’s use of rents as consistent with beneficial ownership. Overall, under a Neale-style jurisdiction, Claire should receive title-based equitable relief.

1. I assume State X follows the ordinary U.S. common-law Statute of Frauds for land transfers, but also retains the equitable part-performance doctrine recognized in Neale v. Neales, 76 U.S. (9 Wall.) 1 (1869), and permits promissory estoppel at least as a possible fallback. That assumption matters because, under a strict writing-only regime, Claire loses outright; under a Neale-style regime, equity may enforce an oral land promise where the promisee took possession and made substantial, permanent improvements in reliance, such that refusal to perform would itself operate as a fraud.

2. On these facts, Claire has the stronger claim on her request to enforce the oral promise, and I would recommend equitable relief rather than merely damages. The best remedy is a decree imposing a constructive trust on the heirs and ordering conveyance of legal title to Claire, or equivalently specific performance of Anna’s promise to hold title for Claire’s benefit and convey it. The facts closely track the equitable logic of Neale: oral promise of land within a family setting, marriage and relocation in reliance, possession, long enjoyment, and substantial improvements funded by the promisee with the promisor’s knowledge. Damages under promissory estoppel should be pled in the alternative, but the better view is that Claire should receive the land interest itself.

3. On its face, the dispositive doctrine is the Statute of Frauds rule requiring a signed writing for transfers of interests in land, and likely also for an express trust concerning land. The equitable doctrine that most likely controls the result is part performance, sometimes described as equitable estoppel against pleading the Statute of Frauds. Under Neale, equity may enforce an oral land arrangement when the promisee’s acts of possession, expenditure, and improvement are sufficiently substantial and referable to the promise, so that refusing enforcement would permit the promisor or heirs to use the statute as an instrument of fraud rather than as a shield against false claims.

4. The harder characterization issue is whether this was a bargain supported by consideration or a mere conditional gift. Marriage alone is a problematic form of consideration in Statute of Frauds analysis, and many courts hesitate to treat an intra-family promise of land as an exchange contract. But Claire’s case does not depend on classical bargain theory alone. Her marriage, move onto the property, assumption of occupancy, collection of rents, and especially her $6,500 expenditure on a large addition, outbuildings, landscaping, and utilities are best understood as reliance induced by Anna’s promise, not merely a revocable license. A court could say this was either an oral contract supported by reliance-based consideration or, more cautiously, an oral donative promise that became specifically enforceable in equity because Claire materially changed position in a way Anna invited and observed.

5. The writing requirements triggered are the land-transfer provision and likely the trust-in-land provision; there is also an arguable “agreement upon consideration of marriage” issue because the promise was made before marriage and conditioned on marriage. The land provision is the most important because Claire seeks a deed or trust interest in real property. Part performance is the route around that bar: Claire moved onto the land, exercised incidents of ownership by collecting rent, and made substantial permanent improvements over a long period with Anna’s knowledge and encouragement. Those acts are far more probative than mere oral testimony and are sufficiently referable to an ownership arrangement rather than casual family permission.

6. Claire can proceed under both classical part performance and modern promissory estoppel, but part performance is the stronger theory. Under Neale-style doctrine, possession plus valuable improvements is the classic basis for specific enforcement of an oral land promise. Promissory estoppel also fits because Anna should reasonably have expected Claire to marry, relocate, spend money, and organize her life around the promise, and injustice is obvious if the heirs keep the enhanced property. The uncertainty is that some jurisdictions do not allow promissory estoppel to override the land Statute of Frauds except for reliance damages. Thus, part performance is the surer path to the land itself; promissory estoppel is a prudent alternative if the court is reluctant to decree title.

7. The heirs’ defenses likely weaken but do not defeat Claire’s claim. First, if Anna paid for some improvements, that may reduce the amount of Claire’s exclusive reliance and create factual disputes about how much improvement is truly attributable to Claire. But it does not erase Claire’s own substantial expenditures and labor if those remain significant. Second, Anna’s retention of some rental income may undercut the claim that Claire had complete beneficial ownership from the outset, yet it is still consistent with an imperfectly administered family trust or shared management while Anna retained legal title. Claire’s occupancy and partial receipt of rents still support reliance. Third, the alleged breach that Claire used some rentals for personal expenses should defeat relief only if the oral promise clearly made restricted use of rent a material condition of Claire’s equitable ownership. On the stated facts, that sounds more like a post hoc complaint about property management than a definite condition precedent or subsequent. It is unlikely to justify forfeiture of a land interest after fifteen years of reliance.

8. The heirs’ best doctrinal argument is that Claire’s conduct was not unequivocally referable to an ownership promise, but equally explainable as permissive family occupancy plus improvements made by a daughter living on her mother’s land. To make that argument succeed, the heirs must prove at trial that Anna remained the real economic owner: she substantially funded the improvements, retained meaningful control over rents, taxes, and maintenance, and never surrendered possession except as a revocable family arrangement. If the court sees the facts as a tolerated license rather than reliance on a definite promise of title, the part-performance exception may fail.

9. The main uncertainties are how strictly State X requires acts to be “unequivocally referable” to the oral promise, whether possession alone plus some improvements is enough, whether improvements must be mostly paid by Claire personally, whether receipt of rents is necessary or merely corroborative, and whether promissory estoppel can circumvent the land Statute of Frauds for specific relief or only damages. To maximize success, Claire’s counsel should first plead both specific performance and constructive trust, with promissory estoppel as an express alternative. Second, counsel should develop detailed proof tying Claire’s expenditures, possession, and improvements to Anna’s promise: receipts, witnesses, dates, photos, tax and utility records, and evidence that the improvements were permanent and value-enhancing. Third, counsel should marshal testimony showing Anna’s assent and repeated acknowledgment that the property was for Claire’s benefit, because under Neale the combination of possession, improvements, and promisor acquiescence is what makes denial of enforcement look fraudulent.

Bottom line: assuming State X follows Neale-style equitable doctrine, Claire should prevail on part performance and receive equitable title through a constructive trust or decree of conveyance; promissory estoppel is a secondary fallback, mainly if the court refuses land-specific relief.

1. I assume State X follows the ordinary U.S. common-law equitable approach reflected in Neale v. 2. The clearest conclusion is that Claire should prevail on her claim to enforce the oral promise in equity. 4. The better characterization is not a mere revocable license or naked conditional gift. 5. The writing requirement is triggered first and foremost because this is an oral transfer of land or beneficial ownership in land. Those facts are the type Neale treats as taking the case out of the statute in equity.

6. Under classical part performance, Claire has a strong claim because she has the classic trio of possession, substantial improvements, and reliance known to the promisor. Under modern promissory estoppel, she also has a plausible alternative: Anna made a clear promise, expected reliance, and Claire’s reliance was substantial and foreseeable, so injustice may require a remedy. But part performance is the stronger route because many jurisdictions are wary of using promissory estoppel to circumvent the land Statute of Frauds, or they confine estoppel to reliance damages rather than conveyance. On these facts, Neale-style part performance better supports land-specific relief.

7. The heirs’ defenses are not likely to defeat Claire’s equity claim, though they may affect accounting. Anna’s paying for some improvements does not negate Claire’s own substantial expenditures; it may reduce the reimbursable or equitable amount but does not erase part performance if Claire’s contributions were material and permanent. Anna’s retention of some rental income likewise shows the arrangement was imperfectly executed, not necessarily nonexistent; mixed control over rents is consistent with family informality and may call for an accounting, not denial of title. The alleged “breach” because Claire used some rents for personal expenses is weak unless the heirs prove an actual condition that all rents had to be used solely for the property. 8. If the court sees the arrangement as a familial accommodation rather than a partially executed land transfer, the Statute of Frauds may bar relief.

9. The main uncertainties are doctrinal and evidentiary. To maximize success, Claire’s counsel should first plead with specificity both specific performance and constructive trust, and separately plead promissory-estoppel damages in the alternative. Second, counsel should assemble detailed proof that Claire personally funded permanent, value-enhancing improvements and utilities over time, with dates, receipts, permits, bank records, and valuation evidence. Third, counsel should marshal corroboration that Anna treated the property as Claire’s beneficially—witness testimony to Anna’s statements, evidence of Claire’s possession and control, and proof that Anna knew of and encouraged the improvements—because under Neale-style doctrine the clarity of the promise and the referability of the acts are the decisive facts.

1. I assume State X follows the ordinary U.S. common-law equity approach reflected in Neale v. Neales, 76 U.S. (9 Wall.) 1 (1869): an oral promise concerning land is facially barred by the Statute of Frauds, but equity may enforce it when the promisee’s acts of part performance are clearly referable to the promise and nonenforcement would itself work a fraud. I also assume State X has not categorically rejected promissory estoppel in land cases. That assumption matters because some jurisdictions treat the marriage clause of the Statute of Frauds, or land-transfer rules generally, more rigidly and confine relief to damages or deny estoppel altogether.

2. On these facts, Claire should likely prevail on her claim to enforce the oral promise. The strongest equitable remedy is a decree imposing a constructive trust on the heirs’ legal title and ordering conveyance to Claire, which is functionally equivalent to specific performance; that remedy best fits the fact that Anna never executed a deed but induced Claire to marry, move in, and expend substantial money and labor in reliance on beneficial ownership. Promissory-estoppel damages should be pleaded only as an alternative fallback if State X refuses land-specific equitable enforcement.

3. The Statute of Frauds is facially triggered because the promise concerns a transfer of land, and arguably also because the promise was made in contemplation of marriage. But under Neale, the controlling equitable doctrine is part performance: possession plus substantial, permanent improvements, especially when done with the promisor’s knowledge, can take the case out of the statute because refusal to enforce would make the statute an instrument of fraud. The relevant standard is whether Claire’s conduct is sufficiently definite and referable to the alleged agreement that it would be inequitable to leave the heirs with the land free of the promise.

4. The formation issue is close only because Anna used donative language (“give the lot”), and family arrangements often resemble conditional gifts. Even so, the better characterization is not a mere revocable license but an induced exchange or, at minimum, a reliance-based family settlement: Anna promised beneficial ownership if Claire married and moved onto the property, and Claire then altered her position dramatically by marrying, occupying, building additions and outbuildings, improving utilities and landscaping, and managing rental activity. Marriage alone is often treated cautiously under the Statute of Frauds, but Claire’s expenditures and possession are more than a condition to a gift; they are the very acts that make enforcement equitable under Neale.

5. The writing requirements most clearly implicated are the land-transfer provision and, secondarily, the provision covering agreements upon consideration of marriage. The land aspect is the most dispositive because Claire seeks a deed or trust over real property. Her response is classic part performance: she took possession, made valuable and permanent improvements with her own funds, and exercised incidents of ownership by collecting rent; those acts are the basis for removing the land promise from the statute in equity, even though no signed writing exists.

6. Claire can argue both classical part performance and modern promissory estoppel, but part performance is the stronger theory. Under Neale-style doctrine, the combination of possession and substantial improvements is precisely the sort of conduct that justifies specific relief in land cases. Promissory estoppel also fits because Anna made a clear promise, reliance was foreseeable and substantial, and injustice is evident; however, many courts are less willing to use promissory estoppel to override the Statute of Frauds for land, or they limit relief to reliance damages. Thus the classical equity route is more likely to produce the conveyance Claire actually wants.

7. The heirs’ specific defenses likely weaken, but do not defeat, Claire’s claim. First, evidence that Anna paid for some improvements does not negate Claire’s reliance so long as Claire’s own expenditures and work were substantial; it goes to degree, not necessarily to the existence of part performance. Second, Anna’s retention of some rental income creates ambiguity about who enjoyed beneficial ownership, but it is not fatal if Claire otherwise occupied the property as owner and controlled at least part of the rental stream; shared rents may reduce the clarity of the proof, not eliminate it. Third, the alleged “breach” that Claire used some rents for personal expenses appears weak because the stated condition was that she marry and move onto the property; unless the heirs can prove a specific condition that all rents had to be reinvested in the property, using rents personally is at least as consistent with ownership as with breach.

8. The heirs’ best nonfrivolous argument is that Claire’s conduct was not unequivocally referable to an ownership agreement, but instead to permissive family occupancy under Anna’s continuing ownership. To win on that theory, they would need to prove that Anna remained the true economic owner: that she paid a large share of the improvements, retained meaningful control over rents, supervised the property as owner rather than helper, and never clearly manifested that Claire already held beneficial title. If Claire’s acts can be explained as those of a daughter living on family land with permission, rather than as a beneficiary-owner in possession, a trial court could refuse specific enforcement under a stricter reading of part performance.

9. The main uncertainties are doctrinal and factual: whether State X allows part performance to overcome not only the land provision but also an agreement made in consideration of marriage; how “substantial” improvements must be; whether possession must be exclusive; whether partial receipt of rents matters; and whether promissory estoppel is available to circumvent the statute or only to recover reliance damages. To maximize success, Claire’s counsel should first plead in the alternative for specific performance, constructive trust, and promissory-estoppel damages, expressly tying the equitable claims to Neale-style part performance. Second, counsel should develop a detailed evidentiary record of possession and improvements—receipts, photos, contractor testimony, utility and tax records, rental records, and valuation evidence—to show substantial, permanent reliance traceable to Claire. Third, counsel should secure testimony or admissions from family members and other witnesses that Anna repeatedly described the property as Claire’s or directed Ben to hold for Claire, because the more the promise and Claire’s acts appear uniquely referable to ownership, the more likely a court is to order conveyance.

Overall, under a Neale-based framework, Claire has the better claim and should receive equitable conveyance relief, with promissory-estoppel damages reserved only as a backup if State X applies the Statute of Frauds more rigidly.

1. I assume State X follows the ordinary U.S. common-law equitable approach reflected in Neale v. Neales: an oral transfer or trust of land is facially barred by the Statute of Frauds, but equity may enforce it where the promisee’s part performance is sufficiently definite and reliance-based that refusal to enforce would itself work a fraud. I also assume State X permits promissory estoppel at least as an alternative reliance remedy. That assumption matters because, under a stricter jurisdiction that refuses part-performance relief for intra-family land promises or limits estoppel against the Statute of Frauds, Claire’s case would be weaker. If State X separately treats promises “upon consideration of marriage” as independently within the Statute, that creates an additional uncertainty, but the core analysis still turns on Neale-style equitable enforcement.

2. The better conclusion is that Claire should prevail on her claim to enforce the oral promise. On these facts, the strongest remedy is specific performance by decree compelling the heirs to convey title to Claire, or, if the court prefers equitable language, imposition of a constructive trust in her favor with Ben and the heirs holding bare legal title for her benefit. Damages under promissory estoppel should be pleaded in the alternative, but they are a fallback, not the preferred remedy, because the reliance was directed toward a particular parcel and permanent improvements to it.

3. On its face, the dispositive doctrine is the Statute of Frauds rule requiring a signed writing for transfers of interests in land. The equitable doctrine that most likely controls the result is part performance, sometimes described in this setting as equitable estoppel against asserting the Statute. Neale is the anchor: equity enforced an oral family land arrangement where marriage was followed by possession, improvements, and enjoyment of the property’s benefits, because those acts were sufficiently substantial and referable to the promise that nonenforcement would permit fraud. The governing standard is not merely that Claire changed her position, but that she took possession and made valuable, permanent improvements in reliance on the promise of ownership or beneficial ownership.

4. The facts are stronger than a mere conditional gift, although they do have that appearance at the outset. Anna’s promise was framed as “if Claire married and moved onto the property,” which could be characterized as donative and conditional. But Neale teaches that, in family land cases, the key question is whether the promise induced conduct that equity treats as the equivalent of consideration or reliance of a kind too serious to ignore. Claire married, moved in, paid substantial sums from her own savings, built a large addition and outbuildings, improved utilities and landscaping, and managed rental income over many years. Those acts are best characterized as induced reliance and equitable consideration supporting enforcement, not merely passive satisfaction of a gift condition. At minimum, they convert a donative promise into one that equity will specifically enforce.

5. The writing requirement triggered is the land-transfer provision of the Statute of Frauds, because Claire seeks a conveyance of title or recognition of a trust in land. If State X recognizes a separate marriage-consideration clause, the premarital nature of Anna’s promise may trigger that as well, but the land clause is the central barrier. Claire’s argument for removing the bar is classic part performance: she took possession, exercised incidents of beneficial ownership by collecting rents, and made substantial permanent improvements with Anna’s knowledge. Under Neale, those acts are not adequately compensable as ordinary expenses of occupancy and are sufficiently referable to an ownership promise to justify equitable enforcement despite the absence of a signed writing.

6. Claire can reach relief under both classical part performance and modern promissory estoppel, but part performance is the stronger route. It maps directly onto Neale and supports the preferred remedy of conveyance or trust. Promissory estoppel is useful if the court is hesitant to order title transferred, because Anna made a clear promise, expected reliance, and Claire relied to her detriment for fifteen years. Still, some courts are cautious about using promissory estoppel to defeat the Statute of Frauds entirely, especially for land, and may limit relief to reliance damages. By contrast, the facts here—possession plus major improvements plus partial enjoyment of rents—fit the traditional part-performance pattern that justifies specific equitable relief.

7. The heirs’ defenses likely do not defeat Claire’s claim, though they may affect accounting. First, proof that Anna paid for some improvements does not eliminate Claire’s equity if Claire herself made substantial, permanent expenditures in reliance on the promise. It may reduce the scope of unjust detriment or require offsets, but Neale does not require that every improvement be solely funded by the claimant. Second, the fact that Anna retained some rental income weakens the inference that Claire had complete beneficial ownership, but it is not fatal. Shared rents are consistent with an informal family arrangement and may simply show that the equitable ownership was imperfectly administered; possession and improvements remain the stronger indicators. Third, the asserted breach—that Claire used some rents for personal expenses—looks weak unless the heirs can prove an actual condition of the oral trust that rental proceeds had to be confined to property-related uses. On the stated facts, that restriction does not appear in Anna’s promise. Absent a clearly proved and material condition, misuse of some rents would justify at most an accounting, not forfeiture of Claire’s equitable title.

8. The heirs’ best doctrinal argument is that Claire’s conduct was not unequivocally referable to a contract or trust to convey land, but instead consistent with a revocable family license or expectation of future bounty. Courts are often skeptical where relatives allow one another to live on land and make improvements without formal title. To make that argument succeed, the heirs would need to prove at trial that Anna never made a definite promise of ownership, that Claire’s possession was permissive rather than proprietary, that the improvements were materially funded or directed by Anna, that Anna substantially controlled the rents, and that Claire’s expenditures were the kind an occupant might make without any ownership commitment. If the trial record becomes too equivocal, the Statute of Frauds could reassert itself.

9. The main uncertainties are how strictly State X applies Neale, whether it recognizes part performance for oral family settlements as readily as for commercial land contracts, whether a separate marriage-consideration clause adds an independent Statute of Frauds obstacle, how “substantial” improvements must be, whether possession must be exclusive, and how much weight to give receipt of rents. To maximize success, Claire’s counsel should first plead the case with precision as an oral trust/contract to convey land and, in the alternative, as a constructive-trust and promissory-estoppel claim, tying each remedy to the same reliance facts. Second, counsel should assemble hard proof that Claire’s possession and improvements were permanent and ownership-like: receipts, permits, photos, contractor testimony, utility records, and tenant-payment evidence tracing her $6,500 and subsequent expenditures. Third, counsel should neutralize the heirs’ defenses with a clean accounting showing which improvements Claire funded, what rents she received or Anna retained, and why any personal use of rents was immaterial to the original promise. On the present facts, the recommended disposition is equitable enforcement in Claire’s favor, preferably by decree of conveyance or constructive trust, with accounting adjustments as needed.

1. I assume State X follows the ordinary U.S. common-law Statute of Frauds for transfers of land, but also recognizes the equitable part-performance doctrine in the Neale v. Neales sense: an oral land promise may be specifically enforced when the promisee, in reliance on it, takes possession and makes substantial, permanent improvements so that refusing enforcement would itself work a fraud. I also assume State X permits promissory estoppel at least as a fallback theory, though perhaps more readily for reliance damages than for a conveyance. That assumption matters because, under a strict no-exceptions regime, Claire loses for lack of a signed writing; under Neale-style equity, her possession and expenditures are exactly the kind of facts that can remove the bar.

2. The clearest conclusion is that Claire should prevail on her claim to enforce the oral promise. The best remedy is equitable conveyance: a decree of specific performance ordering Anna’s heirs to convey legal title to Claire, or, because title has passed through intestacy, a constructive trust on the heirs’ title with a direction to reconvey. On these facts, damages under promissory estoppel should be only an alternative fallback if the court declines land-specific relief. Neale strongly supports enforcement where a family land promise made in contemplation of marriage is followed by marriage, possession, and substantial improvements with the promisor’s knowledge.

3. On its face, the dispositive doctrine is the Statute of Frauds category requiring a signed writing for transfers of land and creation of interests or trusts in land. The equitable doctrine that most likely controls the result is part performance, sometimes described in estoppel-like terms. The Neale standard is that equity will not let the Statute be used as an instrument of fraud where the alleged promise has been clearly proved and the promisee has so changed position—especially by taking possession and making valuable improvements in reliance—that nonenforcement would be inequitable. Claire’s long possession, large expenditures, and Anna’s knowledge fit that pattern.

4. This is stronger than a mere gratuitous promise or revocable license. Anna’s promise was framed as “if Claire married and moved onto the property,” and Claire did both, then invested substantial personal funds. Marriage, historically, is treated as valuable consideration, and Claire’s post-promise expenditures are additional reliance of a kind equity treats seriously. A court could say the promise had both bargain and inducement features: marriage and occupancy were stated conditions, but Claire’s costly improvements were not merely incidental; they were the foreseeable and invited performance that makes the promise enforceable in equity. Even if a court hesitated to call it a classic bilateral contract, it is not just a conditional gift once Claire materially changed position in reliance.

5. Two Statute of Frauds gates are implicated. First, the land-transfer requirement is plainly triggered because Anna promised to “give the lot and all buildings” and to hold title for Claire’s benefit. Second, there is a possible “consideration of marriage” writing requirement because the promise was made before marriage and conditioned on marriage. The land provision is the most obviously dispositive bar, but the marriage provision creates doctrinal uncertainty in some jurisdictions. Claire’s answer is part performance: she married, took possession, exercised incidents of ownership by collecting rents, and made substantial permanent improvements. Under Neale, possession plus valuable improvements is the classic combination used to remove the land-transfer bar.

6. Claire can argue both classical part performance and modern promissory estoppel, but part performance is the stronger route. It is stronger because it is tailored to oral land agreements and supports the precise remedy Claire wants: conveyance or trust relief. Promissory estoppel also fits the facts—a clear promise, foreseeable reliance, actual reliance, and injustice absent relief—but many courts are cautious about using promissory estoppel to override the Statute of Frauds in land cases, and some limit it to reliance damages unless denial would be unconscionable. So the better doctrinal path is Neale-style part performance for specific relief, with promissory estoppel as an alternative basis for damages if the court is unwilling to order conveyance.

7. The heirs’ stated defenses are not likely to defeat Claire’s equity claim. If Anna paid for some improvements, that may matter to accounting or to whether Claire’s expenditures were “substantial,” but it does not negate Claire’s own $6,500 payment and fifteen years of possession and improvements. The issue is whether Claire’s reliance was material and permanent, not whether every dollar came from her. If Anna retained some rental income, that may show retained involvement, but it does not defeat Claire’s beneficial claim where Claire also occupied, improved, and collected some rents herself; at most it supports offsets or an accounting. The alleged breach—that Claire used some rental income for personal expenses—looks weak unless the heirs can prove a definite condition that all rents had to be reinvested in the property and that such a restriction was central to the promise. On the stated facts, Anna promised to hold title for Claire’s benefit; using some rents personally is consistent with beneficial ownership, not inconsistent with it.

8. The heirs’ best doctrinal argument is that Claire’s acts are not sufficiently “unequivocally referable” to the alleged oral land contract and instead are explainable as family permissive occupancy under a revocable license or anticipated gift. That argument gains force from the familial setting, Anna’s payment of some improvements, Anna’s retention of some rent, and the absence of any signed writing or formal trust instrument. To make that argument succeed at trial, the heirs would need to prove that Anna retained practical dominion throughout, that Claire’s expenditures were minor, shared, reimbursed, or ordinary maintenance rather than substantial permanent improvements, and that the alleged promise was vague or purely donative rather than a definite commitment to transfer beneficial ownership.

9. The main uncertainties are whether State X allows part performance to overcome not just the land-transfer provision but also any marriage-consideration provision; how “substantial” and “permanent” the improvements must be; whether possession in a family setting is sufficiently referable to a contract; and whether partial receipt of rents by the promisor undermines exclusivity enough to matter. To maximize success, Claire’s counsel should first plead specific performance and constructive trust in the alternative, with promissory-estoppel damages as an express fallback, so the court has a remedial ladder. Second, counsel should assemble a tight chronology with receipts, contractor testimony, utility and permit records, and valuation proof showing that Claire personally funded and caused permanent improvements after and because of Anna’s promise. Third, counsel should secure testimony from Ben and any neutral witnesses about Anna’s statements directing title to be held for Claire’s benefit, plus evidence of Claire’s possession and rent collection, to show the reliance was clear, known to Anna, and referable to the promised conveyance.

Bottom line: assuming State X follows Neale-style part performance, the trial court should enforce the oral promise through specific performance or a constructive trust, subject at most to accounting offsets, and reserve promissory-estoppel damages only as alternative relief.
